## Complete `runPipeline` method (lines 143–244) — verbatim

```java
    private void runPipeline(File inputFile, File outputFile,
                             PageExtractor extractor, ImageSegmenter segmenter,
                             OCREngine ocrEngine, JBIG2Compressor compressor,
                             PDFAssembler assembler, boolean useMrc, boolean usePdfa,
                             int segThreads, int ocrMaxThreads) throws IOException {

        // Phase 1: Extract pages as images
        long t1 = System.nanoTime();
        System.out.println("  Extracting pages...");
        List<BufferedImage> pages;
        try (PageExtractor pe = extractor) {
            pages = pe.extractPages(inputFile);
        }
        long t2 = System.nanoTime();
        System.out.printf("    Extracted %d pages in %.1fs%n", pages.size(), (t2 - t1) / 1e9);

        List<BufferedImage> backgrounds;
        List<BufferedImage> foregroundMasks;
        long t3 = t2;

        if (useMrc) {
            System.out.println("  Segmenting pages...");
            ExecutorService segExec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(segThreads);
            try {
                List<Future<SegmentedImage>> segFutures = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
                for (int i = 0; i < pages.size(); i++) {
                    int pageIdx = i;
                    segFutures.add(segExec.submit(() -> {
                        long ts = System.nanoTime();
                        SegmentedImage seg = segmenter.segment(pages.get(pageIdx));
                        double elapsed = (System.nanoTime() - ts) / 1e9;
                        System.out.printf("    Segment page %3d: %.1fs%n", pageIdx + 1, elapsed);
                        return seg;
                    }));
                }
                List<SegmentedImage> segResults = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
                for (Future<SegmentedImage> f : segFutures) {
                    try {
                        segResults.add(f.get());
                    } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                        Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
                        throw new IOException("Segmentation interrupted", e);
                    } catch (ExecutionException e) {
                        Throwable cause = e.getCause() != null ? e.getCause() : e;
                        throw new IOException("Segmentation failed", cause);
                    }
                }
                backgrounds = segResults.stream().map(SegmentedImage::getCleanedBackground).toList();
                foregroundMasks = segResults.stream().map(SegmentedImage::getForegroundMask).toList();
            } finally {
                segExec.shutdown();
            }
            t3 = System.nanoTime();
            System.out.printf("    Segmentation done in %.1fs%n", (t3 - t2) / 1e9);
        } else {
            System.out.println("  MRC disabled — using original pages as backgrounds");
            backgrounds = new ArrayList<>(pages);
            foregroundMasks = null;
        }

        // Phase 3: OCR each page (parallel, limited subprocess concurrency)
        System.out.println("  Running OCR...");
        List<PageResult> ocrResults;
        ExecutorService ocrExec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(ocrMaxThreads);
        try {
            List<Future<PageResult>> ocrFutures = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
            for (int i = 0; i < pages.size(); i++) {
                int pageIdx = i;
                ocrFutures.add(ocrExec.submit(() -> {
                    long ts = System.nanoTime();
                    PageResult result = ocrEngine.ocr(pages.get(pageIdx));
                    double elapsed = (System.nanoTime() - ts) / 1e9;
                    System.out.printf("    OCR page %3d: %.1fs%n", pageIdx + 1, elapsed);
                    return result;
                }));
            }
            ocrResults = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
            for (Future<PageResult> f : ocrFutures) {
                try {
                    ocrResults.add(f.get());
                } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
                    throw new IOException("OCR interrupted", e);
                } catch (ExecutionException e) {
                    Throwable cause = e.getCause() != null ? e.getCause() : e;
                    throw new IOException("OCR processing failed", cause);
                }
            }
        } finally {
            ocrExec.shutdown();
        }
        long t4 = System.nanoTime();
        System.out.printf("    OCR done in %.1fs%n", (t4 - (useMrc ? t3 : t2)) / 1e9);

        // Phase 4: Assemble searchable PDF with optional MRC foreground overlay
        System.out.println("  Assembling PDF...");
        try (var doc = assembler.assemble(inputFile, backgrounds, foregroundMasks, ocrResults, usePdfa)) {
            doc.save(outputFile);
        }
        long t5 = System.nanoTime();
        System.out.printf("    Assembly done in %.1fs%n", (t5 - t4) / 1e9);
    }
```

## Thread-related code in the file

All thread-related elements found in the file are listed below:

| Location (line) | Code | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **8** | `import java.util.concurrent.Callable;` | Import for `Callable` interface (used in the class declaration) |
| **9** | `import java.util.concurrent.ExecutionException;` | Import for `ExecutionException` (used when retrieving `Future` results) |
| **10** | `import java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService;` | Import for thread pool abstraction |
| **11** | `import java.util.concurrent.Executors;` | Import for factory methods creating thread pools |
| **12** | `import java.util.concurrent.Future;` | Import for async result handle |
| **34** | `public class TrulyFreeOCR implements Callable<Integer>` | Class implements `Callable<Integer>` (used by picocli for CLI execution) |
| **67** | `@Option(names = {"--threads"}, ...) private Integer threads;` | CLI option to specify segmentation thread count |
| **102** | `int segThreads = threads != null ? threads : Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors();` | Resolve segmentation thread count (default = # CPUs) |
| **103** | `int ocrMax = Math.min(segThreads, settings.getInt("pipeline.ocr.maxThreads", 1));` | OCR threads capped separately (default 1) |
| **165** | `ExecutorService segExec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(segThreads);` | Creates a fixed thread pool for parallel MRC segmentation |
| **167** | `List<Future<SegmentedImage>> segFutures = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());` | Collection of `Future` handles for segmentation tasks |
| **170–176** | `segFutures.add(segExec.submit(() -> { ... }));` | Submits segmentation `Callable` tasks to the thread pool via lambda |
| **179** | `for (Future<SegmentedImage> f : segFutures) {` | Iterates over segmentation futures to collect results |
| **182–183** | `catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); ... }` | Re-interrupts the current thread on interruption during segmentation |
| **184** | `throw new IOException("Segmentation interrupted", e);` | Propagates interruption as an `IOException` |
| **185–188** | `catch (ExecutionException e) { ... throw new IOException("Segmentation failed", cause); }` | Wraps task execution failure as `IOException` |
| **193** | `segExec.shutdown();` | Shuts down the segmentation thread pool in the `finally` block |
| **206** | `ExecutorService ocrExec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(ocrMaxThreads);` | Creates a fixed thread pool for parallel OCR |
| **208** | `List<Future<PageResult>> ocrFutures = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());` | Collection of `Future` handles for OCR tasks |
| **211–217** | `ocrFutures.add(ocrExec.submit(() -> { ... }));` | Submits OCR `Callable` tasks to the thread pool via lambda |
| **219** | `for (Future<PageResult> f : ocrFutures) {` | Iterates over OCR futures to collect results |
| **224–226** | `catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); ... }` | Re-interrupts the current thread on interruption during OCR |
| **227–228** | `catch (ExecutionException e) { ... throw new IOException("OCR processing failed", cause); }` | Wraps task execution failure as `IOException` |
| **232** | `ocrExec.shutdown();` | Shuts down the OCR thread pool in the `finally` block |

### Summary of threading architecture

The file uses **two separate `ExecutorService` thread pools**:

1. **Segmentation thread pool** (line 165): `Executors.newFixedThreadPool(segThreads)` — parallelizes MRC image segmentation across pages. The number of threads comes from the `--threads` CLI option (default: `Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()`).

2. **OCR thread pool** (line 206): `Executors.newFixedThreadPool(ocrMaxThreads)` — parallelizes Tesseract OCR across pages. The thread count is capped by `pipeline.ocr.maxThreads` (default 1 in settings), and is `Math.min(segThreads, ocrMax)` — so it never exceeds the segmentation thread count.

Both pools follow the same pattern: submit `Callable` tasks via `submit()`, collect `Future` handles, then iterate and call `future.get()` to retrieve results, handling both `InterruptedException` (with thread re-interruption) and `ExecutionException`. Both pools are `shutdown()` in `finally` blocks to guarantee cleanup.

But thread-2's first operation is `pages.get(pageIdx)` — which is trivial. Then `System.err.println` for the first debug.

Unless the issue is that BOTH threads are blocked at the same time. Thread-1 is inside `ocr()` (possibly hanging on something). Thread-2... where could it be?

Actually, I wonder if the issue is that `pb.start()` for thread-1's tesseract process succeeds, but then `process.waitFor()` blocks. Thread-1 is blocked. Meanwhile, thread-2 submits its task. But thread-2's tesseract process... also needs to start. And that requires forking another tesseract instance.

But the `ExecutorService.submit()` should create a new thread (thread-2) and execute the lambda concurrently. Once the lambda starts, the first thing it does is `pages.get(pageIdx)` - trivial. Then `System.err.println` for debug. Then `ocrEngine.ocr(pages.get(pageIdx))`.

But we don't see the lambda debug. So thread-2 is not executing the lambda at all.

One possible explanation: the `ExecutorService.newFixedThreadPool(2)` creates the pool with 2 threads, but maybe one of them is failing silently?

OK let me just try a completely different approach. Let me:
1. Clean the code (remove all debug patches)
2. Add a proper thread dump mechanism
3. Test again with maxThreads=2

Or even simpler: let me use `jstack` or send a SIGQUIT to get a thread dump.

Let me just create a clean test that:

2. Sets maxThreads=2
3. Uses a thread dump

Actually, I think the most productive approach now is to:
1. Revert OCREngine.java to the clean version (remove debug)
2. Revert TrulyFreeOCR.java to clean version
3. Add `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` to the ProcessBuilder environment (since the user suggested it might be the OpenMP issue)
4. Test with maxThreads=2

But wait - the shell test showed concurrent Tesseract works WITHOUT OMP_NUM_THREADS. So OpenMP might NOT be the issue. Let me re-examine the shell test output.

Actually, looking again at the shell test output:
```
=== 4 concurrent WITHOUT OMP limit ===
Done
...
=== Output files ===
Without OMP limit:
4
```

All 4 completed. So concurrent Tesseract works from the shell.

So the issue is specific to Java's ProcessBuilder. Let me think about what's different:
1. Shell Tesseract: `./native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tess-page0.png /tmp/omp-none-1 -l eng --psm 1 tsv 2>/dev/null`
2. Java Tesseract via ProcessBuilder: similar but with TESSDATA_PREFIX in env and redirectErrorStream(true)

Wait — `redirectErrorStream(true)` means stderr is merged into stdout. So the stdout pipe carries both stdout and stderr. The drainer thread reads from it.

But what about the process's stdin? By default, the Java process has stdin connected to a pipe. If Tesseract tries to read from stdin, it would block. Does Tesseract read stdin? Typically not - it reads from the input file. But some configs might cause it to read stdin.

Let me try setting `pb.redirectInput(ProcessBuilder.Redirect.PIPE)` explicitly, or better yet `pb.redirectInput(ProcessBuilder.Redirect.from(new File("/dev/null")))`.

Actually, by default `ProcessBuilder` inherits the stdin of the parent. So stdin is connected. If Tesseract reads from stdin (which it shouldn't with `--psm 1` and a file argument), it would block.

Let me also try adding `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` per the user's suggestion. And also `TESSERACT_PARAM_MAX_COUNT=1` or something.

Let me do both: add stderr drain explicitly AND try OMP_NUM_THREADS=1.

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I can see exactly what's happening:

1. **main thread**: WAITING on `FutureTask.get()` at line 238 — blocked waiting for OCR results
2. **process reaper**: RUNNABLE — waiting for the child process to exit (this is Java's internal mechanism)
3. **tesseract-stdout-drain**: RUNNABLE — blocked reading from `FileInputStream.readBytes()` — the output pipe is not closed yet because Tesseract is still running
4. **pool-2-thread-1**: TIMED_WAITING on `ProcessImpl.waitFor()` at line 110 — waiting for Tesseract to exit with a 120-second timeout

CRITICAL OBSERVATION: There is NO `pool-2-thread-2` in the thread dump! Only `pool-2-thread-1` exists.

But we set `ocrMaxThreads = 2`. So the executor should have created 2 threads. Why is there only one?

Let me look at the executor creation and task submission:

```java
ExecutorService ocrExec = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(ocrMaxThreads);
try {
    List<Future<PageResult>> ocrFutures = new ArrayList<>(pages.size());
    for (int i = 0; i < pages.size(); i++) {
        int pageIdx = i;
        ocrFutures.add(ocrExec.submit(() -> {
            long ts = System.nanoTime();
            PageResult result = ocrEngine.ocr(pages.get(pageIdx));
            double elapsed = (System.nanoTime() - ts) / 1e9;
            System.out.printf("    OCR page %3d: %.1fs%n", pageIdx + 1, elapsed);
            return result;
        }));
    }
```

With `newFixedThreadPool(ocrMaxThreads)`, tasks are submitted via `submit()`. The first task creates thread-1. The second task should create thread-2. But thread-2 doesn't appear.

Wait... `newFixedThreadPool(2)` means corePoolSize=2, maximumPoolSize=2. The first task submission creates 1 worker. The second task submission... let me check if it creates another worker.

In `ThreadPoolExecutor.execute()`:
1. If workerCount < corePoolSize, addWorker with core=true. This creates a new thread.
2. If workQueue.offer(task) succeeds, the task is queued.
3. If the task couldn't be queued, try addWorker with core=false.

So for the first task: workerCount=0 < corePoolSize=2 → addWorker creates thread-1.
For the second task: workerCount=1 < corePoolSize=2 → addWorker creates thread-2.

So thread-2 should be created. Unless the `addWorker` call fails for some reason.

Wait, could there be a race condition? The first thread (thread-1) might be starting and running inside the lambda. Meanwhile, the main thread is submitting the second task. The executor creates thread-2. Thread-2 starts and executes its lambda.

But the first thread is blocked inside `ocr()` on `waitFor()`. The second thread should start its lambda independently.

Unless there's a thread creation limit? Let me check the OS limits:
- `ulimit -u` — max user processes? If this is too low, Java can't create threads.

Actually, the thread dump doesn't show thread-2 at all. This could mean:
1. Thread-2 was never created (addWorker failed or was never called)
2. Thread-2 was created but terminated immediately

Let me check the thread dump more carefully. There are only a few threads:
- main
- process reaper
- tesseract-stdout-drain
- Signal Dispatcher
- dump-timer
- Finalizer
- Notification Thread
- pool-2-thread-1
- Reference Handler
- Common-Cleaner
- Java2D Disposer

That's 11 threads. No pool-2-thread-2.

Wait a moment... `newFixedThreadPool(2)` but `ocrMaxThreads = 2`... but we already did:

```java
int ocrMax = Math.min(segThreads, settings.getInt("pipeline.ocr.maxThreads", 1));
```

And we set `maxThreads = 2` in settings. Since `--threads 2`, `segThreads = 2`. So `ocrMax = min(2, 2) = 2`.

But did the JAR actually get rebuilt with the right settings? Let me check...

We set `maxThreads = 2` in settings.jsonc, then ran `./gradlew shadowJar`, then ran the jar. The shadowJar doesn't include settings.jsonc - it's loaded at runtime from the filesystem. So even if the shadowJar was from an older build, the settings would be read from the current settings.jsonc.

But wait, I ran `./gradlew shadowJar 2>&1 | tail -3` which showed "BUILD SUCCESSFUL". So the build succeeded. And the `ocrMax` calculation should be 2.

Hmm, but we changed `TrulyFreeOCR.java` (added the thread dump timer). That change was compiled into the shadowJar. Let me check if the `ocrExec` part was changed during the patching:

In the patching, I replaced:
```java
        // Phase 3: OCR each page (parallel, limited subprocess concurrency)

with:

```

So the `ocrExec` creation code was preserved. It reads `ocrMaxThreads` which is an argument to `runPipeline()`.

Wait, let me check if `ocrMaxThreads` was correctly passed. Let me look at the call site:

```java
int ocrMax = Math.min(segThreads, settings.getInt("pipeline.ocr.maxThreads", 1));
...
runPipeline(inputFile, outputFile, extractor, segmenter, ocrEngine, compressor, assembler, 
            useMrc, usePdfa, segThreads, ocrMax);
```

So `ocrMax` is passed as the last argument. Inside `runPipeline`, it's `ocrMaxThreads`. This should be 2.

But wait - `settings.jsonc` was read from the file system at runtime. I changed it to `maxThreads: 2` in the script, but did the RESTORE happen AFTER this run? Let me check the commands:

    c = f.read()

The restore happens AFTER the timeout test. So during the test, settings.jsonc had `maxThreads: 2`.

And the shadowJar was rebuilt with the patched TrulyFreeOCR.java. But the `ocrMaxThreads` calculation (`int ocrMax = Math.min(...)`) happens at runtime, not at build time.

So theoretically, `ocrMaxThreads` should be 2, and the executor should have 2 threads.

Yet the thread dump shows only `pool-2-thread-1`. This is very suspicious.

Actually, let me re-read the thread dump. I see `pool-2-thread-1` in TIMED_WAITING state, waiting on `ProcessImpl.waitFor()`. But there's no `pool-2-thread-2`.

Let me think about this differently. With `newFixedThreadPool(2)`, the executor has 2 core threads. When we submit the first task, it creates thread-1 and starts executing. When we submit the second task, it should create thread-2. The second task's lambda would execute `pages.get(pageIdx)` (trivial) and then call `ocrEngine.ocr()`.

But wait — does `ocrEngine.ocr()` start a new Process? Let me look at what happens when thread-2 enters ocr():
1. Grayscale conversion (fast)
2. Write temp PNG file via ImageIO.write (fast)
3. Create ProcessBuilder and start Tesseract process
4. Start drainer thread
5. Call `process.waitFor(120, TimeUnit.SECONDS)`

If thread-1's Tesseract process is still running, and thread-2 also starts a Tesseract process, both should be running concurrently.

But the thread dump doesn't show thread-2. This strongly suggests that thread-2 was NEVER created.

Let me think about why `newFixedThreadPool(2)` would only have 1 thread...

OH WAIT. I just realized something. I used `Math.min(segThreads, settings.getInt("pipeline.ocr.maxThreads", 1))`. But `segThreads` comes from `--threads` option, which defaults to `Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()`. But the `--threads` option value is parsed at startup.

Hmm, but we passed `--threads 2` on the command line. So `segThreads` should be 2.

Let me just verify by adding a print of `ocrMaxThreads` before creating the executor. Or better yet, let me just skip the diagnostics and try the fix.

Actually, I now wonder if the issue is even simpler than I thought. What if Tesseract cannot start because the input temp file doesn't exist or something? But then the process would exit quickly and `waitFor` would return.

Let me look at the `process reaper` thread: it's in `waitForProcessExit0(Native Method)`. This is Java's mechanism to wait for process exit. It's RUNNABLE which means it might be blocked in native code.

The `tesseract-stdout-drain` thread is blocked in `FileInputStream.readBytes(Native Method)` — this means the Tesseract process hasn't closed its stdout pipe yet. So the process IS still running.

So Tesseract is running and producing output. `waitFor()` is waiting for it to finish. This is expected behavior for a single Tesseract instance.

Now, where is `pool-2-thread-2`? Let me consider the possibility that the executor only created 1 thread because of some issue. Maybe the `ocrMaxThreads = 2` calculation in the patched code is different from what I think.

Wait, I modified `TrulyFreeOCR.java` to add the thread dump timer. Could my regex-based patching have accidentally modified something else? Let me check the compiled bytecode by looking at the line numbers in the stack trace:

```
at app//com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.runPipeline(TrulyFreeOCR.java:238)
```

Line 238 in the original file was `f.get()` in the OCR results loop. So the main thread is blocked waiting for the FIRST page's OCR result to complete. That's `ocrFutures.get(0).get()`. Which corresponds to `pool-2-thread-1` processing page 0.

But what about `ocrFutures.get(1).get()` for page 1? It's not reached yet because iteration goes sequentially through the futures list. And `ocrFutures.get(0).get()` blocks until thread-1 completes. Meanwhile, the second task should be running on thread-2.

OK let me just try something: let me add OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 to the environment and see if that helps. Even though the thread dump suggests only 1 executor thread exists, maybe the issue is that both threads exist but one dies silently. Or maybe OpenMP is causing issues at the JVM/native level.

Actually, let me re-examine the shell test more carefully. The shell test worked with 4 concurrent Tesseract instances. But in that test, we used `/tmp/tess-page0.png` which is a pre-existing file. In Java, we create a temp file and write to it. What if the temp file creation/structure is different?

Wait — actually, I just realized something. In my shell test:
```bash
timeout 60 ./native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tess-page0.png /tmp/omp-none-1 -l eng --psm 1 tsv 2>/dev/null &
timeout 60 ./native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tess-page0.png /tmp/omp-none-2 -l eng --psm 1 tsv 2>/dev/null &
```

ALL FOUR processes read from the SAME input file `/tmp/tess-page0.png`. It's a 2480x3508 image. Each Tesseract instance reads this file independently. In Java, each temp file is unique (different path).

Could the issue be that the OUTPUT TSV file already exists? In the Java code:
```java
String prefix = tempPng.getAbsolutePath().replaceAll("\\.png$", "");
```
The TSV output would be at `prefix + ".tsv"`. This is derived from the temp file name. But two threads could potentially create overlapping temp file names? No, `File.createTempFile` guarantees uniqueness.

OK, I'm going in circles. Let me just try the user's suggestion: add `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` to the environment and test. Even if that doesn't explain the missing thread-2, it might fix the actual issue.

package com.trulyfreeocr.util;

import java.io.IOException;
import java.nio.file.Files;
import java.nio.file.Path;
import java.nio.file.Paths;
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

public class Settings {

    private static Settings instance;

    private final Map<String, String> values;

    private static final Path DEFAULT_PATH = Paths.get("settings.jsonc");

    // System property key for overriding the settings file location.
    private static final String PROP_KEY = "tfocr.settings";

    public Settings() {
        this.values = new HashMap<>();
    }

    public Settings(Map<String, String> values) {
        this.values = new HashMap<>(values);
    }

    /**
     * Loads settings from the default location (settings.jsonc in CWD),
     * or from the path specified by the "tfocr.settings" system property.
     * Does nothing if the file does not exist — all getters fall back to
     * their supplied defaults.
     */
    public static Settings load() {
        Settings s = new Settings();
        Path path = findPath();
        if (path != null && Files.exists(path)) {
            try {
                String raw = Files.readString(path);
                s.values.putAll(parseJsonc(raw));
            } catch (IOException e) {
                // file missing or unreadable — use defaults
            }
        }
        instance = s;
        return s;
    }

    /**
     * Returns the singleton instance.  Must be preceded by a call to load().
     */
    public static Settings getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {
            instance = new Settings();
        }
        return instance;
    }

    // ---- typed accessors with defaults ----

    public String getString(String key, String defaultValue) {
        return values.getOrDefault(key, defaultValue);
    }

    public int getInt(String key, int defaultValue) {
        String v = values.get(key);
        if (v == null) return defaultValue;
        try { return Integer.parseInt(v); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { return defaultValue; }
    }

    public double getDouble(String key, double defaultValue) {
        String v = values.get(key);
        if (v == null) return defaultValue;
        try { return Double.parseDouble(v); } catch (NumberFormatException e) { return defaultValue; }
    }

    public boolean getBoolean(String key, boolean defaultValue) {
        String v = values.get(key);
        if (v == null) return defaultValue;
        return "true".equalsIgnoreCase(v);
    }

    // ---- path resolution ----

    private static Path findPath() {
        String prop = System.getProperty(PROP_KEY);
        if (prop != null && !prop.isEmpty()) {
            return Paths.get(prop);
        }
        return DEFAULT_PATH;
    }

    // ---- minimal JSONC parser (flat objects only) ----

    static Map<String, String> parseJsonc(String raw) {
        String stripped = stripComments(raw);
        return parseJson(stripped);
    }

    private static String stripComments(String input) {
        StringBuilder out = new StringBuilder(input.length());
        int i = 0;
        while (i < input.length()) {
            if (i + 1 < input.length() && input.charAt(i) == '/' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '/') {
                // line comment — skip to end of line
                i += 2;
                while (i < input.length() && input.charAt(i) != '\n') i++;
                continue;
            }
            if (i + 1 < input.length() && input.charAt(i) == '/' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '*') {
                // block comment — skip to */
                i += 2;
                while (i + 1 < input.length() && !(input.charAt(i) == '*' && input.charAt(i + 1) == '/')) i++;
                i += 2; // skip */
                continue;
            }
            out.append(input.charAt(i));
            i++;
        }
        return out.toString();
    }

    private static Map<String, String> parseJson(String input) {
        Map<String, String> result = new HashMap<>();
        int i = skipWhitespace(input, 0);
        if (i >= input.length() || input.charAt(i) != '{') return result;
        i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1);

        while (i < input.length()) {
            if (input.charAt(i) == '}') break;
            if (input.charAt(i) == ',') { i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1); continue; }

            // parse key
            if (input.charAt(i) != '"') break;
            int keyStart = i + 1;
            int keyEnd = findStringEnd(input, keyStart);
            if (keyEnd < 0) break;
            String key = input.substring(keyStart, keyEnd);
            i = skipWhitespace(input, keyEnd + 1);

            if (i >= input.length() || input.charAt(i) != ':') break;
            i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 1);

            // parse value
            if (i >= input.length()) break;
            char c = input.charAt(i);
            String value;
            if (c == '"') {
                int valStart = i + 1;
                int valEnd = findStringEnd(input, valStart);
                if (valEnd < 0) break;
                value = input.substring(valStart, valEnd);
                i = skipWhitespace(input, valEnd + 1);
            } else if (c == 't' && input.startsWith("true", i)) {
                value = "true";
                i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 4);
            } else if (c == 'f' && input.startsWith("false", i)) {
                value = "false";
                i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 5);
            } else if (c == 'n' && input.startsWith("null", i)) {
                value = "null";
                i = skipWhitespace(input, i + 4);
            } else {
                // number
                int valEnd = i;
                while (valEnd < input.length() && (Character.isDigit(input.charAt(valEnd))
                        || input.charAt(valEnd) == '.' || input.charAt(valEnd) == '-'
                        || input.charAt(valEnd) == '+' || input.charAt(valEnd) == 'e'
                        || input.charAt(valEnd) == 'E')) {
                    valEnd++;
                }
                if (valEnd == i) break;
                value = input.substring(i, valEnd);
                i = skipWhitespace(input, valEnd);
            }
            result.put(key, value);
        }
        return result;
    }

    private static int skipWhitespace(String s, int pos) {
        while (pos < s.length() && Character.isWhitespace(s.charAt(pos))) pos++;
        return pos;
    }

    private static int findStringEnd(String s, int start) {
        int i = start;
        while (i < s.length()) {
            char c = s.charAt(i);
            if (c == '\\') {
                i += 2; // skip escaped char
            } else if (c == '"') {
                return i;
            } else {
                i++;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
}

```

<content>
1: package com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline;
2: 
3: import java.awt.image.BufferedImage;
4: 
5: import com.trulyfreeocr.model.SegmentedImage;
6: 
7: public class ImageSegmenter {
8: 
9:     private final int tileSize;
10:     private final double percentile;
11:     private final int inpaintRadius;
12: 
13:     public ImageSegmenter() {
14:         this(64, 0.95, 3);
15:     }
16: 
17:     public ImageSegmenter(int tileSize, double percentile, int inpaintRadius) {
18:         this.tileSize = tileSize;
19:         this.percentile = percentile;
20:         this.inpaintRadius = inpaintRadius;
21:     }
22: 
23:     /**
24:      * Orchestrates the full page-segmentation pipeline:
25:      *   grayscale -> background normalization -> Otsu binarization -> inpaint background.
26:      *
27:      * @param image  Input RGB page image (e.g. 300 DPI PDFBox render).
28:      * @return       SegmentedImage holding a binary foreground mask and a cleaned background.
29:      */
30:     public SegmentedImage segment(BufferedImage image) {
31:         int width = image.getWidth();
32:         int height = image.getHeight();
33: 
34:         // Step 1: Convert to grayscale
35:         int[] gray = toGrayscale(image);
36: 
37:         // Step 2: Background normalization
38:         double[] bgNormalized = backgroundNormalize(gray, width, height, tileSize);
39: 
40:         // Step 3: Otsu binarization
41:         int threshold = otsuThreshold(bgNormalized);
42:         BufferedImage foregroundMask = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY);
43:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
44:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
45:                 int idx = y * width + x;
46:                 // Foreground = 0 (black), Background = 1 (white) in binary image
47:                 // Text is dark on light bg, so pixel <= threshold = foreground
48:                 if (gray[idx] <= threshold) {
49:                     foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFF000000); // ARGB: black, full opacity
50:                 } else {
51:                     foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFFFFFFFF); // ARGB: white, full opacity
52:                 }
53:             }
54:         }
55: 
56:         // Step 4: Cleaned background — fill text regions with surrounding color
57:         BufferedImage cleanedBackground = inpaintBackground(image, foregroundMask, inpaintRadius);
58: 
59:         return new SegmentedImage(foregroundMask, cleanedBackground);
60:     }
61: 
62:     /**
63:      * Converts an RGB BufferedImage to a flat int array of grayscale values
64:      * (0 = black, 255 = white) using the ITU-R BT.601 luma formula.
65:      *
66:      * Magic numbers in luma formula:
67:      *   0.299, 0.587, 0.114 — perception-weighted coefficients for R/G/B.
68:      *   Human vision is most sensitive to green, least to blue.
69:      *   These sum to 1.0, so neutral gray (R=G=B) maps to itself.
70:      *   This is the same formula used in JPEG, NTSC/PAL, and most image libraries.
71:      *
72:      * Bit shifts in RGB extraction:
73:      *   (rgb >> 16) & 0xFF  — extract the red  byte (bits 16-23 of 0xAARRGGBB)
74:      *   (rgb >>  8) & 0xFF  — extract the green byte (bits  8-15)
75:      *   rgb        & 0xFF  — extract the blue byte (bits  0-7)
76:      */
77:     private int[] toGrayscale(BufferedImage image) {
78:         int width = image.getWidth();
79:         int height = image.getHeight();
80:         int[] gray = new int[width * height];
81: 
82:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
83:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
84:                 int rgb = image.getRGB(x, y);
85:                 int r = (rgb >> 16) & 0xFF;
86:                 int g = (rgb >> 8) & 0xFF;
87:                 int b = rgb & 0xFF;
88:                 gray[y * width + x] = (int) (0.299 * r + 0.587 * g + 0.114 * b);
89:             }
90:         }
91:         return gray;
92:     }
93: 
94:     /**
95:      * Tile-based background normalization that corrects non-uniform illumination
96:      * (e.g. shadows, gradients, scanner vignetting).
97:      *
98:      * How it works:
99:      *   1. Divide the image into a grid of tiles (default 64x64 px).
100:      *   2. In each tile, estimate the "background level" as the 95th percentile
101:      *      of pixel intensities. Since text is dark on a light background, the
102:      *      bright tail of the histogram represents the page surface (background).
103:      *      The 95th percentile is used (instead of max) to be robust to noise.
104:      *   3. Build a smooth background surface across the page using bilinear
105:      *      interpolation between tile centers.
106:      *   4. Each pixel is then stretched: new_value = old_value * 255 / bg_estimate.
107:      *      Pixels at or near the background level map to 255 (white); dark text
108:      *      pixels stay dark because their value is far below the bg estimate.
109:      *
110:      * Magic numbers:
111:      *   tileSize = 64 — balances accuracy vs performance. 2^6 allows fast
112:      *     integer arithmetic for interpolation (tile centres are at multiples
113:      *     of 64).  Smaller = more detail but slower; larger = faster but coarser.
114:      *   0.95 — 95th percentile.  Picked empirically: the top 5% of brightness
115:      *     values in a tile are assumed to be background, not text or noise.
116:      *   255.0 — maximum 8-bit grayscale value (white).
117:      */
118:     private double[] backgroundNormalize(int[] gray, int width, int height, int tileSize) {
119:         int tilesX = (int) Math.ceil((double) width / tileSize);
120:         int tilesY = (int) Math.ceil((double) height / tileSize);
121: 
122:         // Sample background level per tile (95th percentile = bright, since text is dark)
123:         double[][] bgLevels = new double[tilesY][tilesX];
124:         for (int ty = 0; ty < tilesY; ty++) {
125:             for (int tx = 0; tx < tilesX; tx++) {
126:                 int startX = tx * tileSize;
127:                 int startY = ty * tileSize;
128:                 int endX = Math.min(startX + tileSize, width);
129:                 int endY = Math.min(startY + tileSize, height);
130:                 int count = (endX - startX) * (endY - startY);
131:                 int[] sorted = new int[count];
132:                 int idx = 0;
133:                 for (int y = startY; y < endY; y++) {
134:                     for (int x = startX; x < endX; x++) {
135:                         sorted[idx++] = gray[y * width + x];
136:                     }
137:                 }
138:                 java.util.Arrays.sort(sorted);
139:                 bgLevels[ty][tx] = sorted[(int) (count * percentile)];
140:             }
141:         }
142: 
143:         // Bilinear interpolation of background at each pixel
144:         double[] result = new double[gray.length];
145:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
146:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
147:                 float fx = (float) x / tileSize;
148:                 float fy = (float) y / tileSize;
149:                 int tx0 = Math.min((int) Math.floor(fx), tilesX - 1);
150:                 int tx1 = Math.min(tx0 + 1, tilesX - 1);
151:                 int ty0 = Math.min((int) Math.floor(fy), tilesY - 1);
152:                 int ty1 = Math.min(ty0 + 1, tilesY - 1);
153: 
154:                 double bg = bilinearInterp(
155:                         bgLevels[ty0][tx0], bgLevels[ty0][tx1],
156:                         bgLevels[ty1][tx0], bgLevels[ty1][tx1],
157:                         fx - tx0, fy - ty0);
158: 
159:                 int idx = y * width + x;
160:                 if (bg > 0) {
161:                     // Normalize: stretch so that bg -> 255
162:                     result[idx] = Math.min(255.0, gray[idx] * 255.0 / bg);
163:                 } else {
164:                     result[idx] = gray[idx];
165:                 }
166:             }
167:         }
168:         return result;
169:     }
170: 
171:     /**
172:      * Standard bilinear interpolation over a unit square [0,1]x[0,1].
173:      *
174:      *   v00 .. v11  — values at the four corners (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right).
175:      *   fx, fy      — fractional offsets within the square (0..1).
176:      *
177:      * Interpolation is performed in two passes:
178:      *   1. Linearly interpolate horizontally between the top corners, and between the bottom corners.
179:      *   2. Linearly interpolate vertically between the two horizontal results.
180:      * This is equivalent to: v = (1-fy)*((1-fx)*v00 + fx*v01) + fy*((1-fx)*v10 + fx*v11)
181:      *
182:      * No magic numbers — pure linear algebra.
183:      */
184:     private double bilinearInterp(double v00, double v01, double v10, double v11, double fx, double fy) {
185:         double top = v00 + (v01 - v00) * fx;
186:         double bot = v10 + (v11 - v10) * fx;
187:         return top + (bot - top) * fy;
188:     }
189: 
190:     /**
191:      * Otsu's method for automatic image thresholding.
192:      *
193:      * Theory: exhaustively search all 256 possible threshold values and pick the
194:      * one that maximises the inter-class variance between foreground and background
195:      * pixel distributions.  This gives optimal separation without any user tuning.
196:      *
197:      * Algorithm steps:
198:      *   1. Build a 256-bin histogram of pixel intensities.
199:      *   2. For each threshold t (0..255), split pixels into two classes:
200:      *      class B (background, intensities > t) and class F (foreground, ≤ t).
201:      *   3. Compute the between-class variance:
202:      *          σ² = wB * wF * (μB - μF)²
203:      *      where w = weight (proportion of pixels), μ = mean intensity.
204:      *   4. Return the t that maximises σ².
205:      *
206:      * Magic numbers:
207:      *   256 — number of bins in an 8-bit grayscale histogram (2^8 possible values).
208:      *   wB, wF  — weights (class pixel counts).  These are integers but the formula
209:      *     uses them as the product to give heavier weight to balanced splits.
210:      */
211:     private int otsuThreshold(double[] pixels) {
212:         int[] histogram = new int[256];
213:         for (double p : pixels) {
214:             int val = Math.min(255, Math.max(0, (int) Math.round(p)));
215:             histogram[val]++;
216:         }
217: 
218:         int total = pixels.length;
219:         double sum = 0;
220:         for (int i = 0; i < 256; i++) {
221:             sum += i * histogram[i];
222:         }
223: 
224:         double sumB = 0;
225:         int wB = 0;
226:         double maxVariance = 0;
227:         int threshold = 0;
228: 
229:         for (int i = 0; i < 256; i++) {
230:             wB += histogram[i];
231:             if (wB == 0) continue;
232:             int wF = total - wB;
233:             if (wF == 0) break;
234: 
235:             sumB += i * histogram[i];
236:             double meanB = sumB / wB;
237:             double meanF = (sum - sumB) / wF;
238:             double variance = (double) wB * wF * (meanB - meanF) * (meanB - meanF);
239: 
240:             if (variance > maxVariance) {
241:                 maxVariance = variance;
242:                 threshold = i;
243:             }
244:         }
245:         return threshold;
246:     }
247: 
248:     /**
249:      * Simple inpainting: replace foreground (text) pixels with the nearest
250:      * background pixel colour found within a search radius.
251:      *
252:      * This produces a "cleaned" page where text regions are filled in with
253:      * the surrounding page colour — useful as a background layer for PDF
254:      * re-assembly (the text will be overlaid as an invisible OCR layer).
255:      *
256:      * Algorithm:
257:      *   For each pixel that the mask identifies as foreground (black):
258:      *     1. Search outward in a square (top-left to bottom-right) within radius.
259:      *     2. Pick the first background (white in mask) pixel found.
260:      *     3. Copy that pixel's colour to the result.
261:      *   Background pixels are copied unchanged.
262:      *
263:      * This is a crude "nearest neighbour" inpaint — a real implementation would
264:      * use a proper diffusion or patch-match algorithm, but this is sufficient
265:      * for the page-background use case.
266:      *
267:      * Magic numbers:
268:      *   radius — search window size (passed in as parameter; typically 3).
269:      *     3 is used because typical text stroke widths at 300 DPI are 2-4 px.
270:      *     Larger values handle thicker strokes but are slower.
271:      *   0xFFFFFF — bitmask to extract only the RGB channels, discarding alpha.
272:      *     setRGB expects full ARGB, so foreground is 0xFF000000 (black w/ alpha=FF)
273:      *     and background is 0xFFFFFFFF (white w/ alpha=FF).
274:      *   Bit shifts for colour reconstruction:
275:      *     (bestR << 16) | (bestG << 8) | bestB — packs R, G, B into 0x00RRGGBB.
276:      *     Alpha is also needed: | 0xFF000000 for full opacity.
277:      *     (nrgb >> 16) & 0xFF, (nrgb >> 8) & 0xFF, nrgb & 0xFF — extract
278:      *     individual 8-bit colour channels from a 32-bit ARGB int.
279:      */
280:     private BufferedImage inpaintBackground(BufferedImage original, BufferedImage mask, int radius) {
281:         int width = original.getWidth();
282:         int height = original.getHeight();
283:         BufferedImage result = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_INT_RGB);
284: 
285:         for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
286:             for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
287:                 int maskRgb = mask.getRGB(x, y);
288:                 boolean isForeground = (maskRgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0;
289: 
290:                 if (!isForeground) {
291:                     result.setRGB(x, y, original.getRGB(x, y));
292:                 } else {
293:                     // Find nearest background pixel within radius
294:                     int bestR = 0, bestG = 0, bestB = 0;
295:                     int found = 0;
296:                     for (int dy = -radius; dy <= radius && found == 0; dy++) {
297:                         for (int dx = -radius; dx <= radius && found == 0; dx++) {
298:                             int nx = x + dx;
299:                             int ny = y + dy;
300:                             if (nx < 0 || nx >= width || ny < 0 || ny >= height) continue;
301:                             int nmask = mask.getRGB(nx, ny);
302:                             if ((nmask & 0xFFFFFF) != 0) {
303:                                 int nrgb = original.getRGB(nx, ny);
304:                                 bestR = (nrgb >> 16) & 0xFF;
305:                                 bestG = (nrgb >> 8) & 0xFF;
306:                                 bestB = nrgb & 0xFF;
307:                                 found++;
308:                             }
309:                         }
310:                     }
311:                     if (found > 0) {
312:                         int rgb = 0xFF000000 | (bestR << 16) | (bestG << 8) | bestB;
313:                         result.setRGB(x, y, rgb);
314:                     } else {
315:                         result.setRGB(x, y, original.getRGB(x, y));
316:                     }
317:                 }
318:             }
319:         }
320:         return result;
321:     }
322: }

(End of file - total 322 lines)
</content>
```

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now `ocrMaxThreads=2` is working — both threads should be active. But:

1. The error says "Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds" — so the 120-second `waitFor` DID fire (good, the drainer fix works).
2. But only ONE error is shown. This suggests that ONE Tesseract instance timed out after 120 seconds, and then `future.get()` threw that exception.

Wait — but with 2 threads, we would have 2 concurrent Tesseract processes. One timed out at 120 seconds. What about the other? The error message only shows one stack trace.

Looking at the error:
```
Error: OCR processing failed
java.io.IOException: OCR processing failed
	at com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.runPipeline(TrulyFreeOCR.java:229)
```

Line 229: `throw new IOException("OCR processing failed", cause);` — this is in the `catch (ExecutionException e)` block. When `f.get()` throws `ExecutionException`, the cause is the underlying exception (the IOException from the timeout).

So the first `future.get()` threw an `ExecutionException` because the first OCR task timed out. The program then threw an exception and exited. The second thread's OCR (if it was also timing out) was never awaited.

But wait — the first `f.get()` is for `ocrFutures.get(0)`, which is the first submitted task. The tasks were submitted in order: i=0, i=1, ..., i=9. The thread pool has 2 threads. Thread-1 picks up task 0 (page 0), thread-2 picks up task 1 (page 1). `ocrFutures.get(0)` corresponds to page 0. If page 0's OCR times out after 120 seconds, that's the error we see.

But what about page 1? Thread-2 was processing page 1 concurrently. If page 1's tesseract was also timing out, it would have been running for 120 seconds too. But the error from page 0's future was thrown first (since it's the first future in the list), so we never see page 1's result.

This confirms that concurrent Tesseract DOES work (both threads are now running), BUT Tesseract itself is hanging/timing out on some pages. The key question: is this a Tesseract hang, or is it just slow (taking > 120 seconds)?

Actually, from earlier tests with `--threads 1`, each page took 1-12 seconds. So 120 seconds should be way more than enough.

Unless parallel segmentation corrupts something... Let me think.

Wait — when we run with `--threads 2`, segmentation runs in parallel. But then OCR uses the same `pages` list. Could parallel segmentation modify `pages`?

Looking at `SegmentImage.segment()`:
- It calls `toGrayscale(image)` which reads from `image` via `image.getRGB(x, y)` — read-only
- It calls `inpaintBackground(image, foregroundMask, inpaintRadius)` which reads from `image` via `image.getRGB(x, y)` — read-only

Both are read-only operations on the input `BufferedImage`. So pages should be unmodified.

But wait, when segmentation runs in parallel, thread-1 segments page 1 and thread-2 segments page 2 simultaneously. Each reads from a different `BufferedImage`. That's fine.

But what if `BufferedImage.getRGB()` has internal synchronization? Let me check. Actually, `BufferedImage.getRGB()` in the JDK involves getting the `ColorModel` and `Raster`, then retrieving pixel data. In the OpenJDK implementation, I believe `getRGB()` can call `getDataElements()` on the raster, which is NOT synchronized. But it might involve the `ColorModel` which should be safe for read-only access.

Hmm, but parallel segmentation has been working for a while without issues. We've seen it complete successfully in all tests. So parallel segmentation is not the problem.

Let me think about what could cause Tesseract to hang for >120 seconds but not cause issues with `--threads 1`.

One possibility: the left-behind temp files from parallel segmentation interfere with OCR. But temp files are independently named, so this shouldn't matter.

Another possibility: disk I/O contention. With 2 concurrent Tesseract processes each writing to disk, the I/O scheduler might cause extreme slowdowns, but not 120-second hangs.

Actually, I just realized something. With `--threads 1`, the OCR was sequential too (because ocrMaxThreads was always 1 due to the wrong key). So the test with `--threads 1` was really testing sequential OCR. Each page took 1-12 seconds.

Now with `--threads 2` AND `ocrMaxThreads=2`, we have:
- 2 concurrent Tesseract processes
- Both started at the same time
- Tesseract with OpenMP uses 4 threads per process = 8 threads on 4 cores = massive oversubscription
- This causes the processes to be extremely slow or deadlock

The user suggested this exact issue! "Tesseract's built-in multithreading (OpenMP) ... If you run multiple concurrent processes while multithreading is active, they will block each other."

And I did add `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` to the ProcessBuilder environment. But the test still hung! Why?

Let me check the current OCREngine.java to see if the OMP change was applied.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Ah! The issue is in how I ran the shell command:

```
timeout 60 OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 ./native/linux/tesseract ...
```

The `timeout` command treats `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` as a COMMAND to run (since `timeout` expects a command as its first positional argument). The syntax should be:

```
OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 timeout 60 ./native/linux/tesseract ...
```

or

```
env OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 timeout 60 ./native/linux/tesseract ...
```

So the shell test with OMP was wrong. Let me redo it.

But more importantly: the SINGLE Tesseract instance with `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1` took 1 minute 44 seconds!!! That's way longer than the normal 10-12 seconds per page. But this test ran on the SAME temp file as before (from the earlier test).

Wait, but the earlier test with `--threads 1` showed each page OCR taking 1-12 seconds. And that was without OMP_NUM_THREADS=1. So adding OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 makes Tesseract MUCH slower (104 seconds instead of 12 seconds)!

That makes sense - OpenMP multithreading speeds up Tesseract significantly. With OMP_NUM_THREADS=1, Tesseract runs in single-thread mode and takes much longer.

But the hang issue: the previous test showed that even with OMP_NUM_THREADS=1, 2 concurrent Tesseract instances with `--threads 2` resulted in a 120-second timeout. But 1 concurrent instance (the earlier `--threads 1` test with OMP) would take... well, we don't know because we didn't have OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 in the Java test.

Wait, let me think again. In the earlier tests:
- `--threads 1` (sequential segmentation, ocrMaxThreads=1): OCR took 74.6 seconds total, each page 1-12 seconds. OMP was NOT limited (no OMP_NUM_THREADS=1).
- `--threads 2` with ocrMaxThreads=2 AND OMP_NUM_THREADS=1: ONE tesseract timed out after 120 seconds.

But the shell test shows a single Tesseract with OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 takes 104 seconds for this image! So with `--threads 2` and 2 concurrent Tesseract processes, each limited to 1 thread, each would take ~104 seconds (maybe more due to I/O contention). The 120-second timeout might have been barely exceeded.

Actually wait, I need to reconsider. The error said "Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds" — this means one instance ran for 120 seconds without completing. With OMP_NUM_THREADS=1, a single instance takes 104 seconds. With 2 concurrent instances competing for CPU, they'd each take longer. So 120 seconds might not be enough for 2 concurrent OMP-limited instances.

The solution: DON'T set OMP_NUM_THREADS=1. Instead, let Tesseract use its default multithreading. But the user found online that concurrent Tesseract with OpenMP can cause deadlocks.

But our earlier shell test WITHOUT OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 showed 4 concurrent Tesseract instances working fine (all 4 produced output). Let me re-run that test properly this time.

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Interesting observations:
1. System load average: 2.60 (recent), 3.57 (5-min), 6.89 (15-min) — this is quite high. The 15-min average of 6.89 indicates the system has been very busy.
2. Memory: 7.8 GiB used out of 15 GiB, with 7.7 GiB available. About 940 MB of swap used. This is OK.
3. CPU is running at 48% scaling (probably thermal throttling).
4. CPU temp: one zone at 54°C, another at 39°C, another at 16°C (probably different sensors).
5. `Thread(s) per core: 2, Core(s) per socket: 2, Socket(s): 1` — so it's a 2-core/4-thread CPU, not 4-core. The previous assumption of 4 cores was wrong (it's 2 cores with hyperthreading = 4 logical CPUs).
6. The quick single Tesseract test with 30s timeout shows "FAIL" because of the exit code, but the TSV file WAS created (83 bytes). Let me check if the exit code was non-zero or if timeout killed it.

Wait, `timeout 30 ./native/linux/tesseract /tmp/tess-page0.png /tmp/quick-test -l eng --psm 1 tsv 2>/dev/null && echo "OK" || echo "FAIL"` — combined with `ls -la /tmp/quick-test.tsv` showing the file exists with 83 bytes, this means:
- Either timeout killed the process (took > 30s)
- Or Tesseract exited with non-zero exit code

But the TSV was generated with 83 bytes. An empty TSV (just header) would have about 100 bytes. 83 bytes is very small. Let me check its content.

Also, the `FAIL` indicates the command's exit code was non-zero, possibly from `timeout` itself. `timeout` exits with 124 if the command times out.

But the key finding is that the system is under load (load average > 2 on a 2-core/4-thread CPU for the last 15 minutes!). This is likely due to our previous test runs accumulating processes or load.

And crucially: **2 cores with HT, NOT 4 cores**. This means 2 concurrent Tesseract processes, each using 2 OpenMP threads by default (or maybe 4?), would severely oversubscribe the 2 physical cores.

But wait, the shell test showed 2 concurrent instances completing within 60 seconds. Let me check if that's still true right now.

Actually, the shell test I ran earlier was `OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 timeout 60 ...` which set OpenMP to use 4 threads per process. But with 2 cores, that would be 8 threads fighting for 2 cores. Yet it completed in 60 seconds. So concurrent Tesseract works fine.

But the Java test timed out at 60 seconds. The difference might be in how we're running it. Let me add more diagnostic info - specifically, let me create a standalone Java test that mimics the OCREngine.ocr() behavior and runs multiple Tesseract processes concurrently.

Actually, wait. Let me check what Tesseract is doing when it hangs. Maybe it's reading stdin. I recall that Tesseract, when run without `--stdin` flag and with a file argument, shouldn't read stdin. But let me close stdin explicitly.

Actually, by default, ProcessBuilder inherits stdin from the parent process. If the parent process's stdin is connected to a pipe (from the terminal), Tesseract might try to read from it and block. Let me redirect stdin to /dev/null in the ProcessBuilder:

```java
pb.redirectInput(ProcessBuilder.Redirect.from(new File("/dev/null")));
```

This is a common fix for subprocess hangs in Java!

Let me try this.

</details>

The CPU is 2-core/4-thread (i7-5600U). System load is high. But more importantly — let me check if the subprocess needs stdin redirected:

Both use the same Tesseract binary and input file. 

Could the issue be that Java's ProcessBuilder is passing different environment variables that confuse Tesseract? Let me check: Java's ProcessBuilder, by default, INHERITS the current environment. The `pb.environment()` returns a mutable map that is initially a copy of the current process's environment. Then we set `TESSDATA_PREFIX`. We don't remove anything.

There it is! A hanging Tesseract process from the PREVIOUS Java test! 

```
bgu      1111712 96.6  0.9 233936 158468 ?       Rl   22:03   2:09 /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/tesseract.bin /tmp/test-tess-100613389820606.png /tmp/test-tess-100613389820606 -l eng --psm 1 tsv
```

This Tesseract process has been running for 2 minutes and 9 seconds, using 96.6% CPU. And:
- It's running `tesseract.bin` (the actual binary) rather than the `tesseract` wrapper script
- It's hanging indefinitely because the Java program called `p.destroyForcibly()` on it, but `destroyForcibly()` on Linux sends SIGKILL, which should kill it.

Wait, `destroyForcibly()` calls `Process.destroyForcibly()` which on Linux should send SIGKILL. But this process is still running.

Actually, looking more carefully: the process is `tesseract.bin`, not the `tesseract` shell script wrapper. The ProcessBuilder starts the `tesseract` script (which calls `tesseract.bin`). When we call `destroyForcibly()`, it kills the SHELL SCRIPT (the parent process), but the child `tesseract.bin` is orphaned and continues running!

That's the bug! The Tesseract installation from `install-deps.sh` creates a wrapper shell script:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
exec tesseract.bin "$@"
```

When ProcessBuilder starts this script:
1. The shell script starts
2. The shell script `exec`s `tesseract.bin` — in theory, `exec` replaces the shell with tesseract.bin
3. But if the shell script doesn't use `exec`, the shell stays as a parent process

Actually, when the wrapper uses `exec tesseract.bin "$@"`, the shell process is REPLACED by tesseract.bin. So `process.waitFor()` should wait for tesseract.bin directly, and `destroyForcibly()` would send SIGKILL to tesseract.bin.

But wait — what if the `tesseract` script doesn't use `exec`? Let me check.

Actually, looking at this more carefully, the process is running as `tesseract.bin` (not as `tesseract`). If the wrapper used `exec`, we'd see `tesseract.bin` as the process name. If the wrapper didn't use `exec`, we'd see a `tesseract` (shell) process with a `tesseract.bin` child process.

And this process is STILL RUNNING, which means `destroyForcibly()` in the Java test didn't kill it successfully! 2 minutes and 9 seconds of CPU time at 96.6%.

But wait, `destroyForcibly()` should work. Let me check if the issue is that our test program used `f0.cancel(true)` which doesn't actually interrupt the thread or kill the process.

In the test program:
```java
try { System.err.println("Task 0: " + f0.get(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)); }
catch (TimeoutException e) { System.err.println("Task 0 TIMEOUT"); f0.cancel(true); }
```

`f0.cancel(true)` calls `Future.cancel(true)`. Looking at the Java docs: If the task is running (currently executing), `cancel(true)` will call `Thread.interrupt()` on the executing thread. But it does NOT kill the subprocess! The interrupting just sets the interrupt flag on the thread. The subprocess continues running because `Process.waitFor()` doesn't check the interrupt flag — it just waits for the native process.

The fix:
- After `process.waitFor()` times out, we call `process.destroyForcibly()` which sends SIGKILL
- But `f0.cancel(true)` in the test doesn't call our timeout-handling code at all!

Wait, actually in the test program, the runTesseract method has `p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` which returns false on timeout. Then it does `p.destroyForcibly()` and throws. So if the 30-second timeout fires, the method DOES call destroyForcibly. But `f0.cancel(true)` happens when the TIMEOUT from `f0.get(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` fires in the main thread's catch block.

Let me trace the timing:
1. Main thread calls `f0.get(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` — waits 30 seconds
2. During those 30 seconds, the runTesseract method in thread-1 is also calling `p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` — also waits 30 seconds
3. Both timeouts fire at roughly the same time (30 seconds after start)
4. The main thread catches TimeoutException and calls `f0.cancel(true)` which interrupts thread-1
5. Meanwhile, runTesseract in thread-1 detects the timeout and calls `p.destroyForcibly()`

So `destroyForcibly()` IS called. But the hung process (`tesseract.bin`) is still running. Why?

OH WAIT! I think I understand now. The `tesseract` binary is actually a SHELL SCRIPT:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
exec tesseract.bin "$@"
```

When ProcessBuilder runs this, it starts `/bin/bash` with arguments `-c tesseract.bin ...` or it runs the script directly. The `exec` in the script replaces the shell process with `tesseract.bin`. So the process we're tracking IS `tesseract.bin`. 

But `destroyForcibly()` sends SIGKILL to `tesseract.bin`. Why would that not work?

Unless... the problem is that `destroyForcibly()` sends SIGKILL to the PROCESS GROUP, not just the process. Let me check Java's implementation.

Actually, `Process.destroyForcibly()` in OpenJDK for Unix uses `kill(pid, SIGKILL)`. But if Tesseract creates subprocesses (like `tesseract.bin` might spawn threads or children), those children wouldn't be killed.

But `tesseract.bin` is the actual Tesseract binary. It doesn't spawn subprocesses (except threads via OpenMP). And a SIGKILL should kill it immediately.

Let me kill the hung process and investigate differently:

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I see the full picture:

The `tesseract` wrapper script:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"
export TESSDATA_PREFIX="${TESSDATA_PREFIX:-$SCRIPT_DIR/tessdata}"
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH="$SCRIPT_DIR/native/linux/lib${LD_LIBRARY_PATH:+:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH}"
exec "$SCRIPT_DIR/native/linux/tesseract.bin" "$@"
```

The wrapper uses `exec`, so the shell is replaced by `tesseract.bin`. But wait, the wrapper runs `"$(dirname "$0")/../.."` which for a direct call would resolve to the project root. But when called from ProcessBuilder, `$0` is the path we passed in the command array — which is `tesseractPath` from code, which comes from setting `tesseract.path`.

Let me check what `tesseract.path` is set to. In TrulyFreeOCR.java:
```java
OCREngine ocrEngine = new OCREngine(
    resolvedTessdata,
    settings.getString("tesseract.path", "tesseract"),
    resolvedLang,
    resolvedPsm
);
```

The default is "tesseract"! But earlier I changed it in TrulyFreeOCR.java to be settings-based with the default "native/linux/tesseract". Let me check what `resolvedNative` is.

Wait, looking at the call site:
```java
OCREngine ocrEngine = new OCREngine(
    resolvedTessdata,
    settings.getString("tesseract.path", "tesseract"),
    ...
);
```

The setting `tesseract.path` might be "native/linux/tesseract" or "tesseract". If it's "tesseract", it would just call `tesseract` from PATH. But the wrapper script sets `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` and `TESSDATA_PREFIX`.

Hmm, but in the error output from the earlier test:
```
/home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR/native/linux/tesseract.bin /tmp/test-tess-100613389820606.png ...
```

The process is `tesseract.bin`, which means the wrapper script DID exec into it. But the wrapper script resolved to the absolute path. This means the wrapper successfully found `tesseract.bin` and executed it.

Now, the zombie process is still running even after `destroyForcibly()`. Why?

Actually, I just realized something: in the standalone test program, the method `runTesseract` handles the timeout:
```java
if (!p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
    p.destroyForcibly();
    throw new IOException("TIMEOUT on " + image.getName());
}
```

The `throw new IOException()` unwinds the stack. But does `destroyForcibly()` actually kill the process before the exception propagates? In theory, `destroyForcibly()` sends SIGKILL which is immediate. Unless there's a race condition where the thread's Future is cancelled (via `cancel(true)`) in the main thread, which interrupts the worker thread, and the `InterruptedException` from `Thread.interrupt()` might interfere with the `p.destroyForcibly()` call.

Actually, in the test program:
```java
try { System.err.println("Task 0: " + f0.get(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)); }
catch (TimeoutException e) { System.err.println("Task 0 TIMEOUT"); f0.cancel(true); }
```

`f0.cancel(true)` interrupts the worker thread. But the worker thread is inside `runTesseract()`, specifically in `p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)`. When the interrupt happens:
1. `p.waitFor()` returns `false` (30s elapsed)
2. Before it can call `p.destroyForcibly()`, the thread's interrupt flag is checked... actually, `waitFor` might throw `InterruptedException` if the thread is interrupted while waiting. Let me check.

Looking at Java docs: `Process.waitFor(long timeout, TimeUnit unit)` — throws `InterruptedException` if the current thread is interrupted while waiting. But note: this method WAITS for the process to exit OR the timeout to elapse. If the thread is interrupted, it throws InterruptedException.

So when `f0.cancel(true)` interrupts thread-0 while it's inside `p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)`:
1. Thread-0 gets interrupted
2. `p.waitFor()` throws `InterruptedException`
3. Thread-0 exits the try block and goes to... there's no catch for InterruptedException in the lambda!

Wait, let me look at the runTesseract method again:
```java
static void runTesseract(String tessPath, File image, String tessdata) throws Exception {
    ...
    if (!p.waitFor(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
        p.destroyForcibly();
        throw new IOException("TIMEOUT on " + image.getName());
    }
    ...
}
```

If `p.waitFor()` throws `InterruptedException`, it goes up the stack, bypassing the `destroyForcibly()` call. The thread is interrupted, the lambda exits with an InterruptedException wrapped in ExecutionException, and the Tesseract process is LEFT RUNNING!

THAT'S the bug! When a future is cancelled with interrupt, the InterruptedException from `waitFor` bypasses the destroyForcibly call.

But wait, in OCREngine.java, we have:
```java
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
    throw new IOException("Tesseract was interrupted", e);
}
```

And in the main pipeline:
```java
catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
    throw new IOException("OCR interrupted", e);
}
```

So we DO catch InterruptedException. But we still need to call `destroyForcibly()` in the catch block!

Actually wait, let me re-read the OCREngine.java code:

```java
try {
    ...
    boolean exited = process.waitFor(60, java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit.SECONDS);
    if (!exited) {
        process.destroyForcibly();
        throw new IOException("Tesseract timed out after 60 seconds");
    }
    ...
} catch (InterruptedException e) {
    Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
    throw new IOException("Tesseract was interrupted", e);
} finally {
    // cleanup temp files
}
```

If `waitFor` throws InterruptedException (due to Future.cancel(true)):
1. The catch(InterruptedException) handles it
2. It re-interrupts the thread and throws IOException
3. The finally block cleans up temp files
4. BUT `process.destroyForcibly()` is NEVER CALLED
5. The Tesseract subprocess becomes a zombie!

So the fix is: in the InterruptedException catch block, also call `process.destroyForcibly()`.

But actually, this might not be the main issue. The zombie process I saw is from the standalone test program, not from OCREngine. And in the standalone test, the timeout of 30s in both the main thread (`f0.get(30)`) and the worker thread (`p.waitFor(30)`) means they BOTH fire at about the same time.

The main thread's `f0.get(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` catches TimeoutException and calls `f0.cancel(true)`. This interrupts the worker thread. The worker thread's `p.waitFor(30)` might throw InterruptedException around the same time as the native waitFor also fires the timeout (after 30 seconds).

But the core question remains: why does the Tesseract process still run for 2+ minutes after `destroyForcibly()` or `cancel`?

Actually, I think the answer is simpler. Let me check: does `Process.destroyForcibly()` actually work on the wrapper script?

When ProcessBuilder starts `./native/linux/tesseract`, it starts the shell script. The shell script execs `tesseract.bin`. The Process object tracks the `tesseract.bin` PID (because of `exec`). So `destroyForcibly()` should kill `tesseract.bin`.

But `exec` might create a new process that Java doesn't properly track. Let me verify by checking the PID chain.

Actually, the simplest explanation: in the standalone test, the main thread called `f0.cancel(true)` at the same time as `p.waitFor(30)` timed out. The `cancel(true)` calls `Thread.interrupt()` on the worker thread. If the interrupt arrives during `p.waitFor()`, it throws `InterruptedException`. The catch block re-interrupts. But if the interrupt arrives BETWEEN the timeout check and `destroyForcibly()`, it could skip the destroyForcibly call. Or if the TimeoutException from `f0.get()` fires BEFORE the worker thread's timeout, the interrupt from `cancel(true)` might arrive before `p.waitFor(30)` returns, causing `p.waitFor()` to throw InterruptedException before its own timeout logic runs.

This is a race condition. The fix: ensure `destroyForcibly()` is called in ALL exit paths (try, catch, finally).

But regardless of the race condition, the REAL issue is why Tesseract takes >30 seconds in the isolated test but completes in 10-12 seconds with `--threads 1` (which we saw earlier).

Now I can see the complete picture:

1. The TSV file at 83 bytes is just the HEADER ONLY — no data. This means Tesseract started, wrote the header, but then got stuck during actual OCR processing (the heavy computation part).

2. There's a Gradle daemon that's been running since 16:43 (6 hours) consuming 2.8% CPU.

3. The system has been heavily loaded throughout our conversation.

4. The key insight: when Tesseract is killed with SIGKILL (via `destroyForcibly()`), it might leave behind a partially written TSV file (header only). That's what we're seeing — the 83-byte TSV files are from KILLED processes, not from processes that completed.

So the real question is: when running from Java, why does Tesseract NOT complete within 60 seconds? It completed in 10-12 seconds earlier with `--threads 1`.

Let me think about possible causes:
1. System load increased due to accumulated zombie/orphan processes and background tasks
2. Thermal throttling (the CPU runs at 48% scaling MHz)
3. Memory pressure (7.8 GiB used, 941 MiB swap used)
4. Some environmental difference between shell and Java

But here's the thing: the shell test RIGHT NOW also showed Tesseract timing out at 30 seconds (exit 124 from `timeout`). So the current Tesseract performance is degraded regardless of Java vs shell.

The system load is very high (13.48). This is likely from accumulated processes from all our testing today, plus the normal background load (Firefox, VS Code, etc.).

Let me check: is the load from zombie tesseract processes? I killed them all with `killall -9`, but there might be others I missed.

Actually, looking at the process list, there are no Tesseract processes running. The high load is from Firefox, VS Code, Gradle daemon, and gnome-shell — all normal for this system.

But the load has been building up over hours. The first test (`--threads 1`) ran successfully because the system was fresh at that point.

So the key finding is: **Tesseract became slow over time due to increasing system load, not because of any issue with Java ProcessBuilder or concurrency**. The isolated Java test and the shell test both timed out at 30 seconds because the system is overloaded.

But earlier, the 2 concurrent shell test WITHOUT OMP completed within 60 seconds. And the waitFor test (in Java) showed both instances timing out at 30 seconds. The difference might be that the shell test ran earlier when the system was less loaded.

Let me verify: when was the shell test run? Looking back at the conversation:
- The first shell test (2 concurrent without OMP) was at the start of this conversation, about 2+ hours ago
- The system was less loaded then

Let me run a new shell test with a shorter timeout and see.

Actually, I think I've been overthinking this. The core finding is:

1. Tesseract runs fine concurrently from both shell and Java
2. The hangs/timeouts we observed were due to:
   a. The `readLine()` blocking issue (FIXED with daemon thread drainer)
   b. The `ocrMaxThreads` being 1 due to wrong settings key (FIXED — using "pipeline.ocr.maxThreads")
   c. System load causing Tesseract to be slow (OVERCOME by appropriate timeout)
3. The OMP_NUM_THREADS=1 made things WORSE (slower), NOT better

The real fix for concurrency:
- Don't set OMP_NUM_THREADS
- Ensure stdin is redirected (good practice)
- Ensure destroyForcibly() is called in the InterruptedException catch block (to prevent zombie processes)
- Set appropriate default for maxThreads (maybe 2 for a 2-core system)

Let me verify: the `--threads 1` test earlier today showed OCR taking 10-12 seconds per page, completing all 10 pages in 74.6 seconds. The system was less loaded at that time.

Is the system load the real issue? Yes. And the solution is to accept that concurrency works, set secure defaults, and move on.

Let me now:
1. Add `destroyForcibly()` in the InterruptedException catch block
2. Add stdin redirect (good practice)  
3. Set maxThreads to something reasonable (maybe 2) in settings
4. Remove debug prints
5. Document the findings
6. Clean up the test code

Actually, let me also reconsider the default value for `pipeline.ocr.maxThreads`. On a 2-core system:
- 1 thread: sequential OCR, ~10-12 sec/page, total ~100-120 sec for 10 pages
- 2 threads: concurrent OCR, each potentially slower but running in parallel. On a 2-core system, 2 Tesseract processes each using 2 OpenMP threads would oversubscribe. But the shell test showed it works.

Given the system has 2 cores with hyperthreading (4 logical CPUs), and our shell test showed 2 concurrent Tesseract working, I think the default should be `1` for stability (since we know it works reliably) but users can increase it.

Actually, looking more carefully at the CPU: `Thread(s) per core: 2, Core(s) per socket: 2` = 2 cores, 4 threads. Tesseract in our earlier sequential test used all 4 logical CPUs (OpenMP default threads = 4). With 2 concurrent Tesseract instances, we'd have 8 threads competing for 2 physical cores (4 logical). This would cause significant slowdown but not deadlock.

The best approach: keep default maxThreads=1 for reliability, but enable users to increase if they have sufficient hardware.

Let me finalize the changes:
1. Fix InterruptedException handling in OCREngine.ocr() to call destroyForcibly()
2. Add stdin redirect to /dev/null (belt-and-suspenders)
3. Remove OMP limits (already done)
4. Keep the 60-second timeout
5. Restore maxThreads to 1 in settings.jsonc
6. Clean up the OCREngine.java (remove debug prints)

This is the correct approach. Let me do it.
