```
10: import org.apache.pdfbox.Loader;
11: import org.apache.pdfbox.cos.COSName;
12: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDDocument;
13: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDDocumentCatalog;
14: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDPage;
15: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.PDPageContentStream;
16: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.common.PDRectangle;
17: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.font.PDFont;
18: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.font.PDType0Font;
19: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.font.PDType1Font;
20: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.font.Standard14Fonts;
21: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.color.PDOutputIntent;
22: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.image.CCITTFactory;
23: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.image.JPEGFactory;
24: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.image.LosslessFactory;
25: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.image.PDImageXObject;
26: import org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.graphics.state.RenderingMode;
27: 
28: import com.trulyfreeocr.model.PageResult;
29: import com.trulyfreeocr.model.TextBlock;
30: import com.trulyfreeocr.util.Settings;
31: 
32: /**
33:  * Re-assembles a searchable PDF from per-page inputs:
34:  *   - Source PDF (copied for page dimensions / media boxes)
35:  *   - Cleaned background images
36:  *   - Binary foreground masks (CCITT G4 compressed stencil overlay)
37:  *   - OCR results (invisible text layer)
38:  *
39:  * MRC-like layout (per page):
40:  *   1. Background layer: cleaned page image (Lossless PNG for now).
41:  *   2. Foreground mask: CCITT G4 compressed binary mask, drawn as a
42:  *      PDF ImageMask stencil in black — reveals only the text pixels
43:  *      on top of the background.
44:  *   3. Text layer: invisible OCR text (RenderingMode.NEITHER = Tr 3),
45:  *      selectable and searchable but not visible.
46:  *
47:  * Rendering mode NEITHER makes text invisible on screen/print while
48:  * keeping it selectable and searchable.
49:  *
50:  * Coordinate transformation:
51:  *   Page images at 300 DPI → PDF user space at 72 DPI.
52:  *   scaleX = pageWidth_pts  / imageWidth_px
53:  *   scaleY = pageHeight_pts / imageHeight_px
54:  *   Y is flipped: image origin is top-left, PDF origin is bottom-left.
55:  */
56: public class PDFAssembler {
57: 
58:     private final PDType1Font font;
59:     private final float minFontSize;
60:     private final MetadataPreserver preserver = new MetadataPreserver();
61:     private File pdfaFontFile;
62: 
63:     public PDFAssembler() {
64:         this("HELVETICA", 1f);
65:     }
66: 
67:     public PDFAssembler(String fontName, float minFontSize) {
68:         Standard14Fonts.FontName resolved;
69:         try {
70:             resolved = Standard14Fonts.FontName.valueOf(fontName);
71:         } catch (IllegalArgumentException e) {
72:             resolved = Standard14Fonts.FontName.HELVETICA;
73:         }
74:         this.font = new PDType1Font(resolved);
75:         this.minFontSize = minFontSize;
76:     }
77: 
78:     public void setPdfaFont(File fontFile) {
79:         this.pdfaFontFile = fontFile;
80:     }
81: 
82:     /**
83:      * Builds a searchable PDF with MRC-like foreground mask overlay.
84:      *
85:      * When foregroundMasks is provided, each mask is CCITT G4 compressed
86:      * and drawn as a PDF ImageMask stencil in black on top of the background.
87:      * This reproduces the original text pixels at full sharpness, while
88:      * the background layer carries the cleaned (de-speckled) page image.
89:      *
90:      * @param sourcePdf       Original PDF (for per-page media boxes).
91:      * @param backgrounds     Per-page cleaned background images.
92:      * @param foregroundMasks Per-page binary foreground masks (TYPE_BYTE_BINARY,
93:      *                        black=text, white=background), or null to skip.
94:      * @param ocrResults      Per-page OCR results.
95:      * @param usePdfa         Enable PDF/A-2b output with XMP metadata.
96:      * @return A new PDDocument with foreground mask overlay and searchable text.
97:      */
98:     public PDDocument assemble(File sourcePdf,
99:                                List<BufferedImage> backgrounds,
100:                                List<BufferedImage> foregroundMasks,
101:                                List<PageResult> ocrResults,
102:                                boolean usePdfa) throws IOException {
103:         PDDocument output = new PDDocument();
104:         List<PDPage> outPages = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
105: 
106:         try (PDDocument source = Loader.loadPDF(sourcePdf)) {
107:             int pageCount = source.getNumberOfPages();
108:             for (int i = 0; i < pageCount; i++) {
109:                 PDPage sourcePage = source.getPage(i);
110:                 PDRectangle mediaBox = sourcePage.getMediaBox();
111: 
112:                 BufferedImage bgImage = backgrounds.get(i);
113:                 PageResult ocr = ocrResults.get(i);
114: 
115:                 PDPage outPage = new PDPage(mediaBox);
116:                 output.addPage(outPage);
117:                 outPages.add(outPage);
118: 
119:                 try (PDPageContentStream cs = new PDPageContentStream(output, outPage)) {
120:                     float pageW = mediaBox.getWidth();
121:                     float pageH = mediaBox.getHeight();
122: 
123:                     // Layer 1: background image
124:                     PDImageXObject bgXObject;
125:                     if (foregroundMasks == null) {
126:                         bgXObject = JPEGFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage, 0.85f);
127:                     } else {
128:                         bgXObject = LosslessFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage);
129:                     }
130:                     cs.drawImage(bgXObject, 0, 0, pageW, pageH);
131: 
132:                     // Layer 2: foreground mask as CCITT G4 stencil overlay
133:                     if (foregroundMasks != null && i < foregroundMasks.size()) {
134:                         BufferedImage fgMask = foregroundMasks.get(i);
135:                         BufferedImage inverted = invertBinaryImage(fgMask);
136:                         PDImageXObject fgCCITT = CCITTFactory.createFromImage(output, inverted);
137:                         fgCCITT.getCOSObject().setBoolean(COSName.IMAGE_MASK, true);
138:                         fgCCITT.getCOSObject().removeItem(COSName.COLORSPACE);
139:                         cs.setNonStrokingColor(0f, 0f, 0f);
140:                         cs.drawImage(fgCCITT, 0, 0, pageW, pageH);
141:                     }
142: 
143:                     // Layer 3: invisible OCR text
144:                     float scaleX = pageW / ocr.getWidth();
145:                     float scaleY = pageH / ocr.getHeight();
146:                     PDFont pageFont = (usePdfa && pdfaFontFile != null && pdfaFontFile.exists())
147:                             ? PDType0Font.load(output, pdfaFontFile)
148:                             : font;
149:                     cs.beginText();
150:                     cs.setFont(pageFont, minFontSize);
151:                     cs.setRenderingMode(RenderingMode.NEITHER);
152: 
153:                     for (TextBlock tb : ocr.getTextBlocks()) {
154:                         float x = tb.getBbox().x * scaleX;
155:                         float y = pageH - (tb.getBbox().y + tb.getBbox().height) * scaleY;
156:                         float fontSize = Math.max(tb.getBbox().height * scaleY, minFontSize);
157:                         cs.setFont(pageFont, fontSize);
158:                         cs.newLineAtOffset(x, y);
159:                         cs.showText(tb.getWord());
160:                         cs.newLineAtOffset(-x, -y);
161:                     }
162:                     cs.endText();
163:                 }
164:             }
165: 
166:             // Copy metadata from source to output
167:             preserver.preserve(source, output, outPages);
168: 
169:             if (usePdfa) {
170:                 addPdfaMetadata(output);
171:             }
172:         }
173: 
174:         return output;
175:     }
176: 
177:     private void addPdfaMetadata(PDDocument doc) throws IOException {
178:         PDDocumentCatalog catalog = doc.getDocumentCatalog();
179: 
180:         // PDF/A-2b XMP metadata
181:         String xmp = """
182:                 <?xpacket begin="\\uFEFF" id="W5M0MpCehiHzreSzNTczkc9d"?>
183:                 <x:xmpmeta xmlns:x="adobe:ns:meta/">
184:                   <rdf:RDF xmlns:rdf="http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#">
185:                     <rdf:Description rdf:about=""
186:                       xmlns:pdfaid="http://www.aiim.org/pdfa/ns/id/">
187:                       <pdfaid:part>2</pdfaid:part>
188:                       <pdfaid:conformance>B</pdfaid:conformance>
189:                     </rdf:Description>
190:                     <rdf:Description rdf:about=""
191:                       xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/">
192:                       <dc:format>application/pdf</dc:format>
193:                     </rdf:Description>
194:                   </rdf:RDF>
195:                 </x:xmpmeta>
196:                 <?xpacket end="w"?>""".stripIndent();
197: 
198:         org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.common.PDMetadata metadata =
199:                 new org.apache.pdfbox.pdmodel.common.PDMetadata(doc);
200:         metadata.importXMPMetadata(xmp.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8));
201:         catalog.setMetadata(metadata);
202: 
203:         // sRGB output intent
204:         try (InputStream srgbStream = getClass().getResourceAsStream("/sRGB Color Space Profile.icm")) {
205:             if (srgbStream != null) {
206:                 PDOutputIntent intent = new PDOutputIntent(doc, srgbStream);
207:                 intent.setInfo("sRGB IEC61966-2.1");
208:                 intent.setOutputCondition("sRGB IEC61966-2.1");
209:                 intent.setOutputConditionIdentifier("sRGB IEC61966-2.1");
210:                 intent.setRegistryName("http://www.color.org");
211:                 catalog.addOutputIntent(intent);
212:             }
213:         }
214:     }
215: 
216:     private static BufferedImage invertBinaryImage(BufferedImage mask) {
217:         int w = mask.getWidth();
218:         int h = mask.getHeight();
219:         BufferedImage inverted = new BufferedImage(w, h, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY);
220:         for (int y = 0; y < h; y++) {
221:             for (int x = 0; x < w; x++) {
222:                 int rgb = mask.getRGB(x, y);
223:                 inverted.setRGB(x, y, (rgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0 ? 0xFFFFFFFF : 0xFF000000);
224:             }
225:         }
226:         return inverted;
227:     }
228: 
229: }

(End of file - total 229 lines)
</content>
```

But let me think about what could cause it. Let me check the `invertBinaryImage` method:

```java
private static BufferedImage invertBinaryImage(BufferedImage mask) {
    int w = mask.getWidth();
    int h = mask.getHeight();
    BufferedImage inverted = new BufferedImage(w, h, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY);
    for (int y = 0; y < h; y++) {
        for (int x = 0; x < w; x++) {
            int rgb = mask.getRGB(x, y);
            inverted.setRGB(x, y, (rgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0 ? 0xFFFFFFFF : 0xFF000000);
        }
    }
    return inverted;
}
```

The mask from ImageSegmenter has: black=0xFF000000 (foreground/text), white=0xFFFFFFFF (background).

After inversion:
- black (foreground/text) → white (0xFFFFFFFF) — becomes the stencil "on" pixels
- white (background) → black (0xFF000000) — becomes the stencil "off" pixels

Then CCITTFactory.createFromImage creates a CCITT G4 compressed image from the inverted mask.

Then ImageMask is set to true, which means "1" (white) pixels are drawn in the current color (black), and "0" (black) pixels are transparent.

So the text should appear in black on top of the background. This looks correct.

Hmm, but what if `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` has an issue with TYPE_BYTE_BINARY images? Let me check — actually, CCITT G4 compression is designed for binary (1-bit) images, and `createFromImage` should handle TYPE_BYTE_BINARY correctly.

Wait, I just noticed something. The foreground mask is drawn with `cs.setNonStrokingColor(0f, 0f, 0f)` which sets the drawing color to black. The ImageMask stencil draws the current color where mask pixels are 1. Then `cs.drawImage(fgCCITT, 0, 0, pageW, pageH)` draws the mask.

But the mask is drawn ON TOP of the background. The background was already drawn with `cs.drawImage(bgXObject, 0, 0, pageW, pageH)`. So the order is: background first, then foreground mask on top.

Now, the problem: if the foreground mask is the WRONG WAY ROUND (i.e., text=white, background=black before inversion), then after inversion, text=black and background=white. Then the ImageMask stencil would draw black where background=white, effectively covering the entire background in black. That would cause a "black background"!

```java
// Foreground = 0 (black), Background = 1 (white) in binary image
// Text is dark on light bg, so pixel <= threshold = foreground
if (gray[idx] <= threshold) {
    foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFF000000); // ARGB: black, full opacity
} else {
    foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFFFFFFFF); // ARGB: white, full opacity
}
```

So text=black, background=white. This is correct.

After `invertBinaryImage`: text→white, background→black. This is correct for ImageMask (1=white=draw, 0=black=transparent).

```json
  "command": "cd /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR\n\n# Extract first page's background image from output PDF and check if it's black\npython3 << 'PY'\nimport re\n\nwith open(\"output.pdf\", \"rb\") as f:\n    raw = f.read()\n\n# Find stream objects — look for FlateDecode streams (background images)\n# The structure is: first FlateDecode stream per page pair = background\nstreams = list(re.finditer(rb'stream\\n(.+?)\\nendstream', raw, re.DOTALL))\nprint(f\"Found {len(streams)} streams\")\n\n# Extract first stream (should be first page background)\nbg_raw = streams[0].group(1)\nprint(f\"First stream size: {len(bg_raw)} bytes\")\n\n# Try to decompress with zlib\nimport zlib\ntry:\n    bg_data = zlib.decompress(bg_raw)\n    print(f\"Decompressed size: {len(bg_data)} bytes\")\n    print(f\"Expected (2550*3299*3): {2550*3299*3} bytes\")\n    \n    # Check pixel values\n    # If all bytes are 0, the image is black\n    non_zero = sum(1 for b in bg_data if b != 0)\n    print(f\"Non-zero bytes: {non_zero} / {len(bg_data)}\")\n    \n    if non_zero == 0:\n        print(\"*** IMAGE IS ALL BLACK (all bytes = 0) ***\")\n    else:\n        # Check first few pixels\n        print(f\"First 20 bytes: {bg_data[:20]}\")\n        print(f\"Last 20 bytes: {bg_data[-20:]}\")\n        \n        # Sample some random positions\n        import random\n        for _ in range(5):\n            pos = random.randint(0, len(bg_data) - 3)\n            print(f\"  Pixel at byte {pos}: RGB=({bg_data[pos]},{bg_data[pos+1]},{bg_data[pos+2]})\")\nexcept Exception as e:\n    print(f\"Decompress error: {e}\")\n\n# Also check mask stream (second stream)\nmask_raw = streams[1].group(1)\nprint(f\"\\nMask stream size: {len(mask_raw)} bytes\")\n# Check if mask is all-1 (which would make the entire page black)\nif len(mask_raw) > 0:\n    # For CCITT, check if decompressed data is all 0xFF or all 0x00\n    print(f\"First 20 mask bytes: {mask_raw[:20].hex()}\")\nPY",
  "description": "Check if background image is black"
}
```


After creating the CCITT image and setting ImageMask=true, the COLORSPACE is removed. But `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` for a binary image might NEED the color space to be DeviceGray. When it's removed, some PDF renderers might treat the mask correctly, but others might render it incorrectly.

Actually, for ImageMask=true, the color space SHOULD be removed. The PDF spec says: "If this flag is true, the value of BitsPerComponent must be 1 and Mask and ColorSpace shall not be specified."

So the color space removal is correct per PDF spec.

But wait, `setNonStrokingColor(0f, 0f, 0f)` is setting RGB black. For an ImageMask mask, the current non-stroking color is used as the "paint" color where mask pixels are 1. This should work.

Hmm, let me think about another possibility: what if `LosslessFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage)` produces an image with an alpha channel? If the PNG has transparency and the PDF viewer interprets it as black...

Actually, LosslessFactory from PDFBox creates a PNG-like stream. It should not produce transparency for TYPE_INT_RGB images.

Wait — I just realized something. The background image `bgImage` comes from the `inpaintBackground` method. Let me check what TYPE this image has:

```java
BufferedImage result = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_INT_RGB);
```

TYPE_INT_RGB has no alpha channel (alpha is always 0xFF). LosslessFactory should handle this correctly.

Let me look at the actual output from the PDF structure analysis again:

```
/Subtype /Image
/Filter /FlateDecode
/BitsPerComponent 8
/Width 2550
/Height 3299
/ColorSpace /DeviceRGB
```

So the background image IS RGB. And the mask IS ImageMask=true. The structure looks correct.

So why is the background black? Let me think about this from the user's perspective. The user ran the tool with the new temp dir change and got a black background. Let me check if the temp dir change could have affected the background images indirectly.

The temp dir change:
1. After extraction, saves each page as grayscale PNG: `BufferedImage page = pages.get(i); if (not gray) convert to gray; ImageIO.write(gray, ...)`
2. The `pages` list is unchanged (still contains original RGB images)
3. Segmentation uses `pages.get(pageIdx)` — original RGB images
4. Assembly uses `backgrounds` (from segmentation) — these are the inpainted images

Wait — the grayscale conversion in the temp dir section converts the LOCAL `page` variable to grayscale and writes it. But this also creates a NEW `BufferedImage` object (the grayscale version). Does this somehow affect the original image in the `pages` list?

No! `BufferedImage page = pages.get(i)` gets a REFERENCE to the original image. Then `page = gray` makes the local variable POINT to the new grayscale image. The original image in the `pages` list is unaffected.

So the temp dir change cannot affect the background images. The bug must be a pre-existing issue.

```java
// Layer 1: background image
PDImageXObject bgXObject;
if (foregroundMasks == null) {
    bgXObject = JPEGFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage, 0.85f);
} else {
    bgXObject = LosslessFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage);
}
cs.drawImage(bgXObject, 0, 0, pageW, pageH);
```

`cs.drawImage(bgXObject, 0, 0, pageW, pageH)` draws the background at (0,0) filling the entire page. This should show the background image as the page.

Then the foreground mask is drawn on top:
```java
cs.setNonStrokingColor(0f, 0f, 0f);
cs.drawImage(fgCCITT, 0, 0, pageW, pageH);
```

This draws the mask at the same position. Where mask=1, black is drawn. Where mask=0, the background shows through.

Wait, the order is:
1. Draw background
2. Draw foreground mask (black stencil on top)

If the mask is ALL ONES (all white before inversion, all black after inversion), then after CCITT and ImageMask, the mask would cover the ENTIRE page in black, totally obscuring the background.

When would the mask be all ones? If the Otsu threshold is very high, everything would be classified as foreground (text), making the mask all black (before inversion).

Or if `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` fails somehow...

Actually, let me just try to fix the issue by adding a quick test or debug output. Let me create a simple test program that creates a sample PDF using PDFAssembler and see if the background is correct.

Actually no — the simplest fix is to just quickly debug by saving the in-memory background image to a file during assembly. Let me add a quick temporary debug line to save the first page's background.

Wait, I just noticed something. In the `assemble` method, when `foregroundMasks != null`, the background image is written with `LosslessFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage)`. But the `bgImage` might be in a color model that LosslessFactory doesn't handle well.

Actually, I wonder if the issue is that the inpainted background image has ALL ITS PIXELS values at 0 (black) due to a bug in the inpainting logic. Let me check the inpaintBackground method again:

```java
// Find nearest background pixel within radius
int bestR = 0, bestG = 0, bestB = 0;
int found = 0;
for (int dy = -radius; dy <= radius && found == 0; dy++) {
    for (int dx = -radius; dx <= radius && found == 0; dx++) {
        int nx = x + dx;
        int ny = y + dy;
        if (nx < 0 || nx >= width || ny < 0 || ny >= height) continue;
        int nmask = mask.getRGB(nx, ny);
        if ((nmask & 0xFFFFFF) != 0) {
            int nrgb = original.getRGB(nx, ny);
            bestR = (nrgb >> 16) & 0xFF;
            bestG = (nrgb >> 8) & 0xFF;
            bestB = nrgb & 0xFF;
            found++;
        }
    }
}
if (found > 0) {
    int rgb = 0xFF000000 | (bestR << 16) | (bestG << 8) | bestB;
    result.setRGB(x, y, rgb);
} else {
    result.setRGB(x, y, original.getRGB(x, y));
}
```

For a pixel that's NOT foreground (background pixel), the code does:
```java
result.setRGB(x, y, original.getRGB(x, y));
```

This copies the original pixel color. So background pixels should be white/cream (page color).

For a foreground pixel, it searches within a radius for a background pixel. If found, it copies that pixel's color. If not found (which would mean there's no background pixel within the radius), it falls back to `original.getRGB(x, y)`.

This logic seems correct. The inpainted background should look like the original page with text regions filled in by surrounding colors.

Unless... there's a bug in the segmentation that produces an ALL-BLACK mask. Let me check the `segment` method:

```java
int threshold = otsuThreshold(bgNormalized);
BufferedImage foregroundMask = new BufferedImage(width, height, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY);
for (int y = 0; y < height; y++) {
    for (int x = 0; x < width; x++) {
        int idx = y * width + x;
        if (gray[idx] <= threshold) {
            foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFF000000); // black = foreground
        } else {
            foregroundMask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFFFFFFFF); // white = background
        }
    }
}
```

If the Otsu threshold is 0 (all values are 0 or negative), then ALL pixels would be <= threshold, making the entire mask black. But that would only happen for an all-black input image, which is unlikely.

If the Otsu threshold is 255 (all values are >= 255), then NO pixels would be <= threshold, making the entire mask white (all background). This would result in a clean background (no text visible) but no black overlay.

Let me check the Otsu threshold calculation for this specific image... Actually, I can't check it without running the code and seeing the intermediate values.

OK, let me just try to reproduce the issue. I'll add some debug output to save intermediate images and check them.

x++)\n            for (int y = 150; y < 200; y++)\n                mask.setRGB(x, y, 0xFF000000);\n        ImageIO.write(mask, \"png\", new File(\"/tmp/test-mask-ref.png\"));\n        \n        // Create mock OCR result\n        PageResult ocr = new PageResult(1, w, h, List.of());\n        \n        // Assemble with MRC\n        PDFAssembler assembler = new PDFAssembler();\n        try (var doc = assembler.assemble(sourcePdf, List.of(bg), List.of(mask), List.of(ocr), false)) {\n            doc.save(outputPdf);\n        }\n        System.out.println(\"Output: \" + outputPdf);\n        \n        // Write a second output without MRC\n        try (var doc2 = assembler.assemble(sourcePdf, List.of(bg), null, List.of(ocr), false)) {\n            doc2.save(new File(\"/tmp/test-nomrc.pdf\"));\n        }\n        System.out.println(\"Output (no MRC): /tmp/test-nomrc.pdf\");\n    }\n}\nJAVA\n\n./jdk/bin/javac -cp build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar -d /tmp /tmp/TestAssembly.java 2>&1\necho \"Compiled: $?\"\n\n./jdk/bin/java -cp /tmp:build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar TestAssembly tests/test-files/simple-text.pdf /tmp/test-assembly.pdf 2>&1\necho \"EXIT=$?\"\n\n# Check outputs\nls -la /tmp/test-*.pdf /tmp/test-*-ref.png 2>/dev/null",

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

There it is! The MRC-rendered PDF is ALL BLACK: every sampled pixel is (0,0,0). The non-MRC PDF renders correctly (white/light background).

So the issue is in the MRC foreground mask path. The mask layer is covering the entire page in black.

But wait, the reference background image (`/tmp/test-bg-ref.png`) is correct (white background). And the mask (`/tmp/test-mask-ref.png`) has mostly white pixels (255 = background) with some black pixels (0 = foreground/text) — the correct mask.

So the issue must be in how the mask is processed in PDFAssembler. Let me look at the mask processing code again:

```java
BufferedImage fgMask = foregroundMasks.get(i);
BufferedImage inverted = invertBinaryImage(fgMask);
PDImageXObject fgCCITT = CCITTFactory.createFromImage(output, inverted);
fgCCITT.getCOSObject().setBoolean(COSName.IMAGE_MASK, true);
fgCCITT.getCOSObject().removeItem(COSName.COLORSPACE);
cs.setNonStrokingColor(0f, 0f, 0f);
cs.drawImage(fgCCITT, 0, 0, pageW, pageH);
```

Wait — the mask is DRAWN AT THE FULL PAGE SIZE: `pageW x pageH`. The media box for the source page might be 612x792 points (letter size at 72 DPI). But the mask is 2550x3300 pixels.

`cs.drawImage(fgCCITT, 0, 0, pageW, pageH)` scales the mask to fill the page. If the mask coordinates are wrong (e.g., 1 pixel in the mask covers a large area of the page), the mask would appear incorrectly.

But the mask is binary (1-bit). CCITT G4 compressed. ImageMask=true. It should act as a stencil. The scaling should work correctly.

Actually, I think I found the issue! Let me look at the `invertBinaryImage` method again:

```java
private static BufferedImage invertBinaryImage(BufferedImage mask) {
    int w = mask.getWidth();
    int h = mask.getHeight();
    BufferedImage inverted = new BufferedImage(w, h, BufferedImage.TYPE_BYTE_BINARY);
    for (int y = 0; y < h; y++) {
        for (int x = 0; x < w; x++) {
            int rgb = mask.getRGB(x, y);
            inverted.setRGB(x, y, (rgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0 ? 0xFFFFFFFF : 0xFF000000);
        }
    }
    return inverted;
}
```

The mask from segmentation has: text=0xFF000000 (black), background=0xFFFFFFFF (white).

After inversion:
- `(rgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0` → true for 0xFF000000 (text), so set to 0xFFFFFFFF (white)
- `(rgb & 0xFFFFFF) == 0` → false for 0xFFFFFFFF (background), so set to 0xFF000000 (black)

So inverted has: text=white, background=black.

Then `CCITTFactory.createFromImage(output, inverted)` creates CCITT G4 from the inverted image.

Then: `fgCCITT.getCOSObject().setBoolean(COSName.IMAGE_MASK, true)` — makes it an ImageMask.

For ImageMask: pixel=1 (white) → draws current color, pixel=0 (black) → transparent.

So:
- Text pixels: white → draw current color (black) → text appears in black ✓
- Background pixels: black → transparent → background shows through ✓

This should be correct! Let me check if the issue is instead in `CCITTFactory.createFromImage`. Maybe it produces image data where all pixels are treated as 1.

Actually, let me check if the inverted mask is correct. Let me write the mask after inversion to a file for inspection.

But wait — I can't easily modify the test. Let me instead add more debug to the assembly code to write the inverted mask:

</details>

**Found it!** The MRC output is all-black, non-MRC renders correctly. The mask layer is covering everything. Let me check the inverted mask:

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I can see the issue clearly!

1. **Background image**: Correct — white (255,255,255) at all sampled points.

2. **Foreground mask**: Has 15 black pixels and 843 white pixels sampled → most of the page is white (background), only some black pixels (text/foreground). This is correct!

3. **Inverted mask**: Has 843 black pixels and 15 white pixels sampled → after inversion, MOST pixels are BLACK. The background pixels (white before) became black, and text pixels (black before) became white.

4. **Rendered output**: ALL sampled pixels are (0,0,0) — completely black!

So the issue is: after inversion, the mask has:
- Background → black (transparent in ImageMask)
- Text → white (drawn as black in ImageMask)

This is CORRECT logic. Text should draw as black, and background should be transparent.

But the rendered output is ALL BLACK. This means the ImageMask is covering the ENTIRE page.

Wait — let me re-check the inverted mask proportions. The mask before inversion: 15 black : 843 white (mostly white = background). The mask after inversion: 843 black : 15 white (mostly black = transparent). So ONLY 15 out of 858 samples are text (white → draws black). 

But the rendered output shows EVERY pixel is black (0,0,0). So the mask is somehow drawing black EVERYWHERE, not just on text pixels.

This suggests that `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` is not properly preserving the binary nature of the image, or the ImageMask is set up incorrectly.

OR — maybe the issue is that `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` creates a CCITT image that has pixels inverted from the input. The factory might treat black=1 and white=0, while the PDF ImageMask treats 1=draw and 0=transparent.

Let me check: `CCITTFactory.createFromImage(output, inverted)`. The inverted image has text=white, background=black. After CCITT compression, the data might be stored such that black=1 and white=0 (reverse of binary). 

In PDF's CCITT encoding:
- 1 = white
- 0 = black

So if the image has white=text and black=background, CCITT encodes white as 1 and black as 0.

For ImageMask:
- 1 (white) = draw current color
- 0 (black) = transparent

1 → white → draw → should draw text
0 → black → transparent → should show background

So this should work. Unless CCITTFactory encodes things differently.

Actually, let me try something different. Instead of removing the COLORSPACE, let me try setting Decode array on the mask. Or let me try a different approach: DON'T invert the mask, and use white as the drawing color instead of black.

Wait, actually I think the issue might be that `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` doesn't produce correct binary data for this inverted image. Let me check by using `LosslessFactory` instead for the mask.

But that would produce a much larger PDF...

Actually, I think the issue is more subtle. The `CCITTFactory.createFromImage` might be fine, but the `removeItem(COSName.COLORSPACE)` is failing because the color space item has a different name. Let me check the actual PDF structure.

Let me check the raw PDF stream for the mask:

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now I can see the mask format. The CCITT image has:
- /Filter /CCITTFaxDecode
- /BitsPerComponent 1
- /ImageMask true
- Width 2550, Height 3299
- DecodeParms reference

I notice there's NO `/ColorSpace` (good, that was removed by the code). But I also notice there's NO `/Decode` array.

For ImageMask in PDF, the default Decode is [0, 1] which means:
- 0 = white (or "off") = transparent
- 1 = black (or "on") = draw current color

Wait, that's not right. Let me check the PDF specification.

In PDF, for an image:
- Decode [0, 1] is default
- For ImageMask, the decode array determines which values are considered "on" (draw) vs "off" (transparent)
- Decode[0] is the minimum value that maps to 0, Decode[1] maps to 1
- The default Decode for 1-bit is [0, 1]
- But the COLOR is encoded as: 0 = white, 1 = black (for CCDecode, K=0 is white, K=1 is black)

Actually, for CCITT Group 4 (CCITTFaxDecode), the data is encoded with:
- 0 = white
- 1 = black

This is standard TIFF convention for CCITT.

For ImageMask:
- pixel value 0 (after decode) = transparent
- pixel value 1 (after decode) = draw with current color

With default Decode [0, 1], the pixel values map as:
- CCITT 0 (white) → ImageMask 0 → transparent
- CCITT 1 (black) → ImageMask 1 → draw black

So in the ImageMask, we want:
- Background pixels → CCITT 0 → transparent
- Text pixels → CCITT 1 → draw black

Currently, in the inverted mask:
- Background → black (inverted: 0xFF000000)
- Text → white (inverted: 0xFFFFFFFF)

After CCITTFactory:
- black pixels → CCITT 1 (black is K=1)
- white pixels → CCITT 0 (white is K=0)

With default Decode [0, 1]:
- CCITT 0 → ImageMask 0 → transparent → white pixels (text) → TRANSPARENT?? That's wrong!
- CCITT 1 → ImageMask 1 → draw black → black pixels (background) → DRAWS BLACK?? That's also wrong!

Wait, that means the logic is INVERTED from what we expect!

In standard CCITT encoding:
- 0 = white
- 1 = black

So CCITT 0 (white pixels) → ImageMask 0 → transparent
   CCITT 1 (black pixels) → ImageMask 1 → draw current color

But we WANT:
- Background → transparent
- Text → draw black

Our inverted mask has: text=white, background=black.
After CCITT: white pixels → CCITT 0 → ImageMask 0 → transparent → TEXT IS TRANSPARENT!
After CCITT: black pixels → CCITT 1 → ImageMask 1 → draw black → BACKGROUND IS DRAWN BLACK!

This is the OPPOSITE of what we want!

So the fix is: DON'T invert the mask. Pass the original mask (text=black, background=white) directly to CCITTFactory.

With original mask:
- Text=black → CCITT 1 → ImageMask 1 → draw black ✓
- Background=white → CCITT 0 → ImageMask 0 → transparent ✓

Wait, but that's also wrong for text because in the original mask:
- Text pixels → black (0xFF000000) → the red, green, blue components are 0
- Background → white (0xFFFFFFFF) → the red, green, blue components are 255

For CCITTFactory, it needs to know which pixels are 0 and which are 1. If the image is TYPE_BYTE_BINARY, CCITTFactory should extract the correct bit values.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The TYPE_BYTE_BINARY BufferedImage uses a palette where index 0 = black (0xFF000000) and index 1 = white (0xFFFFFFFF). When CCITTFactory processes this image:
- It gets the pixel data from the raster
- Index 0 (black) → stored as CCITT 1 (black)
- Index 1 (white) → stored as CCITT 0 (white)

So for the ORIGINAL mask (text=black=index 0, background=white=index 1):
- Text pixels → CCITT 1 → should be ImageMask 1 → draw black
- Background → CCITT 0 → should be ImageMask 0 → transparent

This is what we WANT! But the current code inverts the mask first, which makes text=white=index 1 and background=black=index 0, resulting in the wrong behavior.

So the bug is in the `invertBinaryImage` call! We should NOT invert the mask.

Let me remove the inversion and use the original mask directly.

But wait, the current code also removes the COLORSPACE. Does CCITTFactory set the color space correctly for a 1-bit image?

Actually, for ImageMask=true, the PDF spec says ColorSpace must NOT be specified. So removing it is correct.

Let me just remove the inversion call and test.

</details>

Found the bug! The `invertBinaryImage` is wrong. CCITT encodes 0=white, 1=black, and ImageMask maps 1=draw, 0=transparent. The original mask (text=black=1) should go directly to CCITTFactory. Inverting it swaps everything: text becomes 0 (transparent), background becomes 1 (draws black over everything).

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

Now the output is correct! The background is white (255,255,255 at corners) and there are non-black pixels (207 from sampling) which represent the rendered text.

The bug was in `invertBinaryImage()`. The original mask has:
- Text = black (0xFF000000) 
- Background = white (0xFFFFFFFF)

CCITT encoding maps: 0=white, 1=black
ImageMask maps: 1=draw, 0=transparent

So the original mask (text=black=CCITT 1, background=white=CCITT 0) maps correctly:
- Text → CCITT 1 → ImageMask 1 → draws black ✓
- Background → CCITT 0 → ImageMask 0 → transparent ✓

The inversion was WRONG because it swapped them:
- Text (black) → inverted to white → CCITT 0 → ImageMask 0 → transparent (text disappears!)
- Background (white) → inverted to black → CCITT 1 → ImageMask 1 → draws black over everything (black background!)

Now let me also test with the original Sherlock Holmes file:

</details>

**Fixed!** The bug was `invertBinaryImage()` — the original mask (text=black, background=white) maps correctly to CCITT/ImageMask without inversion. The inversion flipped everything: background became "draw black" and text became "transparent."

Let me now test with the original 10-page file:

```
	at com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.call(TrulyFreeOCR.java:31)
	at picocli.CommandLine.executeUserObject(CommandLine.java:2045)
	at picocli.CommandLine.access$1500(CommandLine.java:148)
	at picocli.CommandLine$RunLast.executeUserObjectOfLastSubcommandWithSameParent(CommandLine.java:2465)
	at picocli.CommandLine$RunLast.handle(CommandLine.java:2457)
	at picocli.CommandLine$RunLast.handle(CommandLine.java:2419)
	at picocli.CommandLine$AbstractParseResultHandler.execute(CommandLine.java:2277)
	at picocli.CommandLine$RunLast.execute(CommandLine.java:2421)
	at picocli.CommandLine.execute(CommandLine.java:2174)
	at com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.main(TrulyFreeOCR.java:286)
Caused by: java.io.IOException: Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds
	at com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline.OCREngine.ocr(OCREngine.java:81)
	at com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.lambda$runPipeline$1(TrulyFreeOCR.java:235)
	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.FutureTask.run(FutureTask.java:317)
	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor.runWorker(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:1144)
	at java.base/java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor$Worker.run(ThreadPoolExecutor.java:642)
	at java.base/java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:1583)
EXIT=1

=== Render first page ===
I/O Error: Couldn't open file '/tmp/sherlock-fixed.pdf': No such file or directory.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 3, in <module>
  File "/home/bgu/anaconda3/lib/python3.12/site-packages/PIL/Image.py", line 3277, in open
    fp = builtins.open(filename, "rb")
         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/tmp/sherlock-render-1.png'

```

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The OCR timed out at 120 seconds for page 1 (took 106.2 seconds!). It's the same system load issue — Tesseract is very slow. On the simple-text PDF it took 26.5 seconds, but on the Sherlock Holmes page it took 106.2 seconds (which is over the 120-second timeout but not by much — it was close).

The important thing is that page 1's OCR DID complete in 106.2 seconds, but the 120-second FUTURE timeout was already hit. Wait, actually the error says "Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds" which is the SUBPROCESS timeout. But page 1 took 106.2 seconds, which is under 120 seconds. So why did it time out?

Looking more carefully at the output:
```
    OCR page   1: 106.2s
Error: OCR processing failed
```

Wait — page 1 completed with 106.2 seconds! That's under the 120-second timeout. But then the error says "Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds."

Hmm, this is confusing. The page 1 OCR completed, but then the pipeline errored. Let me look at the stack trace:
```
Caused by: java.io.IOException: Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds
	at com.trulyfreeocr.pipeline.OCREngine.ocr(OCREngine.java:81)
	at com.trulyfreeocr.TrulyFreeOCR.lambda$runPipeline$1(TrulyFreeOCR.java:235)
```

Wait, this is from a DIFFERENT page's OCR. The first page completed (106.2s), but maybe the SECOND page's OCR (running concurrently with the first? No, `--threads 1` means single-threaded) started after page 1 and timed out.

But wait, with `--threads 1`, `ocrMaxThreads = 1` (since `threads != null`, `ocrMax = segThreads = 1`). So OCR is sequential. Page 1 took 106.2s. Then page 2 starts. If page 2 takes >120s, it would time out.

But the error message shows:
```
Caused by: java.io.IOException: Tesseract timed out after 120 seconds
```

This could be page 2 timing out. Since the total time is 106.2s (page 1) + time elapsed on page 2 + assembly time. The 300-second timeout killed the process before we could see page 2's timing.

Anyway, the important fix is the `invertBinaryImage` bug. The MRC output now renders correctly (as shown with the simple-text PDF test). The Sherlock Holmes test failed only because of Tesseract slowness, not because of the mask bug.

Let me clean up and summarize.

</details>

The mask fix works — `simple-text.pdf` renders correctly with white background. The Sherlock test timed out purely due to Tesseract slowness (page 1 took 106s), not a code issue.

**Root cause:** `invertBinaryImage()` was unnecessary and incorrect. Original mask: text=black, background=white. CCITT encodes 0=white, 1=black. ImageMask maps 1=draw, 0=transparent. So text→CCITT 1→draw black ✓, background→CCITT 0→transparent ✓. The inversion swapped them, making background draw black over everything.

Let me clean up the debug files and remove the temp dir from this run:

### Tool: bash

**Input:**

```json
{
  "command": "cd /home/bgu/GitHub/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR\nrm -rf /tmp/debug-bg-*.png /tmp/debug-mask-*.png /tmp/debug-inv-*.png /tmp/render-*.png /tmp/test-*.pdf /tmp/TestAssembly.* /tmp/render-*.png\nrm -rf tfocr-*/ 2>/dev/null; ls -d tfocr-* 2>/dev/null || echo \"no tfocr-* leftovers\"",
  "description": "Clean up temp files"
}
```

**Output:**

```
no tfocr-* leftovers

```

All clean. The black background was caused by the `invertBinaryImage()` call in `PDFAssembler.java` — it reversed the mask so background pixels drew black and text pixels became invisible. Fixed by passing the original mask directly to `CCITTFactory.createFromImage`.

Ended: 2026-06-28T06:56:50.743Z
## User

How to run OCR without MRC compreission?

<details>
<summary>Thinking</summary>

The user is asking how to run OCR without MRC compression. Looking at the code, there's already a `--no-mrc` flag in TrulyFreeOCR.java:

```java
@Option(names = {"--no-mrc"}, description = "Disable MRC compression (output original images + text layer only)")
private boolean noMrc;
```

And it's used at line 100:
```java
boolean useMrc = !noMrc && settings.getBoolean("pipeline.mrc.enabled", true);
```

So the user can run with `--no-mrc` to skip MRC compression. The pipeline will then use the original pages as backgrounds (line 198-200):

```java
System.out.println("  MRC disabled — using original pages as backgrounds");
backgrounds = new ArrayList<>(pages);
foregroundMasks = null;
```

And in PDFAssembler, when `foregroundMasks == null`, it writes the background as JPEG:
```java
if (foregroundMasks == null) {
    bgXObject = JPEGFactory.createFromImage(output, bgImage, 0.85f);
}
```

I'm in plan mode, so I should just answer the question simply.

</details>

`--no-mrc` flag:

```bash
./jdk/bin/java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar --no-mrc input.pdf
```

This skips segmentation entirely — uses original page images as the background layer with JPEG compression and adds the invisible OCR text on top. No foreground mask (CCITT G4 stencil) is applied.

      "url": "https://www.dynamsoft.com/blog/imaging/mrc-compression-pdf",
      "title": "MRC Compression for PDF Files | Dynamsoft Blog",
      "publish_date": "2022-11-21",
      "excerpts": [
        "The MRC 3-layer model was also implemented in other digital document formats, including .tfx (TIFF-FX), .ldx (LuraDocument), and .djvu (DjVu). [[2]]()\n\n## Benefits of MRC Compression\n\nMRC compression was developed initially to compress scanned color pages/images for fax transmission. [[2]]() Nowadays, the method is used on images from document scans and snapshots from cameras as well.\n\nThe major benefit of Mixed Raster Content compression is obvious - a smaller size. This reduces the bandwidth in transit and thus speeds up file transmission. Also, a smaller file size leads to decreased storage space in the database.\n\nThe MRC method comes with some beneficial side effects too:\n\n* Sharper text. With the text layer separated from the foreground and the background, it’s possible to sharpen the text to make it easier to read.\n* Cleaned-up background. The practice of 3-layer segmentation helps with cleaning up the background as well.\nTexture and shadows on the background could be distracting to readers. Removing the shadows or softening the texture can improve the reading experience.\n\n## MRC Improves the Accuracy of OCR\n\nBased on the discussion above, we can understand that Mixed Raster Content compression results in clean and isolated text blocks. This improves the accuracy of the downstream OCR. With OCR turning the image PDF into a searchable PDF, employee efficiencies are further improved.\n\n## Support for MRC\n\n[Dynamic Web TWAIN](https://www.dynamsoft.com/web-twain/overview/) will introduce the MRC compression feature soon. Stay tuned.\n\n#### References\n\n[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mixed\\_raster\\_content  \n[2] http://www.djvu-soft.narod.ru/planetdjvu/the\\_mrc\\_mixed\\_raster\\_content\\_model\\_and\\_djvu.htm\n\n*  PDF\n\n[Privacy Statement](https://www.dynamsoft.com/privacy-statement/) / [Site Map](https://www.dynamsoft.com/sitemap/) / [Home](https://www.dynamsoft.com/) / [Purchase](https://www.dynamsoft.\ncom/purchase-center/) / [Support](https://www.dynamsoft.com/company/customer-service/)"

        "## Navigation Menu\n\nToggle navigation\n\nAppearance settings\n\nSearch or jump to...\n\n# Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests...\n\nAppearance settings\n\nResetting focus\n\nsahithottikunta / **pdf\\_ocr\\_extraction** Public\n\n* Notifications You must be signed in to change notification settings\n* Fork 0\n* Star 0\n\n# sahithottikunta/pdf\\_ocr\\_extraction\n\nmain\n\nBranches Tags\n\n \n\nGo to file\n\nCode\n\nOpen more actions menu\n\n## Folders and files\n\n|Name |Name |Last commit message |Last commit date |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n|## Latest commit\n\n## History\n\n[4 Commits](https://github.com/sahithottikunta/pdf_ocr_extraction/commits/main/)\n\n 4 Commits |\n|.gitignore |.gitignore | | |\n|Analyze\\_a\\_scanned\\_PDF.ipynb |Analyze\\_a\\_scanned\\_PDF.ipynb | | |\n|LICENSE |LICENSE | | |\n|README.md |README.md | | |\n|requirements.txt |requirements.txt | | |\n|View all files |\n\n## Repository files navigation\n\n* README\n* MIT license\n\n# PDF OCR and Structured Data Extraction\nThis project is a Python pipeline that uses Optical Character Recognition (OCR) to extract text and structured data from scanned PDF documents. It processes each page, cleans the recognized text, identifies key information based on keywords, and exports the findings into a structured JSON file.\n\nThis notebook is ideal for batch processing scanned documents, such as mortgage deeds or invoices, to pull out key fields.\n\n_( **Note:** To add an image here, take a screenshot of one of the matplotlib outputs from your notebook, upload it to your GitHub repository, and change the line above to `!OCR Bounding Box Visualization` )_\n\n## Features\n\n* **PDF to Image Conversion:** Uses `PyMuPDF (fitz)` to render each PDF page as a high-resolution image.\n* **Image Preprocessing:** Applies `OpenCV` filters (grayscale, binary threshold, and median blur) to improve OCR accuracy.\n* **OCR Engine:** Leverages `Tesseract` via the `pytesseract` wrapper to extract text and its bounding box coordinates from the preprocessed images.\n* **Text Cleaning:** A custom `clean_text_overall` function uses regular expressions ( `re` ) to fix common OCR errors (e.g., `1s` \\-> `is` , `Oate` \\-> `Date` ) and normalize data.\n* **Keyword Filtering:** Intelligently filters the raw OCR output to keep only text that contains relevant keywords (like \"borrower\", \"loan\", \"date\") or digits, reducing noise.\n* **Data Visualization:** Uses `Matplotlib` to display the original PDF pages with green bounding boxes and red text labels drawn over the detected words.\n* **Structured Export:** Parses the cleaned text to find key fields (like \"Lender\", \"Loan Amount\") and exports them, along with their bounding box, into a clean `mortgage_structured.json` file.\n\n## Technologies Used\n\n* **Python 3**\n* **Tesseract OCR Engine:** The core system-level OCR engine.\n* **Python Libraries:**\n\n...\n\ncom/)[your-username]/[your-repository-name].git\n cd [your-repository-name]\n```\n\n**3\\. Set up a Python Environment and Install Dependencies**\n\nIt is highly recommended to use a virtual environment.\n\n```\n#  Create a virtual environment \npython3 -m venv venv\n\n #  Activate it \n source venv/bin/activate  #  On Unix/macOS \n. \\v env \\S cripts \\a ctivate    #  On Windows \n\n #  Create a 'requirements.txt' file with the content below \n #  and then run the install command \npip install -r requirements.txt\n```\n\n**`requirements.txt` file content:**\n\n```\npymupdf\npytesseract\nopencv-python\npillow\nnumpy\nmatplotlib\n```\n\n## Usage\n\n1. **Add a PDF:** Place a sample scanned PDF (like the `MTG_10009588.pdf` you used) into the repository's root folder, or in a `data/` subfolder.\n2. **Update Path:** Change the `pdf_path` variable in the notebook to match your file's name (e.g., `pdf_path = \"MTG_10009588.pdf\"` ).\n3. **Run the Notebook:** Open and run the `Analyze_a_scanned_PDF.ipynb` notebook.\n4."

GitHub PDF to Word OCR Overview\n  \n    1. Technical Principles\n    2. Pros and Cons\n    3. Comparison with Professional PDF OCR Tools\n* Part 2. Quickly Set up PDF to Word OCR on GitHub\n* Part 3. Bonus: PDF to Word OCR Professional Tool\n* Final Words\n\n[Home](https://updf.com \"Home\") » [OCR](https://updf.com/ocr/ \"OCR\") » GitHub - PDF to Word OCR: The Simple Playbook for Beginners\n\nNeed to batch process scanned PDFs into Word?\nWorried about the cost of Adobe Acrobat or privacy risks of online tools? Open-source GitHub projects offer a free alternative, so this article we will delve deeper into whether this method is truly suitable for us, as well as its advantages, disadvantages, and detailed operating steps. And if you need a lightweight, easy-to-use, and secure professional OCR tool, this article helps you choose the right OCR approach.\n\n## Part 1. GitHub PDF to Word OCR Overview\n\n**GitHub PDF to Word OCR** tools scan pages and recognize text to create editable Word documents from scans. They are flexible and private but harder to install, less accurate, and weaker at keeping complex layouts. Most options work through command-line steps and scripts to help users get comfortable with technical workflows. Now, we will explore how these tools work, where they help, and where they still struggle.\n\n### Technical Principles\n\nThese tools turn each PDF page into images and then recognize the text inside them.\nLet’s break their working process into a few simple technical steps.\n\n1. **Page Image Conversion:** At the start, the program changes every PDF page into a clear image. This gives the OCR engine clean visual data, ready for further analysis.\n2. **Text Detection:** Next, the engine scans each image to find paragraphs, individual lines, and possible text blocks. Non-text elements like photos and graphics are filtered out to keep focus on writing.\n3. **Character Recognition:** During this stage, the model studies letter and number shapes, then maps them to real characters. Learning language patterns helps reduce mistakes and make entire words and phrases more accurate.\n4. **Export to Document:** In the final step, recognized text is saved into formats that Word and other editors understand.         "Effortlessly extract tables, charts, and key figures from dense financial reports, invoices, and bank statements. The high accuracy and layout-aware parsing reduce manual data entry, minimize errors, and accelerate financial workflows and analysis.\n\nFinancial Report Analysis with DeepSeek-OCR\n\nLLM Data Pipelines\n\n## Powering Next-Gen AI with Quality Data\n\nThe ultimate data source for LLMs.\n\nGenerate vast, high-quality datasets for training Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs). DeepSeek-OCR's efficiency makes it economically viable to process millions of documents, creating diverse and rich training corpora.\n\nDiverse Document Processing for LLMs\n\nDigital Archiving\n\n## Creating Efficient Digital Libraries\n\nArchive, search, and manage documents.\n\nTransform physical documents into a lightweight, searchable digital archive.\nThe optical compression ensures that storage requirements are minimized while the high-quality text extraction makes every document instantly discoverable.\n\nDigital Archiving with DeepSeek-OCR\n\n## A Breakthrough in Efficiency and Accuracy\n\nDeepSeek-OCR isn't just another text recognition tool. It's a fundamental rethinking of how machines process visual information, built to be both powerful and lightweight.\n\n📦\n\n### Contexts Optical Compression\n\nThe core innovation. We represent high-resolution documents with a fraction of the vision tokens, dramatically reducing computational costs and increasing processing speed.\n\n🎯\n\n### State-of-the-Art Accuracy\n\nAchieve up to 97% OCR precision on complex benchmarks. DeepSeek-OCR surpasses leading models while using significantly fewer resources.\n\n📄\n\n### Versatile Document Parsing\n\nGo beyond plain text. Intelligently extract data from complex layouts, including academic papers, financial charts, chemical formulas, and geometric figures.\n\n🚀\n### Built for Massive Scale\n\nEngineered for real-world deployment, capable of processing over 200,000 pages per day on a single GPU, making it ideal for large-scale data annotation and LLM training.\n\n🌍\n\n### Global Language Recognition\n\nTrained on a vast dataset covering nearly 100 languages, allowing for accurate text extraction from international documents without changing models.\n\n🧠\n\n### Low-Memory Architecture\n\nThe novel DeepEncoder design processes high-resolution images while maintaining low activation memory, enabling deployment on less powerful hardware and reducing operational costs.\n\n## Frequently Asked Questions\n\nWhat is DeepSeek-OCR? ▼\n\nDeepSeek-OCR is a new vision-language model that specializes in recognizing and extracting text and data from documents using an innovative technique called 'Contexts Optical Compression'. What makes 'Contexts Optical Compression' different? ▼\nInstead of converting every detail of an image into a large number of tokens, it intelligently compresses the visual information. This means it can represent a full, complex page with up to 10x fewer data points than traditional models, making it incredibly fast and efficient. Is DeepSeek-OCR free to use? ▼\n\nYes, the model is open-source and the live demo on this site is free to use. For commercial or large-scale use, you can access the model weights and code from the official GitHub repository. Is there an official API available? ▼\n\nCurrently, there is no official hosted API. However, the project is open-source, allowing you to deploy and host your own instance. We recommend following the GitHub project for future announcements about a public API. What file types can I analyze? ▼\n\nThe underlying model can process a wide range of image formats (PNG, JPEG, WebP) and is particularly effective on pages from PDF documents. The live demo supports direct image uploads and pastes."

      "url": "https://tesseractocr.org/",
      "title": "Tesseract OCR — The World's Best Open Source OCR Engine",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "Process 1 page or 10 million pages at zero cost.\n\n#### Offline by Default.\n\nAbsolute privacy. Your images never leave your server. Perfect for medical, legal, and financial PII.\n\n#### Leptonica Powered.\n\nBuilt-in integration with the Leptonica image processing library allows for implicit thresholding, scaling, and noise removal.\n\n#### Continuous Evolution.\n\nOriginally developed at HP in 1985, open-sourced in 2005, and significantly upgraded by Google until 2017. Currently maintained by Stefan Weil and Zdenko Podobny, honoring the legacy of Ray Smith.\n\n#### Hardware Agnostic.\n\nRuns perfectly on a Raspberry Pi, a standard web server, or high-end multicore clusters via OpenMP parallelization.\n\n#### Direction/Script Aware.\n\nAutomatically detects orientation and script type (OSD mode), adjusting its parsers internally without human intervention.\n\n## Frequently asked questions about Tesseract OCR.\n\nWhat does Tesseract OCR do in real life?\nIn the software world, Tesseract takes raw images containing text (like scanned invoices, receipts, and old books) and translates the pixels into machine-readable, searchable, and editable text. It is the engine powering thousands of data automation tools, digital archives, and mobile scanning apps worldwide. Is Tesseract OCR free to use commercially?\n\nYes! Tesseract OCR is 100% free and open-source. It is released under the permissive Apache 2.0 License, allowing you to use it for personal, academic, and commercial production projects without paying any licensing or API fees whatsoever. Is Tesseract OCR still good in 2026?\n\nAbsolutely. Since the introduction of the deep learning LSTM neural network engine in version 4 (further optimized in version 5), Tesseract remains highly competitive with expensive paid OCR APIs—especially for printed material—while offering the massive benefit of running entirely offline for absolute data privacy.\nAs of 2026, it remains the most reliable open-source solution for global document digitization. Is Tesseract OCR considered AI?\n\nYes. While older versions (v3) relied on traditional pattern-matching algorithms, modern Tesseract (v4 and v5) is entirely AI-driven. It utilizes Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) recurrent neural networks, a form of deep learning architecture explicitly designed to handle sequential data like text lines. What is the difference between Tesseract v4 and v5?\n\nTesseract 5 offers substantially better performance (speed) than version 4 while retaining the highly accurate LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) neural network models. Many memory leaks were fixed, and training tools were modernized. Tesseract 5 is the current recommended version for production deployments. Does Tesseract include a graphical user interface (GUI)?\n\nNo. Tesseract is primarily a C/C++ library and a command-line interface (CLI) program."
      ]
    },

      "url": "https://products.fileformat.com/ocr/java/tess4j/",
      "title": "Free Java OCR Library for TIFF, JPEG, GIF, PNG, & BMP Image ...",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "1. [Products](https://products.fileformat.com/)\n2. [OCR](https://products.fileformat.com/ocr/)\n3. [Java](https://products.fileformat.com/ocr/java/)\n4. Tess4J\n\n# Free Java Library for OCR Text Extraction & Document Analysis\n\n## Open Source Java OCR Library for Incorporating OCR capabilities into Java apps and allows Extracting Text from Images as well as Scanned Documents.\n\nFor software developers seeking a powerful and accessible Java OCR API, Tess4J emerges as a premier open source Java OCR library that seamlessly integrates the renowned Tesseract engine into Java applications. This robust solution is specifically designed to perform OCR operations on JPEG, BMP & PNG files, as well as a wide range of other image formats. It empowers applications to recognize text from images with high accuracy, converting static pictures into machine-readable data.\nThis capability is fundamental for automating data entry, digitizing archives, and building intelligent systems that require Java optical character recognition to process scanned documents and digital images efficiently.\n\nA key strength of this free Java OCR library is its extensive format support and versatility. Developers can leverage its straightforward API to extract text from PNG images, perform OCR operations on GIF files, and handle numerous other formats with ease. Built as a Java wrapper for Google's Tesseract engine, Tess4J provides exceptional cross-platform compatibility and supports advanced features like image preprocessing and multi-language recognition to ensure reliable text extraction from even low-quality scans. This makes it an indispensable tool for any project that requires accurate and efficient conversion of visual information into actionable textual data.\n\n* At a Glance\n* Platform Independence\n* Supported File Formats\n\n### At A Glance\nAn overview of Tess4J features.\n\nFeatures Overview\n\n* Perform OCR\n* Add OCR Capabilities\n* Recognize Image text\n* Convet images of text\n* Recognized Font text\n* Searc PDF\n* Over 100 Languages\n* Create OCR apps\n* Save to browser\n* Extract Text\n* Multi-threading Support\n\nTess4J\n\nTess4J supports popular image file formats listed below.\n\nReader\n\n* [PNG](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/png/) , [JPEG](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/jpeg/) , [BMP](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/bmp/) , [TIFF](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/tiff/) , TGA, DICOM\n\nWriter\n\n* [PNG](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/png/) , [JPEG](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/jpeg/) , [BMP](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/bmp/) , [TIFF](https://docs.fileformat.com/image/tiff/)\n\nTess4J\n\n### Platform Independence\n\nTess4J can work with any Java-based programming language\n\n* Java\n\nTess4J\n\nPrevious Next\n\n## Getting Started with Tess4J\n\nThe recommend way to install Tess4J is using Maven."

of the [supported compilers](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/supported-compilers.html) .\n\n## Running Tesseract\n\nBasic **[command line usage](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/Command-Line-Usage.html)** :\n\n```\ntesseract imagename outputbase [-l lang] [--oem ocrenginemode] [--psm pagesegmode] [configfiles...]\n```\n\nFor more information about the various command line options use `tesseract --help` or `man tesseract` .\n\nExamples can be found in the [documentation](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/Command-Line-Usage.html) .\n\n## For developers\n\nDevelopers can use `libtesseract` [C](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/blob/main/include/tesseract/capi.h) or [C++](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/blob/main/include/tesseract/baseapi.h) API to build their own application.\nIf you need bindings to `libtesseract` for other programming languages, please see the [wrapper](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/AddOns.html) section in the AddOns documentation.\n\nDocumentation of Tesseract generated from source code by doxygen can be found on [tesseract-ocr.github.io](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/) .\n\n## Support\n\nBefore you submit an issue, please review **[the guidelines for this repository](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md)** .\n\nFor support, first read the [documentation](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/) ,\nparticularly the [FAQ](https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/FAQ.html) to see if your problem is addressed there.\nIf not, search the [Tesseract user forum](https://groups.google.com/g/tesseract-ocr) , the [Tesseract developer forum](https://groups.google.com/g/tesseract-dev) and [past issues](https://github.\ncom/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/issues) , and if you still can't find what you need, ask for support in the mailing-lists.\n\nMailing-lists:\n\n* [tesseract-ocr](https://groups.google.com/g/tesseract-ocr) \\- For tesseract users.\n* [tesseract-dev](https://groups.google.com/g/tesseract-dev) \\- For tesseract developers.\n\nPlease report an       "url": "https://tesseract-ocr.github.io/tessdoc/Installation.html",
      "title": "Introduction - tessdoc",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "# Introduction\n\n## Tesseract documentation\n\n[View on GitHub](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tessdoc)\n\n# Introduction\n\nTesseract is an open source [text recognition (OCR)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_character_recognition) Engine, available under the [Apache 2.0 license.](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0) It can be used directly, or (for programmers) using an [API](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract/blob/main/include/tesseract/baseapi.h) to extract printed text from images. It supports a wide variety of languages.\n\nTesseract doesn’t have a built-in GUI, but there are several available from the 3rdParty page.\n\n# Installation\n\nThere are two parts to install, the engine itself, and the traineddata for the languages.\n\nTesseract is available directly from many Linux distributions. The package is generally called **‘tesseract’** or **‘tesseract-ocr’** \\- search your distribution’s repositories to find it.\nPackages for over 130 languages and over 35 scripts are also available directly from the Linux distributions. The language traineddata packages are called **‘tesseract-ocr-langcode’** and **‘tesseract-ocr-script-scriptcode’** , where `langcode` is three letter language code and `scriptcode` is four letter script code.\n\n**Examples:** tesseract-ocr-eng ( **English** ), tesseract-ocr-ara ( **Arabic** ), tesseract-ocr-chi-sim ( **Simplified Chinese** ), tesseract-ocr-script-latn ( **Latin Script** ), tesseract-ocr-script-deva ( **Devanagari script** ), etc.\n\n\\*\\* FOR EXPERTS ONLY. \\*\\*\n\nIf you are experimenting with OCR Engine modes, you will need to manually install language training data beyond what is available in your Linux distribution.\n\nVarious types of training data can be found on [GitHub](https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/) . Unpack and copy the .traineddata file into a ‘tessdata’ directory.\nThe exact directory will depend both on the type of training data, and your Linux distribution. Possibilities are `/usr/share/tesseract-ocr/tessdata` or `/usr/share/tessdata` or `/usr/share/tesseract-ocr/4.00/tessdata` .\n\nTraining data for obsolete Tesseract versions [=< 3.02](https://sourceforge.net/projects/tesseract-ocr-alt/files/?source=navbar) reside in another location.\n\n## Platforms\n\nIf Tesseract is not available for your distribution, or you want to use a newer version than they offer, you can compile your own .\n\n### Ubuntu\n\nYou can install Tesseract and its developer tools on Ubuntu by simply running:\n\n```\nsudo apt install tesseract-ocr\nsudo apt install libtesseract-dev\n```\n\n**Note for Ubuntu users** : In case `apt` is unable to find the package try adding `universe` entry to the `sources.list` file as shown below.\n\n```\nsudo vi /etc/apt/sources.list\n\nCopy the first line \"deb http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu bionic main\" and paste it as shown below on the next line."
      ]

      "title": "Tesseract OCR with Java with Examples - byby.dev",

      "excerpts": [
        "**Extensive Language Support** : Provides support for many languages, making it applicable for worldwide deployment.\n4. **Versatile Operating Modes** : Different selections of modes for generalized page layout analysis, lines, words, and character recognition allow for usage on the desired projects.\n5. **Multi-Page PDF and Image Processing** : Manages multifaced PDF documents and even images, supporting many tasks covering complex document processing areas.\n6. **Modular Architecture** : It Includes a modular design where the system can be customized and expanded in the capabilities of other data sets through training, which addresses a wide range of OCR applications.\n\n## Basic Example of Tesseract OCR in Java.\n\nSuch a basic example shows the mechanism of readied file image and extracting from it the text by Tesseract OCR, to be able to convert image-based text into digital format through programming.\n\n### Creating a Simple Java Program\nTo begin with, make sure Tesseract is installed in your system along with setting up of the environment scenario. What follows is a Java program that makes use of the Tesseract API to carry out OCR on an image. This case involves the Tesseract library that is available for use in your project as a Maven dependency or manually added to the library path.\n\n```\nimport net . sourceforge . tess4j . * ; import java . io . File ; public class TesseractExample { public static void main ( String [ ]  args ) { File  imageFile  = new File ( \"path/to/your/image/file.jpg\" ) ; ITesseract  instance  = new Tesseract ( ) ; // Please provide the absolute path to the Tesseract executable \n        instance . setDatapath ( \"path/to/tesseract/data\" ) ; try { String  result  =  instance . doOCR ( imageFile ) ; System . out . println ( result ) ; } catch ( TesseractException  e ) { System . err . println ( e . getMessage ( ) ) ; } } }\n```\n\n### Programming and interpreting output.\nTo run the program, launch it from your IDE of choice or the command line offered by your development environment if the environment is configured properly. The program opens the image file that is being read by the program while performing OCR using Tesseract and displays the recognized text on the console.\n\n### Note:\n\nThere may be variations in the accuracy of text extraction based on the quality of the original image and the complicity of the written text. Better results can be obtained with high-quality images, and readable text without problems of ambiguity. If the output of the OCR is not as productive as anticipated, there are a few possibilities for reworking the image supplantations to enhance its picture quality or tuning Tesseract’s design parameters for better performance .\n\n## Advanced Usage of Tesseract OCR\n\nTesseract OCR has some advanced features such as accuracy, language support, and document processing.\n\n* **Enhancing OCR Accuracy**"

PDFs generated by bin/recode\\_pdf should be PDF/A 3b and\nPDF/UA compatible.\n\nSome of the tooling also supports specific Internet Archive file formats (such\nas the \"scandata.\nxml\" files, but the tooling should work fine without those\nfiles, too.\n\nWhile the code is already being used internally to create PDFs at the Internet\nArchive, the code still needs more documentation and cleaning up, so don't\nexpect this to be super well documented just yet.\n\n## Features\n\n* Reliable: has produced over 6 million PDFs in 2021 alone (each with many\n  hundreds of pages)\n* Fast and robust compression: Competes directly with the proprietary software\n  offerings when it comes to speed and compressibility (often outperforming in\n  both)\n* MRC compression of images, leading to anywhere from 3-15x compression ratios,\n  depending on the quality setting provided.\n* Creates PDF from a directory of images\n* Improved compression based on OCR results (hOCR files)\n* Hidden text layer insertion based on hOCR files, which makes a PDF searchable\n  and the text copy-pasteable.\n* PDF/A 3b compatible.\n* Basic PDF/UA support (accessibility features)\n* Creation of 1 bit (black and white) PDFs"

        "* Supports integration into Java programs or scripts\n\n**User Experience:** PDFtk-java is optimized for command-line usage, making it fast and efficient for bulk PDF operations. While not visually rich, its reliability, automation features, and cross-platform support make it an essential open source PDF editor for developers and advanced users who need precise PDF control.\n\n### 8\\. Ghostscript\n\n[Ghostscript](https://ghostscript.com/) is a powerful, versatile best open source PDF editor and PostScript interpreter widely used for PDF processing, printing, and conversion.\n\nKnown for its high performance and small footprint, Ghostscript can handle raster and vector outputs, color management, and workflow automation. It is ideal for developers, printing professionals, and anyone looking for a robust open source PDF editor Windows solution.\n\n**Supported Platforms:** Windows, macOS, Unix/Linux, VMS, and embedded systems\n\n**Core Features:**\n\n* PostScript and PDF interpretation\n* Conversion between PDF, PCL, XPS, and raster formats\n* Advanced color management and screening\n* OCR support for converting scanned images to searchable PDFs\n* PDF compression and optimization\n* SDK integration and command-line automation\n* Overprint simulation and output device emulation\n\n**User Experience:** Ghostscript excels in high-performance PDF processing, making it suitable for enterprise-grade tasks like printing, PDF generation, and workflow automation.\n\nIts command-line interface may require technical knowledge, but it provides unmatched flexibility and speed for professionals. Ghostscript is a reliable open source PDF editor for both Windows and other platforms.\n\n## FAQs of Open-source PDF Editor\n\n### 1\\. Is there an open-source alternative to Adobe Acrobat?\n\nYes, there are several open-source alternatives to Adobe Acrobat, including LibreOffice Draw, Inkscape, and Stirling PDF. These tools let you edit, annotate, and manage PDFs without paid software.\n\n### 2\\.\nWhat is the best free open-source PDF editor?\n\nThe best free open-source PDF editor depends on your needs. LibreOffice Draw is ideal for text and image editing, Inkscape for graphics-heavy PDFs, and Stirling PDF for OCR and batch processing.\n\n### 3\\. Can I edit PDF text and images with open-source software?\n\nYes, tools like LibreOffice Draw, Inkscape, and PDFedit allow you to edit PDF text, replace images, and modify layouts directly.\n\n### 4\\. Are open-source PDF editors safe to use?\n\nMost open-source PDF editors are secure and privacy-friendly, as their source code is publicly available. Examples include LibreOffice Draw (GPLv3) and Stirling PDF.\n\n### 5\\. Can I split, merge, or rearrange PDF pages with open-source tools?\n\nYes, PDFsam Basic, PDFtk-java, and GhostScript let you merge, split, and reorder PDF pages efficiently. Some tools also support batch processing.\n\n### 6\\. Do open-source PDF editors support multiple platforms?"

      "url": "https://github.com/udiram/SpectrePDF",
      "title": "GitHub - udiram/SpectrePDF: A tool for processing and redacting PDFs based on target words using OCR · GitHub",
      "publish_date": null,
      "excerpts": [
        "**Run the Processor** : Use the `process_pdf` function from `SpectrePDF.anonymizer` .\n   \n   Here's a basic example script:\n   \n   ```\n   from spectrepdf . anonymizer import process_pdf \n    import json \n   \n    # Load targets from redaction dict keys \n    with open ( \"redaction.json\" , \"r\" ) as f :\n       redaction_dict = json . load ( f )\n    target_words = list ( redaction_dict . keys ())\n   \n    # Process the PDF (redact with replacements) \n    process_pdf (\n       input_pdf = \"input.pdf\" ,\n       output_pdf = \"output_redacted.pdf\" ,\n       target_words = target_words ,\n       redaction_json_path = \"redaction.json\" ,\n       redact_targets = True ,\n       black_redaction = False ,  # False for text replacement; True for black boxes \n       dpi = 500 ,\n       tesseract_cmd = \"/usr/local/bin/tesseract\" ,  # Adjust to your Tesseract path \n       verbosity = 1  # 0: silent, 1: progress, 2: detailed \n   )\n   ```\n   \n   This will scan `input.\npdf` for words like \"confidential\" or \"john doe\", redact them with replacements from the JSON, and save the result as `output_redacted.pdf` .\n\n## Understanding the `process_pdf` Function\n\nThe `process_pdf` function is the core entry point of the SpectrePDF library. It enables users to load a PDF document, perform OCR-based detection of specific words or phrases, and then either annotate the document by drawing boxes around detected elements or redact sensitive information. The process involves rendering PDF pages as high-resolution images, running OCR to extract text data, grouping words logically, and applying modifications before saving a new PDF.\n\nThis function is highly configurable, allowing for various modes of operation such as simple detection with annotations, targeted redaction with replacements, or secure blackouts. Below, I'll break down its purpose, workflow, and every parameter in detail to ensure you can use it effectively."

[ReadTheDocs](https://camo.githubusercontent.\n\n...\n\nOn Windows, if `PATH` does not provide a Tesseract binary, we use the highest version number that is installed according to the Windows Registry.\n\n## Documentation and support\n\nOnce OCRmyPDF is installed, the built-in help which explains the command syntax and options can be accessed via:\n\n```\nocrmypdf --help\n```\n\nOur [documentation is served on Read the Docs](https://ocrmypdf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) .\n\nPlease report issues on our [GitHub issues](https://github.com/ocrmypdf/OCRmyPDF/issues) page, and follow the issue template for quick response.\n\n## Feature demo\n\n```\n#  Add an OCR layer and require PDF/A \nocrmypdf --output-type pdfa input.pdf output.pdf\n\n #  Convert an image to single page PDF \nocrmypdf input.jpg output.pdf\n\n #  Add OCR to a file in place (only modifies file on success) \nocrmypdf myfile.pdf myfile.pdf\n\n #  OCR with non-English languages (look up your language's ISO 639-3 code) \nocrmypdf -l fra LeParisien.pdf LeParisien.pdf\n#  OCR multilingual documents \nocrmypdf -l eng+fra Bilingual-English-French.pdf Bilingual-English-French.pdf\n\n #  Deskew (straighten crooked pages) \nocrmypdf --deskew input.pdf output.pdf\n```\n\nFor more features, see the [documentation](https://ocrmypdf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html) .\n\n## Requirements\n\nIn addition to the required Python version, OCRmyPDF requires external program installations of Ghostscript and Tesseract OCR. OCRmyPDF is pure Python, and runs on pretty much everything: Linux, macOS, Windows and FreeBSD.\n\n## Plugins\n\nOCRmyPDF provides a plugin interface allowing its capabilities to be extended or replaced. Here are some plugins we are aware of:\n\n* [OCRmyPDF-AppleOCR](https://github.com/mkyt/ocrmypdf-AppleOCR) : replaces the standard Tesseract OCR engine with Apple Vision Framework. Requires macOS.\n* [OCRmyPDF-EasyOCR](https://github.com/ocrmypdf/OCRmyPDF-EasyOCR) : replaces the standard Tesseract OCR engine with EasyOCR, a newer OCR engine based on PyTorch.\n\n...\n\nde/ratgeber/Durchsuchbare-PDF-Dokumente-mit-OCRmyPDF-erstellen-4607592.html)\n* [Excellent Utilities: OCRmyPDF](https://www.linuxlinks.com/excellent-utilities-ocrmypdf-add-ocr-text-layer-scanned-pdfs/)\n* [LinuxUser Texterkennung mit OCRmyPDF und Scanbd automatisieren](https://www.linux-community.de/ausgaben/linuxuser/2021/06/texterkennung-mit-ocrmypdf-und-scanbd-automatisieren/)\n* [Y Combinator discussion](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=32028752)\n\n## Business enquiries\n\nOCRmyPDF would not be the software that it is today without companies and users choosing to provide support for feature development and consulting enquiries. We are happy to discuss all enquiries, whether for extending the existing feature set, or integrating OCRmyPDF into a larger system.\n\n## License\n\nThe OCRmyPDF software is licensed under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL-2.0).\nThis license permits integration of OCRmyPDF with other code, included commercial and closed source, but asks you to publish source-level modifications you make to OCRmyPDF.\n\nSome components of OCRmyPDF have other licenses, as indicated by standard SPDX license identifiers or the DEP5 copyright and licensing information file.
