      "url": "https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/ai/llamaindex",
      "title": "LlamaIndex | Nic's notes",
      "publish_date": "2026-06-11",
      "excerpts": [
        "[Nic's notes](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/)\n\n1,030 notes / [tags](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/tags) / [2020](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2020/) [2021](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2021/) [2022](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2022/) [2023](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2023/) [2024](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2024/) [2025](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2025/) [2026](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/2026/) 23 Jun 2026 20:20\n\n# [LlamaIndex](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/ai/llamaindex \"Permalink to LlamaIndex\")\n\nEnterprise platform for AI agents that handle document OCR and multi-step workflows.\n\n26 Apr 2026 | in [ai](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/ai/) tags: [AI](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/tag/ai.html) [OCR](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/tag/ocr.html) [agents](https://notes.nicolasdeville.com/tag/agents.html)\n\nTable of Contents\n\n* What it does\n* Agent Workflows\n* Recent launches\n* Why it matters\n**LlamaIndex** is the platform behind what they call \"agentic OCR\" - AI agents that parse, extract, and process documents across 50+ file types. It started as an open-source RAG framework and has evolved into a full document intelligence platform for enterprises.\n\nlogo\n\nLlamaIndex | AI Agents for Document OCR + Workflows\n\nAutomate knowledge work starting with documents\n\n<https://www.llamaindex.ai/>\n\n## What it does\n\nThe core idea is task-specific agents that break down content (text, charts, tables, handwritten notes) and route each piece to the right extraction model. Instead of a single OCR pass, multiple agents collaborate on a document.\n\nThe platform has four pillars:\n\n* **LlamaParse** \\- the parsing engine. Handles complex layouts, embedded images, multi-page tables, handwritten notes. Supports 50+ unstructured file types.\n* **LlamaAgents** \\- one-click deployment of document agents. Pre-built templates for common document workflows.\n* **Extract** \\- structured data extraction from parsed documents.\n* **Index** \\- ingestion and RAG pipeline for search and retrieval.\n\n## Agent Workflows\n\nLlamaIndex introduced **Agent Workflows** with ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) integration. This means filesystem tools, MCP servers, persistent memory, and multi-step orchestration. You can chain agents together for end-to-end document automation.\n\nThey also ship pre-built document agent templates for instant deployment, plus a \"vibe-coding\" extraction mode where you describe what you want in natural language.\n\n## Recent launches\n\n* **LlamaAgents** for one-click agent deployment\n* **LlamaParse v2** with improved accuracy\n* **LlamaSheets** for messy spreadsheet parsing\n* **LlamaSplit** for document separation\n* **Parsebench** on Kaggle - the first document OCR benchmark for AI agents\n\n## Why it matters\n\nThis is where RAG meets document processing."

      "title": "LlamaIndex — AI Agent Framework Review 2026 | Agentlas",
      "publish_date": "2026-03-01",
      "excerpts": [
        "Agent las\n\n* Home\n* Frameworks\n* Compare\n\n1. Home\n2. / Frameworks\n3. / LlamaIndex\n\n# LlamaIndex — AI Agent Framework Review 2026\n\nCompany\n\nLlamaIndex Inc.\n\nLanguages\n\nPython, TypeScript\n\nLicense\n\nMIT\n\nGitHub Stars\n\n47k\n\nPricing\n\nFree / Open Source (LlamaCloud has paid tiers for managed ingestion and retrieval)\n\nLinks\n\n[GitHub](https://github.com/run-llama/llama_index) · [Website](https://llamaindex.ai)\n\n## Key Features\n\n* ✓ Advanced data connectors for 160+ data sources via LlamaHub\n* ✓ Multiple index types including vector, keyword, tree, and knowledge graph\n* ✓ Sophisticated query engines with sub-question decomposition\n* ✓ Agent framework with tool abstraction and ReAct reasoning\n* ✓ Workflows engine for event-driven, multi-step orchestration\n* ✓ Structured output extraction from unstructured documents\n* ✓ LlamaCloud managed parsing and retrieval service\n* ✓ LlamaParse for high-fidelity document parsing (PDF, tables, charts)\n* ✓ Evaluation framework for RAG pipeline quality measurement\n* ✓ Property graph and knowledge graph index support\n\n## Overview\n\nLlamaIndex has carved out a distinct position in the AI framework landscape as the premier solution for connecting large language models to private and external data. While other frameworks cast a wide net across the entire LLM application stack, LlamaIndex goes deep on the problems that matter most for data-intensive applications: ingestion, indexing, retrieval, and query understanding. This focused approach has earned it over 47,000 GitHub stars and a reputation as the go-to framework when your application needs to reason over documents, databases, and knowledge bases.\n\nFounded by Jerry Liu in late 2022 (originally as GPT Index), LlamaIndex has evolved from a simple vector indexing tool into a comprehensive data framework.\nIn 2026, LlamaIndex’s positioning has shifted toward Document AI and Agentic RAG, with new products like **LlamaParse v2** (multi-modal document understanding), **LlamaSheets** (spreadsheet-native AI extraction), and **LlamaSplit** (intelligent document chunking). Its evolution reflects a key insight: the hardest part of building useful LLM applications is not the model interaction itself, but getting the right data to the model at the right time in the right format.\n\n## Architecture\n\nLlamaIndex’s architecture is organized around a clear data pipeline: **load, index, query** .\n\nThe **data ingestion layer** provides connectors for over 160 data sources through LlamaHub, the community-maintained connector registry. These connectors handle everything from local files and databases to cloud services like Notion, Slack, Google Drive, and Confluence."

  "oldString": "# Similar Projects Comparison\n\n## Overview\n\nA survey of open-source tools that produce searchable, compressed PDFs from scanned\ndocuments or input PDFs.  The focus is on license compatibility, MRC compression,\nself-contained deployment, and Java portability.\n\n---\n\n## Project Matrix\n\n| Feature | TrulyFreeOCR | OCRmyPDF | archive-pdf-tools | pdf2pdfocr | Docling | PaddleOCR |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Language** | Java 21 (pure) | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python / C++ |\n| **License** | Apache 2.0 | MPL-2.0 | AGPL-3.0 | Apache 2.0 | MIT | Apache 2.0 |\n| **GitHub Stars** | — | ~29k | ~140 | ~300 | ~62k | ~84k |\n| **OCR Engine** | Tesseract 5 CLI | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract | Tesseract / pluggable | PaddleOCR (own) |\n| **MRC Compression** | Yes (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) | Yes (via `--optimize`) | Yes (JPEG2000 + JBIG2) | No | No (extraction only) | No |\n| **Self-Contained** | Yes (single JAR) | No (Python + Ghostscript + Tesseract) | No (Python + system deps) | No | No (Python + models) | No (Python + GPU) |\n| **PDF/A** | Yes (2b) | Yes (1b/2b/3b) | Yes (3b) | No | Focused on extraction | No |\n| **No Native Deps** | Only Tesseract subprocess | Ghostscript + Tesseract | Kakadu/OpenJPEG + MuPDF | pdftk + qpdf + ghostscript | Models downloaded at runtime | PyTorch / GPU |\n| **Image Preprocessing** | Otsu + background normalization | Deskew, clean, deskew | Sauvola binarization | None | Vision-based layout models | DL-based |\n| **Concurrent OCR** | Optional (configurable threads) | Yes (multi-core) | Yes | Yes | Yes (batching) | Yes (GPU batch) |\n| **Language Support** | 20 languages (tessdata) | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages |\n| **CLI Interface** | picocli | argparse | Custom | argparse | Typer | Custom |\n| **Active Development** | Yes | Yes (v17.7.0, Jun 2026) | Stale (last release 2021) | Low (last commit 2023) | Yes (v2.x, weekly) | Yes (v3.7.0, Jun 2026) |\n\n---\n\n## Detailed Analysis\n\n### OCRmyPDF (ocrmypdf/OCRmyPDF)\n- **License**: MPL-2.0 — business-friendly, allows closed-source integration if\n  modifications to OCRmyPDF itself are published.\n- **Strengths**: Gold standard for PDF OCR.  Battle-tested on millions of PDFs.\n  Rich preprocessing (deskew, clean, deskew, rotate).  Full PDF/A support.\n  100+ languages.  Plugin architecture (AppleOCR, EasyOCR, PaddleOCR backends).\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + system dependencies (Ghostscript, Tesseract).  Not\n  self-contained.  MPL-2.0 requires source disclosure of any modifications to\n  OCRmyPDF code itself.  Ghostscript is AGPL (used as subprocess, not linked).\n- **Relevance**: Directly comparable in output quality and feature set.  Our\n  advantage is Java portability (single JAR) and Apache 2.0 license (no\n  publication requirement).\n\n### archive-pdf-tools (internetarchive/archive-pdf-tools)\n- **License**: AGPL-3.0 — **not compatible** with commercial use without\n  disclosing entire application source.\n- **Strengths**: Mature MRC compression with JPEG2000.  Proven at scale (6M PDFs\n  in 2021).  PDF/A-3b and PDF/UA support.  Mask viewing tools.\n- **Weaknesses**: AGPL-3.0 is a non-starter for commercial use.  Requires\n  system JPEG2000 codec (Kakadu or OpenJPEG).  Python + complex dependency tree.\n  Stale (last release 2021).\n- **Relevance**: Shows that MRC + OCR at Internet Archive scale is feasible.\n  Our MRC approach (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) is simpler and avoids JPEG2000\n  licensing issues.\n\n### pdf2pdfocr (LeoFCardoso/pdf2pdfocr)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 — same as ours.\n- **Strengths**: Simple, straightforward.  No re-encoding of source images when\n  possible.  GUI available.  Cross-platform (Windows batch script included).\n- **Weaknesses**: No MRC compression.  No PDF/A.  No image preprocessing.\n  Low community (303 stars).  1.2.0 \"marapurense\" release never formalized.\n- **Relevance**: Demonstrates the simplest possible OCR sandwich approach.\n  Our tool adds MRC compression and full PDF/A for a material improvement.\n\n### Docling (docling-project/docling)\n- **License**: MIT — permissive.\n- **Strengths**: IBM Research quality.  Vision-based layout analysis (avoids\n  pixel-level OCR for clean PDFs).  TableFormer for table extraction.  30x faster\n  than OCR for born-digital PDFs.  Huge ecosystem (LangChain, LlamaIndex).\n  61k+ stars.\n- **Weaknesses**: Focused on *extraction* to Markdown/JSON, not on producing\n  compressed searchable PDFs.  Heavy model downloads (GBs).  Python-only.\n  Requires PyTorch for local inference.\n- **Relevance**: Complementary — Docling is for RAG pipelines, not for\n  producing archive-quality PDF output.  If we ever add a structure-preserving\n  extraction mode, Docling's approach (vision models for layout) would be the\n  direction.\n\n### PaddleOCR (PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: SOTA accuracy (reported better than Tesseract for many\n  scripts).  GPU-accelerated.  84k+ stars.  LLM-ready output (JSON/Markdown).\n  Mature document parsing pipeline.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch/PaddlePaddle runtime.  GPU strongly\n  recommended for practical throughput.  Model downloads are large.  No\n  searchable PDF output (focused on text extraction).\n- **Relevance**: PaddleOCR is the best OCR *engine* available today.  If we\n  ever add a pluggable OCR backend, PaddleOCR CLI (via subprocess, like\n  Tesseract) would be a natural second option for higher accuracy.\n\n### olmOCR (allenai/olmOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (model weights, code).\n- **Strengths**: VLM-based OCR that preserves reading order, handles tables,\n  equations, handwriting.  Designed for high-throughput (estimated $178/1M\n  pages).\n- **Weaknesses**: Requires GPU (FP8 inference).  7B parameter model.  Very new\n  (2025/2026).  No searchable PDF output — text-only.\n- **Relevance**: VLM-based OCR is the emerging paradigm.  Once these models\n  become efficient enough to run on CPU, a subprocess-based integration would\n  be straightforward.\n\n---\n\n## Why TrulyFreeOCR Exists\n\n| Problem | Existing Tools | Our Solution |\n|---|---|---|\n| Commercial-safe license | OCRmyPDF (MPL-2.0) requires publishing modifications; archive-pdf-tools is AGPL | Apache 2.0 — no disclosure obligations |\n| Self-contained deployment | All alternatives require Python + system libs + model downloads | Single fat JAR + bundled native binaries + bootstrap script |\n| Java/enterprise integration | Python tools need subprocess calls from Java code | Native Java API, embeddable in Maven/Gradle projects |\n| MRC compression | Only OCRmyPDF and archive-pdf-tools offer it, with complex deps | PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg with clean fallback |\n| No Ghostscript dependency | OCRmyPDF requires Ghostscript (AGPL) | Pure PDFBox — no AGPL anywhere in the stack |\n\n## Potential Future Directions\n\n1. **Pluggable OCR backends** — Add PaddleOCR CLI as an alternative to\n   Tesseract (subprocess-based, same pattern).\n2. **Deskew/clean preprocessing** — Add Leptonica-based deskew and text\n   cleaning via subprocess (similar to OCRmyPDF's `--deskew`).\n3. **JPEG2000 background** — Add optional JPEG2000 background compression\n   via OpenJPEG subprocess for better compression ratios than PNG.\n4. **Docling-style extraction** — A `--extract-json` mode that outputs\n   structured Markdown/JSON for RAG pipelines.",
  "newString": "# Similar Projects Comparison\n\n## Overview\n\nA survey of open-source tools that produce searchable, compressed PDFs from scanned\ndocuments or input PDFs.  The focus is on license compatibility, MRC compression,\nself-contained deployment, and Java portability.\n\n---\n\n## Project Matrix — Traditional OCR & PDF Tools\n\n| Feature | TrulyFreeOCR | OCRmyPDF | archive-pdf-tools | pdf2pdfocr | Docling | PaddleOCR |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Language** | Java 21 (pure) | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python / C++ |\n| **License** | Apache 2.0 | MPL-2.0 | AGPL-3.0 | Apache 2.0 | MIT | Apache 2.0 |\n| **GitHub Stars** | — | ~29k | ~140 | ~300 | ~62k | ~84k |\n| **OCR Engine** | Tesseract 5 CLI | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract | Tesseract / pluggable | PaddleOCR (own) |\n| **MRC Compression** | Yes (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) | Yes (via `--optimize`) | Yes (JPEG2000 + JBIG2) | No | No (extraction only) | No |\n| **Self-Contained** | Yes (single JAR) | No | No | No | No | No |\n| **PDF/A** | Yes (2b) | Yes (1b/2b/3b) | Yes (3b) | No | Extraction only | No |\n| **No Native Deps** | Only Tesseract | Ghostscript + Tesseract | Kakadu/OpenJPEG + MuPDF | pdftk + qpdf + gs | Models at runtime | PyTorch / GPU |\n| **Image Preprocessing** | Otsu + background norm. | Deskew, clean, rotate | Sauvola binarization | None | Vision-based layout | DL-based |\n| **Concurrent OCR** | Optional (configurable) | Yes (multi-core) | Yes | Yes | Yes (batching) | Yes (GPU batch) |\n| **Language Support** | 20 languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages |\n| **CLI Interface** | picocli | argparse | Custom | argparse | Typer | Custom |\n| **Active Development** | Yes | Yes (v17.7.0) | Stale (2021) | Low (2023) | Yes (v2.x) | Yes (v3.7.0) |\n\n## Project Matrix — VLM / End-to-End OCR Models\n\n| Feature | DeepSeek-OCR | GOT-OCR2.0 | GLM-OCR | MonkeyOCR | Unlimited-OCR | olmOCR |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Language** | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python |\n| **License** | MIT | Apache 2.0 (code), research-only (weights) | MIT (model), Apache 2.0 (code) | Alibaba non-commercial (weights), Apache 2.0 (code) | MIT | Apache 2.0 |\n| **GitHub Stars** | ~23k | ~8k | ~7k | ~6k | ~11k | ~4k |\n| **OCR Engine** | VLM (3B) | VLM (580M) | VLM (0.9B) | VLM (1.2B / 3B) | VLM (3B) | VLM (7B) |\n| **MRC Compression** | No | No | No | No | No | No |\n| **Self-Contained** | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) |\n| **PDF/A** | No | No | No | No | No | No |\n| **Output Format** | Plain text | Plain / formatted text | Markdown / JSON | Markdown / JSON | Markdown | Plain text |\n| **GPU Required** | Yes (A100-40G) | Yes (24GB+) | Yes (4GB VRAM) | Yes (3090+) | Yes (24GB+) | Yes (FP8) |\n| **Speed** | ~2500 tokens/s (A100) | — | 1.86 pages/s | ~1 page/s (4090) | 1+ page/s (A100) | — |\n| **Active Development** | Yes (Oct 2025) | Moderate (2024) | Yes (Feb 2026) | Yes (Jun 2025) | Yes (Jun 2026) | Low (2025) |\n\n## Project Matrix — DL OCR Toolkits & Desktop Tools\n\n| Feature | EasyOCR | surya | docTR | RapidOCR | Umi-OCR | PandaOCR | SwiftOCR | llama_index |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Language** | Python | Python | Python | Python / C++ / Java / C# | Python + QML | AutoIt / Pascal | Swift | Python / TypeScript |\n| **License** | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 (code), OpenRAIL-M (weights) | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 | MIT | Custom freeware | Apache 2.0 | MIT |\n| **GitHub Stars** | ~30k | ~30k | ~6k | ~7k | ~46k | ~5k | ~5k | ~50k |\n| **OCR Engine** | CRAFT + CRNN (PyTorch) | Own VLM (650M) | DBNet + CRNN / ViT | PaddleOCR → ONNX | PaddleOCR / RapidOCR | Cloud API aggregator | NN (Swift) | LlamaParse (cloud) |\n| **MRC Compression** | No | No | No | No | Basic (dual-layer PDF) | No | No | No |\n| **Self-Contained** | No (Python + PyTorch) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + PyTorch) | No (Python) | Yes (Windows exe + runtime) | Yes (Windows exe) | Yes (Swift pkg) | No (Python / cloud API) |\n| **PDF/A** | No | No | No | No | No | No | No | No |\n| **Searchable PDF** | No | No | No | No | Yes (basic dual-layer) | No | No | No |\n| **Concurrent OCR** | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes | Yes | N/A (sequential) | No | Yes (cloud) |\n| **Active Development** | Moderate (v1.7.2) | Yes (v0.20.0) | Moderate | Yes (v3.9.0) | Yes (v2.1.5) | Yes (Pro v5.59) | Deprecated (2020) | Yes (weekly) |\n\n---\n\n## Detailed Analysis\n\n### OCRmyPDF (ocrmypdf/OCRmyPDF)\n- **License**: MPL-2.0 — business-friendly, allows closed-source integration if\n  modifications to OCRmyPDF itself are published.\n- **Strengths**: Gold standard for PDF OCR.  Battle-tested on millions of PDFs.\n  Rich preprocessing (deskew, clean, rotate).  Full PDF/A support.  100+ languages.\n  Plugin architecture (AppleOCR, EasyOCR, PaddleOCR backends).\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + system dependencies (Ghostscript, Tesseract).  Not\n  self-contained.  MPL-2.0 requires source disclosure of modifications to\n  OCRmyPDF code.  Ghostscript is AGPL (used as subprocess, not linked).\n- **Relevance**: Directly comparable in output quality and feature set.  Our\n  advantage is Java portability (single JAR) and Apache 2.0 license (no\n  publication requirement).\n\n### archive-pdf-tools (internetarchive/archive-pdf-tools)\n- **License**: AGPL-3.0 — **not compatible** with commercial use without\n  disclosing entire application source.\n- **Strengths**: Mature MRC compression with JPEG2000.  Proven at scale (6M PDFs\n  in 2021).  PDF/A-3b and PDF/UA support.  Mask viewing tools.\n- **Weaknesses**: AGPL-3.0 is a non-starter for commercial use.  Requires\n  system JPEG2000 codec (Kakadu or OpenJPEG).  Python + complex dependency tree.\n  Stale (last release 2021).\n- **Relevance**: Shows that MRC + OCR at Internet Archive scale is feasible.\n  Our MRC approach (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) is simpler and avoids JPEG2000\n  licensing issues.\n\n### pdf2pdfocr (LeoFCardoso/pdf2pdfocr)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 — same as ours.\n- **Strengths**: Simple, straightforward.  No re-encoding of source images when\n  possible.  GUI available.  Cross-platform (Windows batch script included).\n- **Weaknesses**: No MRC compression.  No PDF/A.  No image preprocessing.\n  Low community (303 stars).  1.2.0 release never formalized.\n- **Relevance**: Demonstrates the simplest possible OCR sandwich approach.\n  Our tool adds MRC compression and full PDF/A for a material improvement.\n\n### Docling (docling-project/docling)\n- **License**: MIT — permissive.\n- **Strengths**: IBM Research quality.  Vision-based layout analysis (avoids\n  pixel-level OCR for clean PDFs).  TableFormer for table extraction.  30x faster\n  than OCR for born-digital PDFs.  Huge ecosystem (LangChain, LlamaIndex).\n  61k+ stars.\n- **Weaknesses**: Focused on *extraction* to Markdown/JSON, not on producing\n  compressed searchable PDFs.  Heavy model downloads (GBs).  Python-only.\n  Requires PyTorch for local inference.\n- **Relevance**: Complementary — Docling is for RAG pipelines, not for\n  producing archive-quality PDF output.  If we ever add a structure-preserving\n  extraction mode, Docling's approach (vision models for layout) would be the\n  direction.\n\n### PaddleOCR (PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: SOTA accuracy (reported better than Tesseract for many\n  scripts).  GPU-accelerated.  84k+ stars.  LLM-ready output (JSON/Markdown).\n  Mature document parsing pipeline.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch/PaddlePaddle runtime.  GPU strongly\n  recommended for practical throughput.  Model downloads are large.  No\n  searchable PDF output (focused on text extraction).\n- **Relevance**: PaddleOCR is the best OCR *engine* available today.  If we\n  ever add a pluggable OCR backend, PaddleOCR CLI (via subprocess, like\n  Tesseract) would be a natural second option for higher accuracy.\n\n### olmOCR (allenai/olmOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (model weights, code).\n- **Strengths**: VLM-based OCR that preserves reading order, handles tables,\n  equations, handwriting.  Designed for high-throughput (estimated $178/1M pages).\n- **Weaknesses**: Requires GPU (FP8 inference).  7B parameter model.  Very new\n  (2025/2026).  No searchable PDF output — text-only.\n- **Relevance**: VLM-based OCR is the emerging paradigm.  Once these models\n  become efficient enough to run on CPU, a subprocess-based integration would\n  be straightforward.\n\n### EasyOCR (JaidedAI/EasyOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: Ready-to-use with 80+ languages.  CRAFT-based text detection +\n  CRNN recognition.  GPU and CPU support.  Active community (~30k stars).\n  Simple Python API.  Custom model training support.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch dependency.  Model downloads (hundreds of MB).\n  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Accuracy lags behind modern\n  VLM approaches for complex layouts.\n- **Relevance**: The most popular general-purpose OCR library.  If we add a\n  Python subprocess-based pluggable OCR backend, EasyOCR would be a candidate\n  for non-Tesseract engines (alongside PaddleOCR).\n\n### surya (VikParuchuri/surya, now datalab-to/surya)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (code), modified OpenRAIL-M (weights — free for\n  research/personal/startups under $5M funding/revenue).  Commercial use beyond\n  limits requires license from Datalab.\n- **Strengths**: 650M param model scoring 83.3% on olmOCR-bench (top under 3B\n  params).  5 pages/s on RTX 5090.  90+ languages.  Includes layout analysis,\n  reading order, table recognition.  CPU, GPU, MPS support.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU recommended.  Model weights have commercial\n  restrictions.  Weighted licensing model complicates commercial use.  No\n  searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.\n- **Relevance**: Surya represents the best lightweight VLM OCR available.\n  Its layout analysis and reading order are superior to Tesseract for complex\n  documents.  If the weight licensing were fully Apache 2.0, it would be the\n  ideal Tesseract replacement for a pluggable backend.\n\n### docTR (mindee/doctr)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: Two-stage (detection + recognition) OCR with PyTorch and\n  TensorFlow backends.  Swappable architectures (DBNet, CRNN, ViTSTR, PARSeq,\n  MASTER).  Comparable accuracy to Google Vision / AWS Textract on public\n  benchmarks.  CPU and GPU support.  FastAPI integration.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch/TensorFlow dependency.  No searchable PDF\n  output.  No MRC compression.  Smaller community (~6k stars).  No built-in\n  layout analysis beyond text detection.\n- **Relevance**: A well-engineered, modular OCR library.  Its architecture\n  flexibility makes it useful for research and custom OCR pipelines, but it\n  lacks the document-to-PDF output pipeline we provide.\n\n### RapidOCR (RapidAI/RapidOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: PaddleOCR models converted to ONNX for cross-platform deployment.\n  Multi-language support (Python, C++, Java, C#, Go).  Multiple inference backends\n  (ONNXRuntime, OpenVINO, PaddlePaddle, PyTorch).  Very fast (3.3M monthly pip\n  downloads).  Active development (v3.9.0).  Good for Chinese + English.\n- **Weaknesses**: Focused on text detection + recognition only.  No searchable\n  PDF output.  No MRC compression.  No layout analysis beyond basic detection.\n  Primarily optimized for Chinese/English (smaller language coverage).\n- **Relevance**: RapidOCR is the fastest pure-OCR library available.  Its Java\n  binding is interesting for JVM integration, but it lacks the full PDF pipeline\n  (compression, PDF/A, text layer) that TrulyFreeOCR provides.\n\n### DeepSeek-OCR (deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-OCR)\n- **License**: MIT (code and weights).\n- **Strengths**: \"Contexts Optical Compression\" — encodes text-rich images into\n  compact vision tokens (64-400 tokens for 600-5000+ text tokens).  ~97%\n  accuracy.  200k+ pages/day on a single A100.  Nearly 100 languages.  3B param\n  model.  Very popular (~23k stars in 8 months).\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + large GPU (A100-40G) for practical throughput.\n  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Research-oriented (no\n  production packaging).  Very new (October 2025).\n- **Relevance**: DeepSeek-OCR pioneered the VLM-as-OCR paradigm.  Its\n  compression technique is conceptually aligned with our MRC approach (compress\n  while preserving information).  The model itself could serve as a Tesseract\n  replacement for highest-accuracy needs, given sufficient hardware.\n\n### GOT-OCR2.0 (Ucas-HaoranWei/GOT-OCR2.0)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (code, HuggingFace weights), research-only (original\n  weights from Vary license).\n- **Strengths**: First unified \"OCR-2.0\" end-to-end model.  580M parameters\n  (smaller than DeepSeek-OCR).  Handles plain text, formulas, tables, charts,\n  sheet music, geometric shapes.  Region-level recognition via coordinates or\n  colors.  Multi-crop for high-resolution images.  Dynamic resolution.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU (24GB+).  Research-only license on original\n  weights (though HF version uses Apache 2.0).  No searchable PDF output.  No\n  MRC compression.  Last code update December 2024.\n- **Relevance**: GOT-OCR2.0 demonstrated that a single end-to-end model could\n  replace the traditional detection+recognition pipeline.  Its approach\n  influenced DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, and the entire VLM OCR trend.\n\n### GLM-OCR (zai-org/GLM-OCR)\n- **License**: MIT (model), Apache 2.0 (code).\n- **Strengths**: State-of-the-art on OmniDocBench V1.5 (94.62).  Only 0.9B\n  parameters — runs on 4GB VRAM.  1.86 pages/second.  Multi-Token Prediction\n  (MTP) loss and RL training.  CogViT visual encoder.  PP-DocLayout-V3 layout\n  analysis.  Excellent for complex tables, code documents, seals.  vLLM,\n  SGLang, Ollama support.\n- **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC\n  compression.  Very new (February 2026).  Pipeline depends on PP-DocLayout-V3\n  (Apache 2.0).\n- **Relevance**: GLM-OCR achieves the best accuracy/size ratio of any VLM OCR\n  model.  Its 0.9B parameter size and 4GB VRAM requirement make it the most\n  accessible high-accuracy OCR model.  Ideal candidate for a future pluggable\n  VLM backend once GPU requirements decrease further.\n\n### MonkeyOCR (Yuliang-Liu/MonkeyOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0 (code), Alibaba non-commercial license (weights).\n- **Strengths**: Structure-Recognition-Relation (SRR) triplet paradigm.\n  Surpasses GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5-Pro on OmniDocBench.  ~1 page/s on RTX 4090.\n  MonkeyOCR-pro-1.2B leaner version available.  MonkeyDoc dataset (3M+ pages).\n  Chinese and English support.  Cross-page table reconstruction (v1.5).\n- **Weaknesses**: Alibaba non-commercial weight license — **not suitable for\n  commercial use**.  Python + GPU required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC\n  compression.  Weight licensing restricts practical deployment.\n- **Relevance**: Among the most accurate document parsers available, but the\n  non-commercial weight license makes it unusable for our commercial-safe\n  requirement.  Demonstrates the SRR paradigm for document understanding.\n\n### Unlimited-OCR (baidu/Unlimited-OCR)\n- **License**: MIT.\n- **Strengths**: One-shot long-horizon parsing (entire multi-page document in\n  single pass).  Sliding window memory prevents OOM on long documents.  3B params,\n  32,768 token context.  Built on DeepSeek-OCR innovations.  MIT license is fully\n  business-friendly.  11k+ stars in one week (June 2026).\n- **Weaknesses**: Just released (June 22, 2026) — very early.  Python + GPU\n  required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Ecosystem and\n  tooling still immature.\n- **Relevance**: The most promising recent VLM OCR entry.  Its one-shot long\n  document capability and MIT license make it ideal for a future pluggable\n  backend once VLM OCR matures and GPU requirements decrease.  Baidu's\n  reputation ensures ongoing development.\n\n### Umi-OCR (hiroi-sora/Umi-OCR)\n- **License**: MIT.\n- **Strengths**: Fully offline desktop OCR app (Windows/Linux).  Screenshot OCR,\n  batch processing, PDF recognition with dual-layer searchable PDF output.\n  100+ languages via PaddleOCR or RapidOCR engines.  QR/barcode scanning.\n  Formula recognition (LaTeX).  No GPU required.  46k stars.  Portable (unzip and\n  run).  HTTP API for external integration.\n- **Weaknesses**: Desktop GUI app — not designed for server/automation use.\n  Dual-layer PDF output is basic (no MRC compression, no PDF/A).  Windows-focused\n  (Linux support experimental).  Python runtime bundled (large download).\n  No CLI-first design.\n- **Relevance**: Umi-OCR is the most comparable tool for *desktop* searchable\n  PDF creation.  Its dual-layer PDF output proves demand for this feature.\n  Our tool targets the server/enterprise space where Umi-OCR cannot operate.\n\n### PandaOCR (miaomiaosoft/PandaOCR / PandaOCR.Pro)\n- **License**: Custom freeware — not open source (source code not provided on\n  GitHub, only binary releases).  PandaOCR.Pro has paid \"Pro\" activation.\n- **Strengths**: Multi-engine OCR aggregator (Baidu, Tencent, Sogou, Youdao, Ali,\n  iFLYTEK, Mathpix, etc.).  Screenshot OCR, translation, TTS, formula/table\n  recognition.  Game translation.  QR/barcode scanning.  Image search.\n  Active development (Pro v5.59, June 2026).  7+ years of updates.\n- **Weaknesses**: Not open source (binary-only releases).  Relies on cloud API\n  keys (no offline OCR).  Windows-only.  Free version has limitations; Pro\n  requires payment.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Privacy\n  concerns (uploads to third-party APIs).\n- **Relevance**: Demonstrates user demand for multi-engine OCR aggregation.\n  Our approach (single bundled Tesseract + optional VLM backends) avoids the\n  complexity of managing multiple cloud API keys and the associated privacy\n  risks.\n\n### SwiftOCR (NMAC427/SwiftOCR)\n- **License**: Apache 2.0.\n- **Strengths**: Native Swift OCR library for iOS/macOS.  Neural network-based.\n  Simple API (6 lines of code).  Good for short alphanumeric codes (serial\n  numbers, gift cards).  GPUImage acceleration.\n- **Weaknesses**: **Deprecated** (last commit May 2020).  Only recognizes short\n  alphanumeric text — not suitable for document OCR.  iOS/macOS only.  No\n  searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  No longer maintained.\n- **Relevance**: Minimal — deprecated and limited in scope.  Included for\n  completeness but not a relevant comparison to our document PDF pipeline.\n\n### llama_index (run-llama/llama_index + LlamaParse)\n- **License**: MIT (core framework).  LlamaParse is a cloud service with paid\n  tiers (free tier: 10k credits/month ≈ 1k pages).\n- **Strengths**: Leading RAG framework (50k+ stars).  LlamaParse provides\n  agentic OCR with layout-aware parsing, multi-tier strategy (Fast, Cost\n  Effective, Agentic).  90+ file types.  Multi-page table handling, embedded\n  images, handwritten notes.  Python + TypeScript SDKs.  Java SDK available.\n  Deep LLM ecosystem integration.\n- **Weaknesses**: LlamaParse is a cloud service (not self-contained).  Paid per\n  page ($1-15/1k pages).  No MRC compression.  No PDF/A output.  Focused on\n  extraction for RAG, not archive-quality PDF production.  Framework is\n  Python-heavy for self-hosted use.\n- **Relevance**: LlamaIndex/LlamaParse represents the cloud/API approach to\n  document OCR.  Our tool competes in the self-contained, offline, commercial-safe\n  space.  If users want cloud OCR with RAG pipelines, LlamaParse is the best\n  option; if they need offline batch PDF production with MRC compression, we\n  provide that.\n\n---\n\n## Why TrulyFreeOCR Exists\n\n| Problem | Existing Tools | Our Solution |\n|---|---|---|\n| Commercial-safe license | OCRmyPDF (MPL-2.0) requires publishing modifications; archive-pdf-tools is AGPL; MonkeyOCR has non-commercial weights; surya has restricted weights | Apache 2.0 — no disclosure obligations; all deps have business-friendly licenses |\n| Self-contained deployment | All alternatives require Python + system libs + model downloads or cloud APIs | Single fat JAR + bundled native binaries + bootstrap script |\n| Java/enterprise integration | Python tools need subprocess calls from Java code | Native Java API, embeddable in Maven/Gradle projects |\n| MRC compression | Only OCRmyPDF and archive-pdf-tools offer it, with complex deps; Umi-OCR offers basic dual-layer | PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg with clean fallback |\n| No Ghostscript dependency | OCRmyPDF requires Ghostscript (AGPL) | Pure PDFBox — no AGPL anywhere in the stack |\n| VLM future-proofing | Current VLM models (DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, Unlimited-OCR) require GPU and produce text only | Architecture supports pluggable backends; Tesseract is performant on CPU today |\n| Offline/privacy | PandaOCR, LlamaParse send data to cloud APIs; EasyOCR/docTR need Python runtime | Fully offline; everything runs locally; no data leaves the machine |\n\n## Potential Future Directions\n\n1. **Pluggable OCR backends** — Add PaddleOCR CLI or RapidOCR as alternatives to\n   Tesseract (subprocess-based, same pattern).  Once VLM models (GLM-OCR,\n   Unlimited-OCR) become CPU-efficient, add them as higher-accuracy backends.\n2. **Deskew/clean preprocessing** — Add Leptonica-based deskew and text\n   cleaning via subprocess (similar to OCRmyPDF's `--deskew`).\n3. **JPEG2000 background** — Add optional JPEG2000 background compression\n   via OpenJPEG subprocess for better compression ratios than PNG.\n4. **Docling-style extraction** — A `--extract-json` mode that outputs\n   structured Markdown/JSON for RAG pipelines.\n5. **Umi-OCR-style desktop GUI** — If desktop demand grows, a lightweight\n   GUI wrapper around the CLI (like Umi-OCR but Java-based) could serve\n   non-technical users."
}

<content>
1: # Similar Projects Comparison
2: 
3: ## Overview
4: 
5: A survey of open-source tools that produce searchable, compressed PDFs from scanned
6: documents or input PDFs.  The focus is on license compatibility, MRC compression,
7: self-contained deployment, and Java portability.
8: 
9: ---
10: 
11: ## Project Matrix — Traditional OCR & PDF Tools
12: 
13: | Feature | TrulyFreeOCR | OCRmyPDF | archive-pdf-tools | pdf2pdfocr | Docling | PaddleOCR |
14: |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
15: | **Language** | Java 21 (pure) | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python / C++ |
16: | **License** | Apache 2.0 | MPL-2.0 | AGPL-3.0 | Apache 2.0 | MIT | Apache 2.0 |
17: | **GitHub Stars** | — | ~29k | ~140 | ~300 | ~62k | ~84k |
18: | **OCR Engine** | Tesseract 5 CLI | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract 5 | Tesseract | Tesseract / pluggable | PaddleOCR (own) |
19: | **MRC Compression** | Yes (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) | Yes (via `--optimize`) | Yes (JPEG2000 + JBIG2) | No | No (extraction only) | No |
20: | **Self-Contained** | Yes (single JAR) | No | No | No | No | No |
21: | **PDF/A** | Yes (2b) | Yes (1b/2b/3b) | Yes (3b) | No | Extraction only | No |
22: | **No Native Deps** | Only Tesseract | Ghostscript + Tesseract | Kakadu/OpenJPEG + MuPDF | pdftk + qpdf + gs | Models at runtime | PyTorch / GPU |
23: | **Image Preprocessing** | Otsu + background norm. | Deskew, clean, rotate | Sauvola binarization | None | Vision-based layout | DL-based |
24: | **Concurrent OCR** | Optional (configurable) | Yes (multi-core) | Yes | Yes | Yes (batching) | Yes (GPU batch) |
25: | **Language Support** | 20 languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages | 100+ languages |
26: | **CLI Interface** | picocli | argparse | Custom | argparse | Typer | Custom |
27: | **Active Development** | Yes | Yes (v17.7.0) | Stale (2021) | Low (2023) | Yes (v2.x) | Yes (v3.7.0) |
28: 
29: ## Project Matrix — VLM / End-to-End OCR Models
30: 
31: | Feature | DeepSeek-OCR | GOT-OCR2.0 | GLM-OCR | MonkeyOCR | Unlimited-OCR | olmOCR |
32: |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
33: | **Language** | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python | Python |
34: | **License** | MIT | Apache 2.0 (code), research-only (weights) | MIT (model), Apache 2.0 (code) | Alibaba non-commercial (weights), Apache 2.0 (code) | MIT | Apache 2.0 |
35: | **GitHub Stars** | ~23k | ~8k | ~7k | ~6k | ~11k | ~4k |
36: | **OCR Engine** | VLM (3B) | VLM (580M) | VLM (0.9B) | VLM (1.2B / 3B) | VLM (3B) | VLM (7B) |
37: | **MRC Compression** | No | No | No | No | No | No |
38: | **Self-Contained** | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + GPU) |
39: | **PDF/A** | No | No | No | No | No | No |
40: | **Output Format** | Plain text | Plain / formatted text | Markdown / JSON | Markdown / JSON | Markdown | Plain text |
41: | **GPU Required** | Yes (A100-40G) | Yes (24GB+) | Yes (4GB VRAM) | Yes (3090+) | Yes (24GB+) | Yes (FP8) |
42: | **Speed** | ~2500 tokens/s (A100) | — | 1.86 pages/s | ~1 page/s (4090) | 1+ page/s (A100) | — |
43: | **Active Development** | Yes (Oct 2025) | Moderate (2024) | Yes (Feb 2026) | Yes (Jun 2025) | Yes (Jun 2026) | Low (2025) |
44: 
45: ## Project Matrix — DL OCR Toolkits & Desktop Tools
46: 
47: | Feature | EasyOCR | surya | docTR | RapidOCR | Umi-OCR | PandaOCR | SwiftOCR | llama_index |
48: |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
49: | **Language** | Python | Python | Python | Python / C++ / Java / C# | Python + QML | AutoIt / Pascal | Swift | Python / TypeScript |
50: | **License** | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 (code), OpenRAIL-M (weights) | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 | MIT | Custom freeware | Apache 2.0 | MIT |
51: | **GitHub Stars** | ~30k | ~30k | ~6k | ~7k | ~46k | ~5k | ~5k | ~50k |
52: | **OCR Engine** | CRAFT + CRNN (PyTorch) | Own VLM (650M) | DBNet + CRNN / ViT | PaddleOCR → ONNX | PaddleOCR / RapidOCR | Cloud API aggregator | NN (Swift) | LlamaParse (cloud) |
53: | **MRC Compression** | No | No | No | No | Basic (dual-layer PDF) | No | No | No |
54: | **Self-Contained** | No (Python + PyTorch) | No (Python + GPU) | No (Python + PyTorch) | No (Python) | Yes (Windows exe + runtime) | Yes (Windows exe) | Yes (Swift pkg) | No (Python / cloud API) |
55: | **PDF/A** | No | No | No | No | No | No | No | No |
56: | **Searchable PDF** | No | No | No | No | Yes (basic dual-layer) | No | No | No |
57: | **Concurrent OCR** | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes (GPU batch) | Yes | Yes | N/A (sequential) | No | Yes (cloud) |
58: | **Active Development** | Moderate (v1.7.2) | Yes (v0.20.0) | Moderate | Yes (v3.9.0) | Yes (v2.1.5) | Yes (Pro v5.59) | Deprecated (2020) | Yes (weekly) |
59: 
60: ---
61: 
62: ## Detailed Analysis
63: 
64: ### OCRmyPDF (ocrmypdf/OCRmyPDF)
65: - **License**: MPL-2.0 — business-friendly, allows closed-source integration if
66:   modifications to OCRmyPDF itself are published.
67: - **Strengths**: Gold standard for PDF OCR.  Battle-tested on millions of PDFs.
68:   Rich preprocessing (deskew, clean, rotate).  Full PDF/A support.  100+ languages.
69:   Plugin architecture (AppleOCR, EasyOCR, PaddleOCR backends).
70: - **Weaknesses**: Python + system dependencies (Ghostscript, Tesseract).  Not
71:   self-contained.  MPL-2.0 requires source disclosure of modifications to
72:   OCRmyPDF code.  Ghostscript is AGPL (used as subprocess, not linked).
73: - **Relevance**: Directly comparable in output quality and feature set.  Our
74:   advantage is Java portability (single JAR) and Apache 2.0 license (no
75:   publication requirement).
76: 
77: ### archive-pdf-tools (internetarchive/archive-pdf-tools)
78: - **License**: AGPL-3.0 — **not compatible** with commercial use without
79:   disclosing entire application source.
80: - **Strengths**: Mature MRC compression with JPEG2000.  Proven at scale (6M PDFs
81:   in 2021).  PDF/A-3b and PDF/UA support.  Mask viewing tools.
82: - **Weaknesses**: AGPL-3.0 is a non-starter for commercial use.  Requires
83:   system JPEG2000 codec (Kakadu or OpenJPEG).  Python + complex dependency tree.
84:   Stale (last release 2021).
85: - **Relevance**: Shows that MRC + OCR at Internet Archive scale is feasible.
86:   Our MRC approach (PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg) is simpler and avoids JPEG2000
87:   licensing issues.
88: 
89: ### pdf2pdfocr (LeoFCardoso/pdf2pdfocr)
90: - **License**: Apache 2.0 — same as ours.
91: - **Strengths**: Simple, straightforward.  No re-encoding of source images when
92:   possible.  GUI available.  Cross-platform (Windows batch script included).
93: - **Weaknesses**: No MRC compression.  No PDF/A.  No image preprocessing.
94:   Low community (303 stars).  1.2.0 release never formalized.
95: - **Relevance**: Demonstrates the simplest possible OCR sandwich approach.
96:   Our tool adds MRC compression and full PDF/A for a material improvement.
97: 
98: ### Docling (docling-project/docling)
99: - **License**: MIT — permissive.
100: - **Strengths**: IBM Research quality.  Vision-based layout analysis (avoids
101:   pixel-level OCR for clean PDFs).  TableFormer for table extraction.  30x faster
102:   than OCR for born-digital PDFs.  Huge ecosystem (LangChain, LlamaIndex).
103:   61k+ stars.
104: - **Weaknesses**: Focused on *extraction* to Markdown/JSON, not on producing
105:   compressed searchable PDFs.  Heavy model downloads (GBs).  Python-only.
106:   Requires PyTorch for local inference.
107: - **Relevance**: Complementary — Docling is for RAG pipelines, not for
108:   producing archive-quality PDF output.  If we ever add a structure-preserving
109:   extraction mode, Docling's approach (vision models for layout) would be the
110:   direction.
111: 
112: ### PaddleOCR (PaddlePaddle/PaddleOCR)
113: - **License**: Apache 2.0.
114: - **Strengths**: SOTA accuracy (reported better than Tesseract for many
115:   scripts).  GPU-accelerated.  84k+ stars.  LLM-ready output (JSON/Markdown).
116:   Mature document parsing pipeline.
117: - **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch/PaddlePaddle runtime.  GPU strongly
118:   recommended for practical throughput.  Model downloads are large.  No
119:   searchable PDF output (focused on text extraction).
120: - **Relevance**: PaddleOCR is the best OCR *engine* available today.  If we
121:   ever add a pluggable OCR backend, PaddleOCR CLI (via subprocess, like
122:   Tesseract) would be a natural second option for higher accuracy.
123: 
124: ### olmOCR (allenai/olmOCR)
125: - **License**: Apache 2.0 (model weights, code).
126: - **Strengths**: VLM-based OCR that preserves reading order, handles tables,
127:   equations, handwriting.  Designed for high-throughput (estimated $178/1M pages).
128: - **Weaknesses**: Requires GPU (FP8 inference).  7B parameter model.  Very new
129:   (2025/2026).  No searchable PDF output — text-only.
130: - **Relevance**: VLM-based OCR is the emerging paradigm.  Once these models
131:   become efficient enough to run on CPU, a subprocess-based integration would
132:   be straightforward.
133: 
134: ### EasyOCR (JaidedAI/EasyOCR)
135: - **License**: Apache 2.0.
136: - **Strengths**: Ready-to-use with 80+ languages.  CRAFT-based text detection +
137:   CRNN recognition.  GPU and CPU support.  Active community (~30k stars).
138:   Simple Python API.  Custom model training support.
139: - **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch dependency.  Model downloads (hundreds of MB).
140:   No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Accuracy lags behind modern
141:   VLM approaches for complex layouts.
142: - **Relevance**: The most popular general-purpose OCR library.  If we add a
143:   Python subprocess-based pluggable OCR backend, EasyOCR would be a candidate
144:   for non-Tesseract engines (alongside PaddleOCR).
145: 
146: ### surya (VikParuchuri/surya, now datalab-to/surya)
147: - **License**: Apache 2.0 (code), modified OpenRAIL-M (weights — free for
148:   research/personal/startups under $5M funding/revenue).  Commercial use beyond
149:   limits requires license from Datalab.
150: - **Strengths**: 650M param model scoring 83.3% on olmOCR-bench (top under 3B
151:   params).  5 pages/s on RTX 5090.  90+ languages.  Includes layout analysis,
152:   reading order, table recognition.  CPU, GPU, MPS support.
153: - **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU recommended.  Model weights have commercial
154:   restrictions.  Weighted licensing model complicates commercial use.  No
155:   searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.
156: - **Relevance**: Surya represents the best lightweight VLM OCR available.
157:   Its layout analysis and reading order are superior to Tesseract for complex
158:   documents.  If the weight licensing were fully Apache 2.0, it would be the
159:   ideal Tesseract replacement for a pluggable backend.
160: 
161: ### docTR (mindee/doctr)
162: - **License**: Apache 2.0.
163: - **Strengths**: Two-stage (detection + recognition) OCR with PyTorch and
164:   TensorFlow backends.  Swappable architectures (DBNet, CRNN, ViTSTR, PARSeq,
165:   MASTER).  Comparable accuracy to Google Vision / AWS Textract on public
166:   benchmarks.  CPU and GPU support.  FastAPI integration.
167: - **Weaknesses**: Python + PyTorch/TensorFlow dependency.  No searchable PDF
168:   output.  No MRC compression.  Smaller community (~6k stars).  No built-in
169:   layout analysis beyond text detection.
170: - **Relevance**: A well-engineered, modular OCR library.  Its architecture
171:   flexibility makes it useful for research and custom OCR pipelines, but it
172:   lacks the document-to-PDF output pipeline we provide.
173: 
174: ### RapidOCR (RapidAI/RapidOCR)
175: - **License**: Apache 2.0.
176: - **Strengths**: PaddleOCR models converted to ONNX for cross-platform deployment.
177:   Multi-language support (Python, C++, Java, C#, Go).  Multiple inference backends
178:   (ONNXRuntime, OpenVINO, PaddlePaddle, PyTorch).  Very fast (3.3M monthly pip
179:   downloads).  Active development (v3.9.0).  Good for Chinese + English.
180: - **Weaknesses**: Focused on text detection + recognition only.  No searchable
181:   PDF output.  No MRC compression.  No layout analysis beyond basic detection.
182:   Primarily optimized for Chinese/English (smaller language coverage).
183: - **Relevance**: RapidOCR is the fastest pure-OCR library available.  Its Java
184:   binding is interesting for JVM integration, but it lacks the full PDF pipeline
185:   (compression, PDF/A, text layer) that TrulyFreeOCR provides.
186: 
187: ### DeepSeek-OCR (deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-OCR)
188: - **License**: MIT (code and weights).
189: - **Strengths**: "Contexts Optical Compression" — encodes text-rich images into
190:   compact vision tokens (64-400 tokens for 600-5000+ text tokens).  ~97%
191:   accuracy.  200k+ pages/day on a single A100.  Nearly 100 languages.  3B param
192:   model.  Very popular (~23k stars in 8 months).
193: - **Weaknesses**: Python + large GPU (A100-40G) for practical throughput.
194:   No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Research-oriented (no
195:   production packaging).  Very new (October 2025).
196: - **Relevance**: DeepSeek-OCR pioneered the VLM-as-OCR paradigm.  Its
197:   compression technique is conceptually aligned with our MRC approach (compress
198:   while preserving information).  The model itself could serve as a Tesseract
199:   replacement for highest-accuracy needs, given sufficient hardware.
200: 
201: ### GOT-OCR2.0 (Ucas-HaoranWei/GOT-OCR2.0)
202: - **License**: Apache 2.0 (code, HuggingFace weights), research-only (original
203:   weights from Vary license).
204: - **Strengths**: First unified "OCR-2.0" end-to-end model.  580M parameters
205:   (smaller than DeepSeek-OCR).  Handles plain text, formulas, tables, charts,
206:   sheet music, geometric shapes.  Region-level recognition via coordinates or
207:   colors.  Multi-crop for high-resolution images.  Dynamic resolution.
208: - **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU (24GB+).  Research-only license on original
209:   weights (though HF version uses Apache 2.0).  No searchable PDF output.  No
210:   MRC compression.  Last code update December 2024.
211: - **Relevance**: GOT-OCR2.0 demonstrated that a single end-to-end model could
212:   replace the traditional detection+recognition pipeline.  Its approach
213:   influenced DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, and the entire VLM OCR trend.
214: 
215: ### GLM-OCR (zai-org/GLM-OCR)
216: - **License**: MIT (model), Apache 2.0 (code).
217: - **Strengths**: State-of-the-art on OmniDocBench V1.5 (94.62).  Only 0.9B
218:   parameters — runs on 4GB VRAM.  1.86 pages/second.  Multi-Token Prediction
219:   (MTP) loss and RL training.  CogViT visual encoder.  PP-DocLayout-V3 layout
220:   analysis.  Excellent for complex tables, code documents, seals.  vLLM,
221:   SGLang, Ollama support.
222: - **Weaknesses**: Python + GPU required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC
223:   compression.  Very new (February 2026).  Pipeline depends on PP-DocLayout-V3
224:   (Apache 2.0).
225: - **Relevance**: GLM-OCR achieves the best accuracy/size ratio of any VLM OCR
226:   model.  Its 0.9B parameter size and 4GB VRAM requirement make it the most
227:   accessible high-accuracy OCR model.  Ideal candidate for a future pluggable
228:   VLM backend once GPU requirements decrease further.
229: 
230: ### MonkeyOCR (Yuliang-Liu/MonkeyOCR)
231: - **License**: Apache 2.0 (code), Alibaba non-commercial license (weights).
232: - **Strengths**: Structure-Recognition-Relation (SRR) triplet paradigm.
233:   Surpasses GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5-Pro on OmniDocBench.  ~1 page/s on RTX 4090.
234:   MonkeyOCR-pro-1.2B leaner version available.  MonkeyDoc dataset (3M+ pages).
235:   Chinese and English support.  Cross-page table reconstruction (v1.5).
236: - **Weaknesses**: Alibaba non-commercial weight license — **not suitable for
237:   commercial use**.  Python + GPU required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC
238:   compression.  Weight licensing restricts practical deployment.
239: - **Relevance**: Among the most accurate document parsers available, but the
240:   non-commercial weight license makes it unusable for our commercial-safe
241:   requirement.  Demonstrates the SRR paradigm for document understanding.
242: 
243: ### Unlimited-OCR (baidu/Unlimited-OCR)
244: - **License**: MIT.
245: - **Strengths**: One-shot long-horizon parsing (entire multi-page document in
246:   single pass).  Sliding window memory prevents OOM on long documents.  3B params,
247:   32,768 token context.  Built on DeepSeek-OCR innovations.  MIT license is fully
248:   business-friendly.  11k+ stars in one week (June 2026).
249: - **Weaknesses**: Just released (June 22, 2026) — very early.  Python + GPU
250:   required.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Ecosystem and
251:   tooling still immature.
252: - **Relevance**: The most promising recent VLM OCR entry.  Its one-shot long
253:   document capability and MIT license make it ideal for a future pluggable
254:   backend once VLM OCR matures and GPU requirements decrease.  Baidu's
255:   reputation ensures ongoing development.
256: 
257: ### Umi-OCR (hiroi-sora/Umi-OCR)
258: - **License**: MIT.
259: - **Strengths**: Fully offline desktop OCR app (Windows/Linux).  Screenshot OCR,
260:   batch processing, PDF recognition with dual-layer searchable PDF output.
261:   100+ languages via PaddleOCR or RapidOCR engines.  QR/barcode scanning.
262:   Formula recognition (LaTeX).  No GPU required.  46k stars.  Portable (unzip and
263:   run).  HTTP API for external integration.
264: - **Weaknesses**: Desktop GUI app — not designed for server/automation use.
265:   Dual-layer PDF output is basic (no MRC compression, no PDF/A).  Windows-focused
266:   (Linux support experimental).  Python runtime bundled (large download).
267:   No CLI-first design.
268: - **Relevance**: Umi-OCR is the most comparable tool for *desktop* searchable
269:   PDF creation.  Its dual-layer PDF output proves demand for this feature.
270:   Our tool targets the server/enterprise space where Umi-OCR cannot operate.
271: 
272: ### PandaOCR (miaomiaosoft/PandaOCR / PandaOCR.Pro)
273: - **License**: Custom freeware — not open source (source code not provided on
274:   GitHub, only binary releases).  PandaOCR.Pro has paid "Pro" activation.
275: - **Strengths**: Multi-engine OCR aggregator (Baidu, Tencent, Sogou, Youdao, Ali,
276:   iFLYTEK, Mathpix, etc.).  Screenshot OCR, translation, TTS, formula/table
277:   recognition.  Game translation.  QR/barcode scanning.  Image search.
278:   Active development (Pro v5.59, June 2026).  7+ years of updates.
279: - **Weaknesses**: Not open source (binary-only releases).  Relies on cloud API
280:   keys (no offline OCR).  Windows-only.  Free version has limitations; Pro
281:   requires payment.  No searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  Privacy
282:   concerns (uploads to third-party APIs).
283: - **Relevance**: Demonstrates user demand for multi-engine OCR aggregation.
284:   Our approach (single bundled Tesseract + optional VLM backends) avoids the
285:   complexity of managing multiple cloud API keys and the associated privacy
286:   risks.
287: 
288: ### SwiftOCR (NMAC427/SwiftOCR)
289: - **License**: Apache 2.0.
290: - **Strengths**: Native Swift OCR library for iOS/macOS.  Neural network-based.
291:   Simple API (6 lines of code).  Good for short alphanumeric codes (serial
292:   numbers, gift cards).  GPUImage acceleration.
293: - **Weaknesses**: **Deprecated** (last commit May 2020).  Only recognizes short
294:   alphanumeric text — not suitable for document OCR.  iOS/macOS only.  No
295:   searchable PDF output.  No MRC compression.  No longer maintained.
296: - **Relevance**: Minimal — deprecated and limited in scope.  Included for
297:   completeness but not a relevant comparison to our document PDF pipeline.
298: 
299: ### llama_index (run-llama/llama_index + LlamaParse)
300: - **License**: MIT (core framework).  LlamaParse is a cloud service with paid
301:   tiers (free tier: 10k credits/month ≈ 1k pages).
302: - **Strengths**: Leading RAG framework (50k+ stars).  LlamaParse provides
303:   agentic OCR with layout-aware parsing, multi-tier strategy (Fast, Cost
304:   Effective, Agentic).  90+ file types.  Multi-page table handling, embedded
305:   images, handwritten notes.  Python + TypeScript SDKs.  Java SDK available.
306:   Deep LLM ecosystem integration.
307: - **Weaknesses**: LlamaParse is a cloud service (not self-contained).  Paid per
308:   page ($1-15/1k pages).  No MRC compression.  No PDF/A output.  Focused on
309:   extraction for RAG, not archive-quality PDF production.  Framework is
310:   Python-heavy for self-hosted use.
311: - **Relevance**: LlamaIndex/LlamaParse represents the cloud/API approach to
312:   document OCR.  Our tool competes in the self-contained, offline, commercial-safe
313:   space.  If users want cloud OCR with RAG pipelines, LlamaParse is the best
314:   option; if they need offline batch PDF production with MRC compression, we
315:   provide that.
316: 
317: ---
318: 
319: ## Why TrulyFreeOCR Exists
320: 
321: | Problem | Existing Tools | Our Solution |
322: |---|---|---|
323: | Commercial-safe license | OCRmyPDF (MPL-2.0) requires publishing modifications; archive-pdf-tools is AGPL; MonkeyOCR has non-commercial weights; surya has restricted weights | Apache 2.0 — no disclosure obligations; all deps have business-friendly licenses |
324: | Self-contained deployment | All alternatives require Python + system libs + model downloads or cloud APIs | Single fat JAR + bundled native binaries + bootstrap script |
325: | Java/enterprise integration | Python tools need subprocess calls from Java code | Native Java API, embeddable in Maven/Gradle projects |
326: | MRC compression | Only OCRmyPDF and archive-pdf-tools offer it, with complex deps; Umi-OCR offers basic dual-layer | PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg with clean fallback |
327: | No Ghostscript dependency | OCRmyPDF requires Ghostscript (AGPL) | Pure PDFBox — no AGPL anywhere in the stack |
328: | VLM future-proofing | Current VLM models (DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, Unlimited-OCR) require GPU and produce text only | Architecture supports pluggable backends; Tesseract is performant on CPU today |
329: | Offline/privacy | PandaOCR, LlamaParse send data to cloud APIs; EasyOCR/docTR need Python runtime | Fully offline; everything runs locally; no data leaves the machine |
330: 
331: ## Potential Future Directions
332: 
333: 1. **Pluggable OCR backends** — Add PaddleOCR CLI or RapidOCR as alternatives to
334:    Tesseract (subprocess-based, same pattern).  Once VLM models (GLM-OCR,
335:    Unlimited-OCR) become CPU-efficient, add them as higher-accuracy backends.
336: 2. **Deskew/clean preprocessing** — Add Leptonica-based deskew and text
337:    cleaning via subprocess (similar to OCRmyPDF's `--deskew`).
338: 3. **JPEG2000 background** — Add optional JPEG2000 background compression
339:    via OpenJPEG subprocess for better compression ratios than PNG.
340: 4. **Docling-style extraction** — A `--extract-json` mode that outputs
341:    structured Markdown/JSON for RAG pipelines.
342: 5. **Umi-OCR-style desktop GUI** — If desktop demand grows, a lightweight
343:    GUI wrapper around the CLI (like Umi-OCR but Java-based) could serve
344:    non-technical users.

(End of file - total 344 lines)
</content>
```

<content>
1: # TrulyFreeOCR
2: 
3: Commercially-safe OCR pipeline to produce fully searchable PDFs.
4: 
5: **Input**: Any PDF (searchable, non-searchable, or partially searchable)<br>
6: **Output**: Fully searchable, MRC-compressed PDF
7: 
8: ---
9: 
10: ## Who It's For
11: 
12: - **Commercial / Enterprise teams** — All dependencies are Apache 2.0 / BSD / MIT.
13:   No GPL, no AGPL, no proprietary components — no obligation to open source your code.
14: 
15: - **DevOps / Deployment engineers** — Every dependency (JDK, Tesseract, Leptonica,
16:   jbig2enc, language data) downloads into project subdirectories via `bootstrap.sh`.
17:   The entire stack is self-contained: copy the folder and it runs. No apt, brew,
18:   or system-wide installs needed on the target machine.
19: 
20: - **Document processing pipelines** — Headless CLI with JSONC settings file.
21:   All parameters (DPI, language, PSM, MRC on/off) are scriptable. The fat JAR
22:   has zero Java-classpath fuss — one file to copy, one command to run.
23: 
24: - **Teams scanning at scale** — MRC compression typically reduces PDF size 5–10×
25:   versus straight JPEG while keeping text razor-sharp via the JBIG2 foreground
26:   mask. Supports 20 languages including English, French, German, Spanish,
27:   Italian, Portuguese, Russian, Arabic, Hindi, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, and more.
28: 
29: - **Open-source contributors / integrators** — Pure-Java segmentation (no
30:   Leptonica native dependency). Clean pipeline API with PageExtractor →
31:   ImageSegmenter → OCREngine → PDFAssembler. Each component is independently
32:   testable and replaceable.
33: 
34: ---
35: 
36: ## Getting Started
37: 
38: ```bash
39: git clone https://github.com/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR.git
40: cd TrulyFreeOCR
41: 
42: # Install dependencies (JDK, Tesseract, tessdata, jbig2enc, native libs or executables)
43: ./bootstrap.sh
44: 
45: # Build the fat JAR (contains all Java dependencies)
46: ./gradlew build
47: 
48: # Run all tests (optional)
49: ./gradlew test
50: ```
51: 
52: ### Basic Usage
53: 
54: ```bash
55: # Minimal: process a PDF with default settings
56: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf
57: 
58: # Specify output path
59: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf -o output.pdf
60: 
61: # Use a different language (fra, spa, deu, chi_sim, chi_tra, jpn)
62: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --language fra
63: 
64: # Adjust Tesseract page segmentation mode
65: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --psm 6
66: 
67: # Lower DPI for faster processing (less accurate)
68: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --dpi 200
69: 
70: # Disable MRC compression (faster assembly, larger files)
71: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --no-mrc
72: 
73: # Enable PDF/A-2b output
74: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --pdfa
75: 
76: # Control segmentation threads (default: # of CPU cores). OCR uses min(threads, 4).
77: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --threads 2
78: 
79: # Custom native/tessdata directories
80: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf \
81:     --native-dir ./custom-native --tessdata-dir ./custom-tessdata
82: 
83: # Load settings from a custom file (overrides settings.jsonc)
84: java -jar build/libs/trulyfreeocr.jar input.pdf --settings /path/to/custom.jsonc
85: 
86: # Using the project-local JDK wrapper
87: TFOCR_JAVA_HOME=jdk/linux ./gradlew run --args="input.pdf -o output.pdf"
88: ```
89: 
90: CLI flags override `settings.jsonc` values, which override hardcoded defaults. Run with `--help` for all options.
91: 
92: ---
93: 
94: ## Pipeline
95: 
96: ```
97: Input PDF
98:     |
99:     v
100: PageExtractor  — PDFBox PDFRenderer (configurable DPI) → per-page BufferedImage
101:     |
102:     +---------+----------+
103:     |         |          |
104:     v         v          v
105: ImageSegmenter  |   OCREngine          (parallel across pages, configurable threads)
106:     |           |      |
107:     |           | Tesseract CLI
108:     |           | word-level bboxes with TSV output
109:     +----+------+
110:          |
111:          v
112: PDFAssembler — Background layer + CCITT G4 foreground mask stencil
113:     |            + invisible OCR text (rendering mode 3)
114:     v
115: Output PDF (searchable, compressed; optionally PDF/A-2b)
116: ```
117: 
118: ---
119: 
120: ## Mixed Raster Content (MRC) Compression
121: 
122: TrulyFreeOCR uses an MRC-like approach (ISO 32000-2 / TIFF-FX) to decompose each page into three layers, each compressed with a codec suited to its content:
123: 
124: | Layer | Content | Codec | Rationale |
125: |---|---|---|---|
126: | **Background** | Cleaned page image (de-speckled, de-noised) | **JPEG** (quality 0.85) | Photographic content compresses well with lossy JPEG; by removing text noise first, quality remains high at small file sizes |
127: | **Foreground mask** | Binary text pixels from Otsu binarization | **CCITT G4** (fax) or **JBIG2** (when jbig2enc is available) | Binary text is ideal for bi-level compression; JBIG2 gives 2–5× smaller than G4 by exploiting symbol repetition |
128: | **Text layer** | OCR words at original positions | **Invisible PDF text** (rendering mode 3) | Vector text is negligible in size, makes the PDF fully searchable and selectable, and requires no embedding (Standard 14 fonts) |
129: 
130: When rendered, layers are stacked: background → foreground stencil (punches through to reveal sharp text pixels) → invisible text (for selection/search). The result is a PDF that looks like the original scan but is fully searchable and dramatically smaller.
131: 
132: ### With vs Without MRC
133: 
134: | Content type | Without MRC (JPEG only) | With MRC (background JPEG + foreground mask + text) | Why |
135: |---|---|---|---|
136: | Text-heavy page (letter, report) | Large — JPEG must preserve every sharp edge, wasting bits on text contrast | Small — text is extracted into a compact binary mask; background becomes smooth (easy to compress) | Mask compresses text at 0.01–0.05 bits/pixel vs 0.5–1.0 bits/pixel in JPEG |
137: | Mixed page (text + photo) | Baseline | Similar savings — photo stays in JPEG, text pulled into mask | Photo unaffected; text layer shrinks |
138: | Full-page photo | Baseline | Slightly larger (mask adds overhead with no text to compress) | MRC detects no foreground text; overhead is negligible |
139: 
140: For a typical text document, MRC output is **5–10× smaller** than JPEG-only while keeping text edges perfectly sharp.
141: 
142: Disable MRC with `--no-mrc` to skip segmentation and output raw JPEG backgrounds with text layer only (faster assembly, larger files).
143: 
144: ---
145: 
146: ## Configuration
147: 
148: All pipeline parameters are configurable via `settings.jsonc` in the project root directory (JSON with `//` and `/* */` comments). CLI flags override file settings, which override hardcoded defaults.
149: 
150: | Setting | Default | Description |
151: |---|---|---|
152: | `native.dir` | `./native` | Native binaries directory (jbig2enc, etc.) |
153: | `tessdata.dir` | `./tessdata` | Tesseract language data directory |
154: | `tesseract.path` | `./native/linux/tesseract` | Tesseract executable (project-local wrapper) |
155: | `tesseract.language` | `eng` | Tesseract language model |
156: | `tesseract.psm` | `1` | Page segmentation mode |
157: | `pipeline.mrc.enabled` | `true` | Enable MRC compression (background + foreground mask + text) |
158: | `pipeline.ocr.maxThreads` | `1` | Max concurrent Tesseract subprocesses (1 = sequential OCR; increase on fast NVMe + >16 GB RAM) |
159: | `rendering.dpi` | `300` | PDF rendering resolution |
160: | `segmenter.tileSize` | `64` | Background normalization tile size (px) |
161: | `segmenter.percentile` | `0.95` | Background level percentile per tile |
162: | `segmenter.inpaintRadius` | `3` | Inpainting search radius (px) |
163: | `pdf.font` | `HELVETICA` | Standard 14 font for OCR text |
164: | `pdf.minFontSize` | `1` | Minimum OCR text font size (pt) |
165: | `pdf.pdfa.enabled` | `false` | Enable PDF/A-2b output (adds XMP metadata, sRGB OutputIntent) |
166: | `pdf.pdfa.fontPath` | `""` | Path to a TrueType font for embedding in PDF/A; empty = Standard 14 (non-embedded) |
167: | `output.file` | `output.pdf` | Default output file path |
168: 
169: CLI flags that override corresponding settings: `--dpi`, `--language`, `--psm`, `--no-mrc`, `--pdfa`, `--threads`, `--native-dir`, `--tessdata-dir`, `-o`/`--output`, `--settings` (path to a custom settings file).
170: 
171: ---
172: 
173: ## OCR Quality
174: 
175: | Metric | Regular prose pages | Notes |
176: |---|---|---|
177: | **Word Error Rate** | 0.9% – 3.2% | Well within the 5% target for standard documents |
178: | **Character Error Rate** | 0.2% – 1.2% | Sub-character accuracy on clean scans |
179: | **Word Recall** | 98.7% | Almost all ground-truth words detected |
180: | **Word Precision** | 98.7% | Very few false positives |
181: | **Mean Confidence** | 89.5 – 94.7 | Tesseract per-word confidence score |
182: 
183: Results measured on a 10-page public-domain prose corpus at 300 DPI, English language.
184: Non-standard layouts (title pages, multi-language extracts) show higher WER (~60–70%)
185: but are not representative of typical document content. See [`Evaluation.md`](Evaluation.md)
186: for the full methodology, per-page breakdown, and parameter sensitivity plans.
187: 
188: ---
189: 
190: ## Testing
191: 
192: Tests use generated sample PDFs in `tests/test-files/`:
193: - `blank.pdf` — single blank US Letter page
194: - `simple-text.pdf` — one page with lorem ipsum text
195: - `multi-page.pdf` — three-page document with distinct content per page
196: - `two-column.pdf` — two-column layout
197: - `with-annotations.pdf` — bookmarks, links, and comments
198: - `noisy-scan.pdf` — simulated scanner noise and skew
199: 
200: Regenerate test PDFs with:
201: ```bash
202: ./gradlew generateTestPdfs
203: ```
204: 
205: See [`Evaluation.md`](Evaluation.md) for the OCR accuracy evaluation plan —
206: WER/CER targets, parameter sensitivity sweeps, and performance baselines.
207: 
208: ---
209: 
210: ## Components
211: 
212: | Component | What it does |
213: |---|---|
214: | **PageExtractor** | Renders each PDF page to a `BufferedImage` at configurable DPI using PDFBox's `PDFRenderer.renderImageWithDPI()` |
215: | **ImageSegmenter** | Pure-Java page segmentation: grayscale conversion → background normalization → Otsu binarization → inpainting. No Leptonica dependency. |
216: | **OCREngine** | Delegates to Tesseract CLI (not JNA/Tess4J) via subprocess with TSV output. Avoids native-library version mismatches. |
217: | **JBIG2Compressor** | Compresses binary foreground masks via jbig2enc (`-p -s`). Falls back to CCITT Group 4 fax encoding via PDFBox when jbig2enc is unavailable. |
218: | **PDFAssembler** | Re-assembles the output PDF: background (JPEG or lossless PNG) + CCITT G4 foreground stencil + invisible OCR text (rendering mode 3, Standard 14 fonts). |
219: | **MetadataPreserver** | Copies document info, outlines (bookmarks), per-page annotations, and XMP metadata from source to output. |
220: 
221: ---
222: 
223: ## Dependencies
224: 
225: ### Build & Runtime (from Gradle)
226: 
227: | Dependency | Version | License | Project |
228: |---|---|---|---|
229: | PDFBox | 3.0.6 | Apache 2.0 | https://pdfbox.apache.org |
230: | picocli | 4.7.6 | Apache 2.0 | https://picocli.info |
231: 
232: ### Build-time Only
233: 
234: | Dependency | Version | License | Project |
235: |---|---|---|---|
236: | Shadow plugin | 8.1.1 | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/johnrengelman/shadow |
237: | Gradle wrapper | 8.0.1 | Apache 2.0 | https://gradle.org |
238: 
239: ### Test (from Gradle)
240: 
241: These dependencies are test-only and never ship in the production fat JAR.
242: 
243: | Dependency | Version | License | Project |
244: |---|---|---|---|
245: | JUnit Jupiter | 5.11.4 | EPL 2.0 | https://junit.org/junit5/ |
246: | JUnit Platform Launcher | 1.11.4 | EPL 2.0 | https://junit.org/junit5/ |
247: 
248: EPL 2.0 (Eclipse Public License) is a weak copyleft license that allows
249: commercial use — the copyleft only covers the EPL library itself, not the
250: code that uses it. Since these are test-only dependencies, they are not
251: distributed with the application.
252: 
253: ### Native / Bundled
254: 
255: All native dependencies are installed in project subdirectories and gitignored.
256: The `bootstrap.sh` script handles setting them up from scratch.
257: 
258: | Dependency | Project Location | License | Project |
259: |---|---|---|---|
260: | OpenJDK 21 LTS | `jdk/` | GPL 2.0 + Classpath Exception | https://adoptium.net |
261: | Tesseract OCR | `native/$OS/tesseract` → `tesseract.bin` + `native/$OS/lib/libtesseract.so.5` | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tesseract |
262: | Leptonica | `native/$OS/lib/liblept.so.5` | BSD 2-Clause | https://github.com/DanBloomberg/leptonica |
263: | jbig2enc | `native/$OS/jbig2enc` → `jbig2enc.bin` + `native/$OS/lib/libjbig2enc.so.0` | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/agl/jbig2enc |
264: | Tesseract language data | `tessdata/*.traineddata` (eng, fra, spa, deu, chi_sim, chi_tra, jpn) | Apache 2.0 | https://github.com/tesseract-ocr/tessdata |
265: 
266: Binary wrappers in `native/$OS/` set `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` to `native/$OS/lib/` so
267: the project-local shared libraries are used instead of system-wide ones.
268: 
269: ---
270: 
271: <details>
272: <summary>Current Status</summary>
273: 
274: | Phase | Component | Status |
275: |-------|-----------|--------|
276: | 1 | Gradle scaffold + CLI | Done |
277: | 2 | PageExtractor | Done |
278: | 3 | OCREngine | Done |
279: | 4 | ImageSegmenter | Done |
280: | 5 | PDFAssembler (basic) | Done |
281: | 6 | JBIG2Compressor + SubprocessRunner | Done |
282: | 7 | PDFAssembler JBIG2 integration | Done |
283: | 8 | MetadataPreserver | Done |
284: | 9 | bootstrap scripts | Done |
285: | 10 | Integration tests | Done |
286: | 11 | Concurrent page processing | Done |
287: | 12 | PDF/A-2b output | Done |
288: | 13 | 20 language bundles | Done |
289: 
290: </details>
291: 
292: ---
293: 
294: ## License
295: 
296: TrulyFreeOCR itself is made available under the Apache 2.0 License. All dependencies listed above use permissive open-source licenses compatible with commercial use. A full `NOTICE.md` with copyright attributions is included in the repository.

(End of file - total 296 lines)
</content>

2. Traditional PDF OCR tools either have restrictive licenses or complex system dependencies.

Traditional tools like OCRmyPDF cover the PDF pipeline but need complex system deps and have license caveats. Desktop tools like Umi-OCR are GUI-only. Cloud services like LlamaParse have per-page costs and no offline mode.   "oldString": "## Why TrulyFreeOCR\n\nA survey of 19 open-source OCR projects (see [`docs/similar-projects-comparison.md`](docs/similar-projects-comparison.md)) found none that combine all five of these properties:\n\n| Capability | TrulyFreeOCR | VLM models (DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, etc.) | OCRmyPDF | Umi-OCR | LlamaParse | EasyOCR / docTR |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Business-friendly license** | Apache 2.0 — no disclosure | Some have restricted/non-commercial weights | MPL-2.0 (must publish OCRmyPDF mods) | MIT | MIT (cloud API paid) | Apache 2.0 |\n| **Self-contained deployment** | Single fat JAR + `bootstrap.sh` | Python + GPU + GBs of models | Python + Ghostscript + system deps | Windows .exe + bundled runtime | Cloud API (no offline) | Python + PyTorch |\n| **MRC compression** | PNG bg + JBIG2/G4 fg + PDF/A-2b | No (text-only output) | Yes (via `--optimize`) | Basic dual-layer only | No | No |\n| **Searchable PDF output** | CCITT G4/JBIG2 mask + invisible text layer | No | Yes | Yes (basic) | Markdown/JSON only | No |\n| **No cloud / no GPU** | CPU-only, fully offline | GPU required (4-80GB VRAM) | CPU (Ghostscript + Tesseract) | CPU | Cloud API | PyTorch required |\n\n**Bottom line**: VLM models produce the best OCR accuracy but require GPUs and only emit JSON/Markdown. Traditional tools like OCRmyPDF cover the PDF pipeline but need complex system deps and have license caveats. Desktop tools like Umi-OCR are GUI-only. Cloud services like LlamaParse have per-page costs and no offline mode. TrulyFreeOCR fills the gap: CPU-only, offline, Apache 2.0, MRC-compressed searchable PDFs — ready for enterprise/server deployment.",
  "newString": "## Why TrulyFreeOCR\n\nA survey of 19 open-source OCR projects (see [`docs/similar-projects-comparison.md`](docs/similar-projects-comparison.md)) found none that combine all five requirements for production document processing:\n\n- **Business-friendly license** — Apache 2.0 (no disclosure obligations)\n- **Self-contained** — single fat JAR + `bootstrap.sh`; no Python, no system deps\n- **MRC compression** — JBIG2/CCITT foreground mask + JPEG/PNG background\n- **Searchable PDF output** — invisible text layer + optional PDF/A-2b\n- **No cloud / no GPU** — CPU-only, fully offline, zero data leaves the machine\n\nWhere other tools fall short:\n\n- **VLM models** (DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, Unlimited-OCR, etc.) produce the best OCR accuracy but require GPUs and only emit JSON/Markdown — no PDF output at all.\n- **OCRmyPDF** covers the full PDF pipeline but needs Ghostscript + Python + system deps; its MPL-2.0 license requires publishing modifications.\n- **Umi-OCR** makes a nice desktop GUI but has no MRC compression, no PDF/A, and is Windows-focused.\n- **LlamaParse** (LlamaIndex) is a cloud API with per-page costs, no offline mode, and no MRC/PDF/A output.\n- **EasyOCR / docTR / PaddleOCR / RapidOCR** are OCR libraries, not PDF tools — they extract text but produce no searchable PDF.\n- **surya / MonkeyOCR** have non-commercial model weight restrictions, making them unsuitable for commercial deployment.\n\nTrulyFreeOCR fills the gap: deploy anywhere, no cloud, no GPU, no license worries — just a JAR and a PDF."
}

<content>
28: 
29: - **Open-source contributors / integrators** — Pure-Java segmentation (no
30:   Leptonica native dependency). Clean pipeline API with PageExtractor →
31:   ImageSegmenter → OCREngine → PDFAssembler. Each component is independently
32:   testable and replaceable.
33: 
34: ---
35: 
36: ## Why TrulyFreeOCR
37: 
38: A survey of 19 open-source OCR projects (see [`docs/similar-projects-comparison.md`](docs/similar-projects-comparison.md)) found none that combine all five requirements for production document processing:
39: 
40: - **Business-friendly license** — Apache 2.0 (no disclosure obligations)
41: - **Self-contained** — single fat JAR + `bootstrap.sh`; no Python, no system deps
42: - **MRC compression** — JBIG2/CCITT foreground mask + JPEG/PNG background
43: - **Searchable PDF output** — invisible text layer + optional PDF/A-2b
44: - **No cloud / no GPU** — CPU-only, fully offline, zero data leaves the machine
45: 
46: Where other tools fall short:
47: 
48: - **VLM models** (DeepSeek-OCR, GLM-OCR, Unlimited-OCR, etc.) produce the best OCR accuracy but require GPUs and only emit JSON/Markdown — no PDF output at all.
49: - **OCRmyPDF** covers the full PDF pipeline but needs Ghostscript + Python + system deps; its MPL-2.0 license requires publishing modifications.
50: - **Umi-OCR** makes a nice desktop GUI but has no MRC compression, no PDF/A, and is Windows-focused.
51: - **LlamaParse** (LlamaIndex) is a cloud API with per-page costs, no offline mode, and no MRC/PDF/A output.
52: - **EasyOCR / docTR / PaddleOCR / RapidOCR** are OCR libraries, not PDF tools — they extract text but produce no searchable PDF.
53: - **surya / MonkeyOCR** have non-commercial model weight restrictions, making them unsuitable for commercial deployment.
54: 
55: TrulyFreeOCR fills the gap: deploy anywhere, no cloud, no GPU, no license worries — just a JAR and a PDF.
56: 
57: ---
58: 
59: ## Getting Started
60: 
61: ```bash
62: git clone https://github.com/msmarkgu/TrulyFreeOCR.git
63: cd TrulyFreeOCR
64: 
65: # Install dependencies (JDK, Tesseract, tessdata, jbig2enc, native libs or executables)
66: ./bootstrap.sh
67: 

(Showing lines 28-67 of 319. Use offset=68 to continue.)
</content>
```
